topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Centre Lathe: Facing, Plain Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Form Turning, Groove Cutting, Knurling, Chamfering, Multi-Start Thread, Square Thread, Internal Thread, and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Centre Lathe: The Universal Father Machine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** A **Centre Lathe** is a manually or power-driven machine tool designed to remove excess material from a workpiece, held between two centres (or a chuck and tailstock centre), using a single-point cutting tool. It is the most versatile machine tool capable of performing turning, facing, threading, knurling, boring, and forming operations on cylindrical and conical workpieces.

The Centre Lathe is referred to in KTU workshops as the **"King of Machine Tools"** because it can perform the maximum variety of operations compared to any other single machine. The classification of operations performed on it forms the **core practical syllabus of Module Experiment 2** of PCMRL408.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL408 – Module 2):** The student is expected to *physically execute* the following operations on a given mild steel workpiece: Facing, Plain Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Form Turning, Groove Cutting, Knurling, Chamfering, Multi-Start Thread, Square Thread, Internal Thread, and Inspection using standard measuring instruments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Potter's Wheel" Mental Model

Imagine a **potter's wheel** spinning a lump of clay. The potter holds a flat wooden tool against the spinning clay to *shape* it. A **Centre Lathe works on the exact same principle** — the workpiece spins, while a stationary cutting tool presses against it, peeling away a thin ribbon of metal (the **chip**).

- The **chuck** is like the potter's hands holding the clay.
- The **cutting tool** is the wooden shaping blade.
- The **chip** is the clay shavings flying off.
- The **carriage feed** is the steady, controlled movement of the potter's hand along the spinning axis.

The only difference: a lathe works on **steel, brass, or aluminium** with micrometre-level precision instead of clay.

### 1.3 Master Classification of Lathe Operations (KTU Module 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All 12 operations listed in the syllabus are classified under 4 functional families:**

| Functional Family | Operations Covered | Key Geometric Output |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Surface Generation** | Facing, Plain Turning | Flat end-face & Cylindrical surface |
| **B. Stepped / Profiled Geometry** | Step Turning, Taper Turning, Form Turning | Multiple diameters, Cones, Irregular profiles |
| **C. Feature Addition** | Groove Cutting, Knurling, Chamfering | Recesses, Knurled grip, Edge break |
| **D. Thread Generation & Inspection** | Multi-Start Thread, Square Thread, Internal Thread + Inspection | External/Internal helical/square threads |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Tool Geometry (Bolded)

- **Spindle Speed (N)** is measured in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**.
- **Feed (f)** is measured in **mm/revolution**.
- **Depth of Cut (d)** is measured in **mm** (radial).
- **Standard Tool Angles**: **Side Rake = 10°–15°**, **End Clearance = 6°–8°**, **Side Clearance = 6°–8°** (for HSS tools on mild steel).
- **Standard Cutting Speed (V)** for mild steel with HSS tool: **V = 20–30 m/min**.
- **Standard Pitch Series (Metric ISO)**: 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0 mm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operation-by-Operation Theoretical Breakdown

### **Operation 1: Facing**
- **Why:** To produce a flat, square end surface perpendicular to the lathe axis.
- **How:** Workpiece rotates; cutting tool is fed **radially inward** (perpendicular to the axis) from the outer diameter toward the centre. The tool is mounted on the cross-slide.
- **Why it's done first:** Facing establishes a true reference face for depth measurement during all subsequent operations.
- **Tool Used:** Right-hand facing tool with cutting edge on the left side.

### **Operation 2: Plain Turning**
- **Why:** To reduce the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece to a specified size over a defined length.
- **How:** Workpiece rotates; tool is fed **parallel to the axis** using the carriage handwheel. Material is removed as a continuous helical chip.
- **Why it matters:** It is the *most fundamental* lathe operation and forms the basis for step, taper, and form turning.

### **Operation 3: Step Turning**
- **Why:** To produce workpieces with **two or more different diameters** (e.g., a shaft with shoulders to seat bearings or gears).
- **How:** Sequence of multiple plain turning operations at different diameters, separated by facing cuts at the step locations.
- **KTU Exam Trick:** Students often confuse the *order* — **always turn the largest diameter first**, then progressively smaller steps.

### **Operation 4: Taper Turning**
- **Why:** To produce a conical surface (e.g., machine tool spindles, morse tapers).
- **Three Methods:**
  1. **Form Tool Method** — using a broad-nose tool shaped to the taper angle.
  2. **Compound Rest Method** — swivelling the compound rest to the **half taper angle (α/2)**.
  3. **Tailstock Set-Over Method** — offsetting the tailstock laterally by a calculated amount.

### **Operation 5: Form Turning**
- **Why:** To generate an irregular, non-circular profile (e.g., spherical handles, contoured knobs) in a single pass.
- **How:** A specially ground form tool (with a profile matching the desired shape) is fed radially or longitudinally. The tool profile is the *negative* of the workpiece profile.

### **Operation 6: Groove Cutting**
- **Why:** To create narrow recesses for **circlip retention, oil sealing, or thread run-out relief**.
- **How:** A parting/grooving tool (narrow blade) is fed radially inward to a pre-set depth, then traversed axially if a wide groove is needed.

### **Operation 7: Knurling**
- **Why:** To produce a **diamond or straight-line patterned rough surface** on the workpiece for improved grip (e.g., on thumbscrews, adjusting nuts).
- **How:** A **knurling tool** (with two hardened rollers carrying the pattern) is pressed against the rotating workpiece under high pressure. The pattern is *impressed*, not cut.
- **Pattern Types:** **Straight knurl**, **Diamond (cross) knurl**.

### **Operation 8: Chamfering**
- **Why:** To remove the sharp 90° edge of a machined component for **safety, ease of assembly, and stress relief**.
- **How:** The tool is fed at a 45° angle to produce a 45° × 45° chamfer (most common). Standard chamfer size = **0.5 × 45° to 2 × 45°**.

### **Operation 9: Multi-Start Thread**
- **Why:** To produce a thread with **multiple parallel helical grooves** (e.g., 2-start, 3-start threads) for fast axial travel per revolution — used in **lead screws, bottle caps, and fast-acting clamps**.
- **How:** After completing the first start, the tool is **indexed axially by 1/n of the pitch** (n = number of starts) and the second groove is cut.

### **Operation 10: Square Thread**
- **Why:** To produce a thread with a **square cross-section** offering maximum efficiency in power transmission (used in **jack screws, valve stems, and clamping devices**).
- **How:** A specially ground square-thread tool (with zero rake angle and 0° clearance on flanks) is used. The tool is fed radially to the full thread depth.
- **Cutting Sequence:** Cut → Calibrate with pitch gauge → Final pass with slight spring cut for clearance.

### **Operation 11: Internal Thread**
- **Why:** To cut threads on the **inside of a hole** (e.g., a nut or threaded bush).
- **How:** A boring bar carries a small threading tool. For small diameters, a **tap** is used instead. Cutting fluid is essential.
- **Critical Setup:** The tool must be **perfectly aligned with the bore axis**, otherwise thread taper or breakage occurs.

### **Operation 12: Inspection**
- **Why:** To verify the geometric and dimensional accuracy of the machined workpiece.
- **How:** Using **Vernier caliper** (length, OD, step dimensions), **micrometer** (precise diameter, ±0.01 mm), **thread pitch gauge**, **radius gauge**, **sine bar** (taper angle), and **surface roughness comparator**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Used In Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$ | rpm | All turning operations |
| 2 | Cutting Velocity | $V = \dfrac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$ | m/min | Verification |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_{r} = f \times N$ | mm/min | All operations |
| 4 | Metal Removal Rate | $MRR = f \times d \times V$ | mm³/min | Turning planning |
| 5 | Machining Time (Plain Turn) | $T_{m} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | min | Plain/Step turning |
| 6 | Machining Time (Facing) | $T_{f} = \dfrac{0.5(D_{o} - D_{i})}{f \times N}$ | min | Facing |
| 7 | Taper (per mm length) | $K = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm/mm | Taper turning |
| 8 | Half Taper Angle | $\tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | degrees | Compound rest setting |
| 9 | Tailstock Set-Over | $S = \dfrac{L_{w} \times (D - d)}{2L}$ | mm | Tailstock method |
| 10 | Multi-Start Axial Index | $I = \dfrac{p}{n}$ | mm | Multi-start threading |
| 11 | Thread Depth (V-thread) | $h = 0.6134 \times p$ | mm | Thread turning |
| 12 | Thread Depth (Square) | $h = 0.5 \times p$ | mm | Square threading |
| 13 | Lead vs Pitch | $L = n \times p$ | mm | Multi-start verification |
| 14 | Spindle Speed for Threading | $N_{t} = \dfrac{1000 \times V_{t}}{\pi \times D_{m}}$ | rpm | Threading |
| 15 | Pitch (TPI system) | $p = \dfrac{25.4}{TPI}$ | mm | Imperial threads |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $D$ = major diameter (mm), $d$ = minor diameter (mm), $L$ = length of cut (mm), $p$ = pitch (mm), $n$ = number of starts, $D_{m}$ = mean thread diameter (mm), $V_{t}$ = cutting speed for threading (typically 5–10 m/min for HSS), $L_{w}$ = workpiece total length.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plain + Step Turning** → manufacture of transmission shafts, axles, spindles.
- **Taper Turning** → Morse tapers for drill shanks, machine tool arbours.
- **Knurling** → grip enhancement on micrometer thimbles, adjusting knobs.
- **Multi-Start Threads** → CNC lead screws, fast-action clamping screws in jigs and fixtures.
- **Square Threads** → high-efficiency power screws in hydraulic presses and screw jacks.
- **Internal Threading** → manufacturing of nuts, threaded bushes, and tapped holes.
- **Inspection** → Statistical Process Control (SPC) and ISO 2768 tolerance verification.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Velocity (V)

**Given:** A mild steel workpiece of diameter $D = 40$ mm is to be turned at cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min using HSS tool. Find spindle speed $N$.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometric relationship:**
The cutting velocity $V$ is the linear tangential speed at the workpiece surface. For a rotating cylinder of diameter $D$ (in mm) rotating at $N$ rpm, the surface speed is:

$$V = \pi \times D \times N \times 10^{-3} \;\; (\text{m/min})$$

**Step 2 — Algebraically isolate N:**

$$N = \dfrac{V \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$N = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{3}}{\pi \times 40} = \dfrac{25000}{125.66}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final value:**

$$N \approx 198.9 \approx 199 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5 — Select nearest available spindle speed on the lathe:**
Typically lathes have stepped speeds: 100, 125, 160, 200, 250 rpm. → **Select N = 200 rpm.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Machining Time for Plain Turning

**Given:** A shaft length $L = 120$ mm is to be turned at feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev at $N = 200$ rpm. Find $T_{m}$.

**Step 1 — Definition:** Machining time = total length to be traversed / linear feed per minute.

$$T_{m} = \dfrac{L}{f_{r}}$$

**Step 2 — Express feed rate in mm/min:** $f_{r} = f \times N$.

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$T_{m} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$T_{m} = \dfrac{120}{0.2 \times 200} = \dfrac{120}{40}$$

**Step 5 — Result:**

$$T_{m} = 3 \text{ minutes}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Taper Turning by Compound Rest (Half Angle)

**Given:** A workpiece of length $L = 100$ mm is to be turned from $D = 50$ mm to $d = 40$ mm (taper at larger end). Find the **compound rest swivel angle** and the **taper ratio**.

**Step 1 — Compute Taper per unit length (K):**

$$K = \dfrac{D - d}{L} = \dfrac{50 - 40}{100} = \dfrac{10}{100} = 0.1 \text{ mm/mm}$$

This is also expressed as **1:10 taper**.

**Step 2 — Compute the full included taper angle α:**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{L} = 0.1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.1) = 5.71^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the half taper angle for compound rest setting:**

$$\alpha/2 = \dfrac{5.71^{\circ}}{2} = 2.855^{\circ} \approx 2^{\circ} \, 51'$$

**Step 4 — Verification using tangent half-angle identity:**
Since $\tan(\alpha) = 0.1$ is small, $\alpha/2 \approx 2.855°$, confirming the compound rest should be set to **~2°51′ from the lathe axis**.

**Step 5 — Alternative using Taper Per mm formula (TPI/TPF):**
Taper per foot (TPF) = $0.1 \times 304.8 = 30.48$ mm/m. Taper per inch (TPI in taper sense) = $0.1 \times 25.4 = 2.54$ mm/inch = **0.1 inch per inch**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tailstock Set-Over Method

**Given:** Same taper as above ($D = 50$, $d = 40$, $L = 100$). Total workpiece length $L_w = 200$ mm. Find the tailstock set-over $S$.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$S = \dfrac{L_{w} \times (D - d)}{2L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$S = \dfrac{200 \times (50 - 40)}{2 \times 100} = \dfrac{200 \times 10}{200}$$

**Step 3 — Result:**

$$S = 10 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Note:** The tailstock body is shifted by 10 mm towards the operator (or away, depending on taper orientation). After the cut, the set-over must be **restored to zero** before the next operation.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Multi-Start Thread Axial Indexing

**Given:** Cut a 3-start thread of pitch $p = 2$ mm on a $\phi 30$ mm shaft. Find axial index per start.

**Step 1 — Recall indexing distance:**

$$I = \dfrac{p}{n} = \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Verify using Lead relationship:**

$$L = n \times p = 3 \times 2 = 6 \text{ mm per revolution}$$

**Step 3 — Verification with thread dial:**
If using a thread dial, the dial must be set so that it engages at the same fractional position for each start. For metric multi-start, **direct measurement with a dial indicator** is the standard KTU lab method.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Thread Cutting Depth (V-Thread)

**Given:** ISO Metric thread of pitch $p = 1.5$ mm. Find theoretical thread depth $h$.

**Step 1 — Standard ISO formula:**

$$h = 0.6134 \times p$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$h = 0.6134 \times 1.5 = 0.9201 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Practical cutting depth:** Since the V-thread has 60° included angle, the radial infeed is:

$$d_{cut} = h = 0.9201 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Number of passes for HSS tool:**
Typically 4–6 passes for $p = 1.5$ mm, with final spring pass of 0.02–0.05 mm for accuracy.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Combinations

| S.No | Given Data | Operations Involved | Key Formula Used | Final Answer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = 60$ mm, $V = 30$ m/min | Plain Turn | $N = 1000V/(\pi D)$ | $N = 159$ rpm |
| 2 | $L = 200$ mm, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev, $N = 250$ rpm | Plain Turn | $T_m = L/(fN)$ | $T_m = 2.67$ min |
| 3 | $D = 80$, $d = 60$, $L = 100$ mm | Taper (Compound) | $\tan(\alpha/2) = (D-d)/(2L)$ | $\alpha/2 = 5.71°$ |
| 4 | $D = 80$, $d = 60$, $L = 100$, $L_w = 300$ mm | Taper (Tailstock) | $S = L_w(D-d)/(2L)$ | $S = 30$ mm |
| 5 | $p = 2$ mm, $n = 4$ starts | Multi-Start | $I = p/n$, $L = np$ | $I = 0.5$ mm, $L = 8$ mm |
| 6 | $p = 1.75$ mm, V-thread | Thread | $h = 0.6134p$ | $h = 1.073$ mm |
| 7 | $p = 3$ mm, Square | Square Thread | $h = 0.5p$ | $h = 1.5$ mm |
| 8 | $D_o = 50$, $D_i = 20$ mm, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev, $N = 300$ rpm | Facing | $T_f = 0.5(D_o - D_i)/(fN)$ | $T_f = 0.33$ min |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centre Lathe — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Source 1.5 to 10 HP Motor] --> B[Headstock Spindle Gearbox]
    B --> C[Chuck 3 Jaw or 4 Jaw]
    C --> D[Workpiece Held Between Centers]
    D --> E[Cutting Tool Single Point HSS]
    E --> F[Carriage and Cross Slide]
    F --> G[Tool Post]
    G --> H[Lead Screw for Threading]
    B --> I[Change Gears for Feed]
    I --> F
    F --> J[Apron and Handwheel Feed Control]
    K[Tailstock with Dead Center] --> D
    L[Bedways hardened and ground] --> F
    L --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Power flows from the **motor → headstock → spindle → chuck → workpiece**. The **carriage** moves linearly along the bed using feed derived from the spindle via **change gears**. The **lead screw** drives the carriage at a precise ratio during **threading operations**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define "Facing" and state two precautions to be observed while performing facing on a centre lathe.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Facing is a lathe operation in which the workpiece is rotated about its axis and the cutting tool is fed **radially inward**, from the outer periphery toward the centre, to produce a **flat surface perpendicular to the axis of rotation**.

> **Precautions (2 marks):**
> 1. The cutting edge of the facing tool must be **exactly at the centre height** of the spindle; otherwise, the tool will "dig in" or produce a concave (dished) face.
> 2. The feed rate should be **reduced near the centre** because the cutting speed approaches zero at the rotational axis, which can cause rubbing and poor surface finish.
> 3. (Additional) Coolant should be applied to prevent overheating at the central region where heat concentrates.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between a V-thread and a Square thread. Give one application of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | V-Thread (60° ISO) | Square Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Thread angle | **60° included angle** | **0° (square profile)** |
| Efficiency | ~ 30–40 % | ~ 60–75 % |
| Application | General fastening (bolts, nuts) | Power transmission (screw jacks, lead screws) |

> **V-Thread Application (½ mark):** Used in general engineering fasteners, e.g., M10 bolt-nut pair.
>
> **Square Thread Application (½ mark):** Used in the **screw jack of a hydraulic press** for high load-bearing, low-friction power transmission.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme — End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern for Lab/Theory integrated courses:** Students must answer **ONE full question** from a choice of **TWO**. Each question carries **14 marks**, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** 
A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter 50 mm and length 200 mm is to be reduced to a diameter of 40 mm over a length of 120 mm by plain turning. Calculate:
**(i)** The spindle speed for a cutting speed of 25 m/min.
**(ii)** The machining time, if the feed is 0.25 mm/rev.
**(iii)** The metal removal rate, assuming depth of cut = 5 mm.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
With a neat sketch, explain the **Compound Rest Method** of taper turning. A taper of 1 in 20 is to be turned on a workpiece of length 150 mm. Determine the **compound rest swivel angle** to be set.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**(i) Spindle Speed:** Using $N = \dfrac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D}$, with the **average diameter** for the calculation:

$$D_{avg} = \dfrac{50 + 40}{2} = 45 \text{ mm}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 45} = \dfrac{25000}{141.37} \approx 176.8 \text{ rpm}$$

Rounding to nearest standard speed → **N = 180 rpm**.

**(ii) Machining Time:** $T_{m} = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$:

$$T_{m} = \dfrac{120}{0.25 \times 180} = \dfrac{120}{45} = 2.67 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) MRR:** $MRR = f \times d \times V$ (where $d$ = depth of cut in mm, $V$ in m/min):

$$MRR = 0.25 \times 5 \times 25 = 31.25 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Verification using $V$ in mm/min:** $V = 25 \times 1000 = 25000$ mm/min; $MRR = f \times d \times V = 0.25 \times 5 \times 25000 = 31250$ mm³/min. ✅ (Same value, different units).

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Compound Rest Method — Explanation (4 marks):**
The compound rest is graduated in degrees and can be swiveled about the cross-slide base. After swiveling, it can be locked. The tool is mounted on the compound rest, and **feed is given by rotating the compound rest handwheel only** (not the cross-slide or carriage).

When the compound rest is set to the **half taper angle (α/2)** and fed along a path parallel to the taper axis, the tool moves at an angle, producing a tapered surface.

**Sketch (1 mark):** [Student must draw: Lathe axis — Workpiece — Compound rest swiveled to α/2 — Tool feeding along the angled path].

**Numerical:**
Taper ratio = 1:20 = $\dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05$ mm/mm.

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.05) = 2.862°$$

$$\text{Compound rest setting} = \alpha/2 = \dfrac{2.862°}{2} = 1.431° \approx 1° 26'$$

**Final Answer:** The compound rest should be set to **1°26′** from the lathe axis.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain the procedure for cutting a **Square Thread of pitch 4 mm on a 30 mm diameter mild steel shaft**. Sketch the tool profile and state any **two advantages** of square threads over V-threads.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
What is **knurling**? With a diagram, explain the difference between **straight knurling and diamond knurling**. Why is knurling performed?

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. Mount and **face both ends** of the 30 mm shaft blank to required length.
2. **Plain turn** the major diameter to the nominal thread OD (≈ 30 mm) with a good surface finish.
3. **Grind the square thread tool** to the required profile:
   - Thread angle = 0° (square cross-section).
   - Width of cutting edge = **0.5 × pitch = 0.5 × 4 = 2 mm**.
   - Rake angle = **0° to 3°** (slight positive rake helps chip flow).
   - Front clearance = **3° to 5°**.
4. Set the **spindle speed to 1/3 of normal turning speed** (low for thread cutting).
5. Set the **half-nut lever** for thread engagement. Engage at the **same dial position each pass** for accuracy.
6. Set the **thread dial** to 4 mm pitch (metric).
7. Take **multiple light cuts** (typically 6–10 passes) of 0.3–0.5 mm radial infeed each.
8. Final pass should be a **spring cut** of 0.02–0.05 mm for accuracy.
9. **Inspect** the thread with a **square thread pitch gauge** and **thread ring gauge**.

**Tool Profile Sketch (1 mark):** Square profile with 0° rake, 0° clearance on flanks, 2 mm width.

**Advantages of Square Threads (1 mark, any two):**
1. **Higher efficiency (60–75%)** compared to V-threads (30–40%) due to perpendicular force transmission.
2. **Better load-bearing capacity** — entire flank surface carries the load.
3. **No radial thrust** — axial force only, ideal for power transmission.

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Knurling is a cold-working process in which a patterned impression is made on a cylindrical surface using a **knurling tool** fitted with hardened, patterned rollers. The pattern is **pressed into the workpiece surface**, not cut away.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
    Straight Knurl Pattern:         Diamond Knurl Pattern:
    ┌───────────────┐              ┌───────────────┐
    │ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║  │              │ ╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲  │
    │ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║ ║  │              │ ╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱╲╱  │
    └───────────────┘              └───────────────┘
    (Parallel ridges)              (Cross-hatched diamond)
```

**Difference Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Straight Knurl | Diamond (Cross) Knurl |
|---|---|---|
| Pattern | Parallel lines along the axis | Criss-cross diamond pattern |
| Grip | Moderate | Superior (omnidirectional) |
| Use | Decorative, light grip | Thumbscrews, adjusting nuts |

**Why Knurling is Done (2 marks):**
1. **To improve grip** on parts that are hand-adjusted frequently (micrometer thimbles, knob shafts).
2. **To increase the effective diameter** slightly for press-fit assemblies.
3. **To provide a non-slip surface** on handles and levers.
4. **To mark identification** (e.g., knurled reference marks on graduated knobs).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 2 Lathe Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Feed" units** — KTU expects feed in **mm/revolution** in formulae, not mm/min. Using the wrong unit is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Spindle Speed using initial diameter** — Always use the **average (mean) diameter** of the start and end diameter, not the initial diameter. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Taper half-angle confusion** — Students often set the compound rest to the **full taper angle (α)** instead of **α/2**. The compound rest MUST be set to the **half-angle**.
> 4. **Thread depth formula** — Mixing V-thread ($0.6134p$) and Square thread ($0.5p$) formulas. KTU is very strict about which formula applies to which thread type.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — $V$ in m/min, $D$ in mm, $L$ in mm, $f$ in mm/rev. Wrong unit in even one quantity → final answer is wrong → 2 marks lost.
> 6. **Not mentioning half-nut engagement** in threading solutions — KTU examiners deduct ½ to 1 mark for omitting this critical operational step.
> 7. **Skipping the sketch** in taper turning questions — At least **1–2 marks are reserved for a labelled sketch** in the KTU answer key. A well-labelled diagram can recover lost marks.
> 8. **Multi-start indexing** — The most common error is indexing by **full pitch (p)** instead of **p/n**. This produces a single-start thread, not multi-start.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **12-Point Rapid Revision — Module Experiment 2 (Centre Lathe Operations)**

- [ ] **Facing** = radial feed from OD to centre → flat square face.
- [ ] **Plain Turning** = longitudinal feed → cylindrical surface.
- [ ] **Step Turning** = multiple plain-turning steps → multiple diameters; **turn largest diameter FIRST**.
- [ ] **Taper Turning** = 3 methods: Form Tool, Compound Rest (set to **α/2**), Tailstock Set-Over ($S = L_w(D-d)/(2L)$).
- [ ] **Form Turning** = special profile tool; tool profile = **negative** of workpiece profile.
- [ ] **Groove Cutting** = narrow blade fed radially; for circlips, oil seals, thread run-out.
- [ ] **Knurling** = cold working, NOT cutting; **diamond knurl gives best grip**.
- [ ] **Chamfering** = 45° edge break; **standard size = 1 × 45°** unless specified.
- [ ] **Multi-Start Thread** = axial index = **p/n** per start; lead = **n × p**.
- [ ] **Square Thread** = depth = **0.5p**; zero rake; tool width = 0.5p.
- [ ] **Internal Thread** = boring bar + single point tool OR tap; cutting fluid essential.
- [ ] **Inspection** = Vernier (±0.02 mm), Micrometer (±0.01 mm), Thread Gauge, Radius Gauge, Surface Comparator.

### Critical Formula Recall (Memorize These 5):

$$N = \dfrac{1000V}{\pi D}, \quad T_m = \dfrac{L}{fN}, \quad \tan(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{D-d}{2L}, \quad h_{V} = 0.6134p, \quad h_{sq} = 0.5p$$

### Standard KTU Lab Viva Quick-Fire Facts:

- **Q: What is the standard rake angle for mild steel with HSS tool?** → 10°–15°.
- **Q: What is the Morse Taper angle?** → 1.49° half-angle (≈ 1°30′).
- **Q: What is the standard cutting speed for threading?** → 5–10 m/min.
- **Q: What is the function of a half-nut in a lathe?** → Engages the lead screw for synchronized carriage movement during thread cutting.
- **Q: Why is knurling called a "cold working" process?** → Material is plastically displaced, not removed; no chips are produced.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Face Milling, End Milling, Spur and Helical Gear Cutting, Milling of Key Ways, T-Slot Cutting, and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Face Milling, End Milling, Spur and Helical Gear Cutting, Milling of Key Ways, T-Slot Cutting, and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Universal Milling Machine
A **milling machine** is a versatile machine tool that uses a multi-tooth rotary cutting tool (the *cutter* or *milling cutter*) to progressively remove material from a stationary or slowly moving workpiece. The cutting action is fundamentally *interrupted cutting* — each tooth of the cutter engages the workpiece only for a fraction of every revolution. This intermittent engagement, combined with the ability to mount dozens of different cutter geometries on a common spindle, makes the milling machine the "Swiss Army Knife" of a mechanical workshop.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Milling is a machining process in which a rotary multi-point cutter is used to remove material from a workpiece by advancing (or feeding) the workpiece against the cutter at a predetermined angle to the cutter axis.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Woodworking Plane" Mental Model
Imagine a carpenter's hand plane used to shave wood. The blade protrudes at a fixed height and is dragged across the surface. Now replace that single blade with a *spinning cylinder that carries ten identical blades arranged around its rim* — every time the cylinder rotates, each blade "shaves" a tiny chip from the wood. Spin the cylinder fast, push the wood sideways slowly, and you have a milling operation. The spinning cylinder is your **milling cutter**, and the slowly moving wood is the **workpiece feed**.

### 1.3 The Six Core Milling Exercises of KTU Module 2

| # | Operation | Cutter Type | Primary Purpose |
|---|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Face Milling** | Fly cutter / Face mill | Produces a flat surface perpendicular to cutter axis |
| 2 | **End Milling** | End mill (flat, ball, bull-nose) | Produces slots, pockets, profiles, 3D contours |
| 3 | **Spur Gear Cutting** | Horizontal milling cutter (involute) | Generates spur gear teeth on a blank |
| 4 | **Helical Gear Cutting** | Helical milling cutter | Generates helical gear teeth on a blank |
| 5 | **Key Way Cutting** | Woodruff cutter or end mill | Cuts a semi-circular or rectangular key slot |
| 6 | **T-Slot Cutting** | T-slot cutter (staggered tooth) | Cuts a T-shaped slot in a milled surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** The above six operations are *universally* asked in the Part A 3-mark question bank. Memorize the cutter name, the workpiece holding device, and one unique safety precaution for each.

### 1.4 The Kinematic Truth Behind All Milling
Regardless of which operation you perform, the fundamental cutting parameters remain identical:

$$\boxed{\text{Cutting Speed } (V_c) = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad [\text{m/min}]}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Spindle Speed } (N) = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} \quad [\text{rpm}]}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Table Feed } (V_f) = f_z \cdot z \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]}$$

Where:
- $D$ = cutter diameter (**mm**)
- $N$ = spindle rotational speed (**rpm**)
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth (**mm/tooth**)
- $z$ = number of effective cutting teeth on the cutter

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** Always convert *cutting speed* $V_c$ from m/min to mm/min by multiplying by 1000, because $D$ is universally given in mm. This is the **#1 mark-killer** in KTU milling numericals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Face Milling — The "Smoothing Iron" of the Workshop
A face mill (or fly cutter) has its cutting teeth arranged on the *periphery* **and** on the *face* of a circular body. The cutter is mounted on an **arbor** whose axis is perpendicular to the workpiece surface. Cutting forces are predominantly axial, which is why a *stiff, short workpiece projection* is mandatory.

**Operating Principle:** The face mill is fed across the workpiece, and the peripheral teeth do the *primary* roughing while the face teeth (inserts) provide a *finishing* action. The insert geometry (rake angle, clearance angle) determines the surface finish.

**Key Cutter Geometry Parameters:**
- **Lead Angle (κ):** Angle between the cutting edge and the workpiece surface. Typical κ = 45° or 75°.
- **Cutter Diameter (D):** Determines contact area and chip load.
- **Insert Geometry:** Square (SEKN), round (RPMT), or octagonal.

**Best Practice Rule:** For maximum tool life, the **radial engagement** (width of cut $a_e$) should be **less than 75% of the cutter diameter** to allow the insert to exit the cut cleanly.

### 2.2 End Milling — The "Sculptor's Chisel"
An end mill resembles a drill bit but has cutting edges on both the *periphery* and the *end face*. This dual-cutting ability allows it to:
- Plunge vertically into the workpiece (drilling action),
- Mill horizontally (peripheral cutting),
- Produce pockets, slots, and complex 3D profiles.

**Two Major Classes of End Mills:**
1. **Peripheral Cutting End Mill** — Cutter diameter ≤ workpiece width; flute ends in a flat or ball nose.
2. **Face Milling End Mill** — Cutter diameter > workpiece width; performs both face and peripheral cutting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direction of Cut Convention:** In **conventional (up) milling**, the cutter rotates *opposite* to the feed direction. In **climb (down) milling**, the cutter rotates *with* the feed. KTU labs predominantly use **up milling** because backlash in the lead screw is taken up, but the part surface is slightly work-hardened. Modern CNC favors climb milling for better finish.

### 2.3 Spur Gear Cutting on a Horizontal Mill
Spur gears are cut on a universal milling machine using an **involute gear cutter** (a disc-shaped cutter with a profile machined to match the involute tooth space of a specific module/pitch). The cutter is mounted on an **arbor**, the gear blank is held on a **mandrel between centers**, and the blank is rotated by a **spiral dividing head** (indexing head) coupled to the table lead screw.

**The Indexing Head (Dividing Head) — The Heart of Gear Cutting:**
A dividing head has a **worm gear ratio of 40:1**, meaning **40 turns of the crank** produce **1 turn of the spindle** (and thus 1 turn of the workpiece).

**Simple Indexing Formula:**
$$\boxed{N = \frac{40}{x}}$$

Where $N$ = number of crank turns, and $x$ = number of equal divisions required on the workpiece.

**For a gear with $T$ teeth, the crank must be turned $\frac{40}{T}$ revolutions per tooth space.**

### 2.4 Helical Gear Cutting — The Twisted Tooth
A helical gear is essentially a spur gear whose teeth are twisted along the axis at a **helix angle (β)**. To cut helical teeth on a universal mill:
1. The **indexing head spindle** is tilted by the **helix angle β** from the horizontal.
2. A **helical milling cutter** (a cutter with helical flutes) is used.
3. The **table lead screw** is geared to the dividing head spindle via a **change gear train** that synchronizes the table feed with the spindle rotation.

**Lead of the Helical Gear:**
$$L = \pi \cdot D \cdot \cot(\beta)$$

**Change Gear Ratio for Helical Cutting:**
$$\boxed{\frac{\text{Table Lead Screw}}{\text{Dividing Head Spindle}} = \frac{L_s \times \sin\beta}{T}}$$

Where $L_s$ = lead of the table lead screw (commonly 6 mm or 10 mm for standard mills).

### 2.5 Key Way Milling
Key ways are milled using either a **Woodruff cutter** (produces a semi-circular bottom slot) or a **plain milling cutter + end mill combination** (produces a rectangular bottom slot). The workpiece is held in a **milling vice**, and the depth is controlled by the **vertical feed hand wheel**.

**Woodruff Cutter Geometry:**
- Standard sizes: 1/2" (12.7 mm) to 1-1/2" (38.1 mm).
- Cutter diameter = width of the keyway.
- Cutter thickness = depth of the keyway.

**Feed Direction Convention:** For Woodruff cutters, always feed *into the workpiece from the periphery* to avoid chatter. The cutter rotates *with* the climb direction (down milling) to give a clean cut on the bottom.

### 2.6 T-Slot Cutting — The Three-Operation Sequence
A T-slot has a profile that cannot be cut in a single pass. The standard KTU sequence is:

| Operation | Cutter | Purpose | Cutter Dia. (D) |
|-----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Slitting saw / end mill** | Cut a rectangular slot | $D_1$ (slot width) |
| 2 | **End mill** | Enlarge slot to T-slot depth | $D_2 > D_1$ |
| 3 | **T-slot cutter** | Cut the undercut T profile | $D_3$ matches T-slot standard |

**T-Slot Cutter Geometry:** A T-slot cutter has a *small diameter shank* and a *larger diameter cutting head* (with staggered teeth) — it must plunge vertically, hence the *shank-first entry* into the pre-milled slot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Three Operations?** A T-slot cutter *cannot plunge into solid material*. The shank is narrower than the cutting diameter, so it must enter a pre-existing slot. Trying to plunge a T-slot cutter into solid steel is the **#1 cause of T-slot cutter breakage** in KTU labs.

### 2.7 Inspection — The "Trust but Verify" Step
After every milling operation, dimensions are verified using:

| Measuring Instrument | Best Used For | Least Count |
|----------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Vernier Caliper** | External/internal lengths up to 300 mm | **0.02 mm** |
| **Micrometer (External)** | Shaft diameter, keyway width | **0.01 mm** |
| **Dial Indicator (DTI)** | Run-out, parallelism, flatness | **0.01 mm** |
| **Depth Micrometer** | Slot depth, T-slot depth | **0.01 mm** |
| **Gear Tooth Vernier** | Tooth thickness (chordal) | **0.02 mm** |
| **Plug Gauge / Ring Gauge** | Go/No-Go inspection of keyway | **Binary** |
| **Surface Plate + Scriber** | Flatness check, marking | Visual |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Lab Manual Mandate:** Every experiment in PCMRL408 must conclude with a *tabular measurement record* signed by the lab instructor. Submitting a record without the signature **invalidates the experiment**.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Cutting Speed $V_c$ | $V_c = \pi D N$ | m/min | Multiply N×π×D, then ÷1000 |
| Spindle Speed $N$ | $N = \frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ | rpm | mm-based formula |
| Table Feed $V_f$ | $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_z$ from machinist handbook |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width |
| Simple Indexing $N$ | $N = \frac{40}{T}$ | turns | $T$ = no. of teeth/divisions |
| Circular Pitch $P_c$ | $P_c = \pi \cdot m$ | mm | $m$ = module |
| Diametral Pitch $P$ | $P = \frac{T}{D}$ | teeth/inch | Imperial gear formula |
| Helix Angle $\beta$ | $\tan\beta = \frac{L}{\pi D}$ | degrees | $L$ = lead, $D$ = PCD |
| Helical Change Gear | $i = \frac{L_s \cdot \sin\beta}{T}$ | ratio | $L_s$ = lead screw pitch |
| Keyway Depth (Rectangular) | $h = 0.45D - r$ | mm | For shaft key, $D$ = shaft dia. |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar symbol has been intentionally avoided in all table entries. For absolute-value expressions in derivations, the `\vert` LaTeX command is used.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Spindle Speed Formula
The cutting speed $V_c$ is the linear velocity at which the cutter periphery moves through the air (or workpiece). For a cutter of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ revolutions per minute:

**Step 1 — Circumference of the cutter:**
$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — Linear distance covered per minute:**
For $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear distance covered is $N$ times the circumference:
$$V_c = N \cdot C = N \cdot \pi \cdot D \quad [\text{mm/min}]$$

**Step 3 — Unit conversion to meters per minute:**
The KTU board expects $V_c$ in **m/min** while $D$ is typically in **mm**. We divide by 1000:
$$V_c \,[\text{m/min}] = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $N$:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad [\text{rpm}]$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Face Milling Cutter Speed
**Given:** A face mill of diameter $D = 100$ mm, recommended cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min for mild steel.
**Find:** Spindle speed $N$ and table feed $V_f$ for a 4-tooth cutter with $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth.

**Step 1:** Apply the spindle speed formula:
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{25000}{314.159} = 79.58 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2:** Round to the nearest standard spindle speed on the milling machine (commonly 80 rpm on a vertical mill):
$$\boxed{N = 80 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the table feed formula:
$$V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.15 \times 4 \times 80 = 48 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 48 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Simple Indexing Formula
A dividing head contains a **40-tooth worm wheel** driven by a **single-thread worm**. Therefore:

**Step 1 — Worm ratio:**
1 revolution of the worm → 1/40 revolution of the worm wheel.
Equivalently, 40 revolutions of the crank → 1 revolution of the workpiece spindle.

**Step 2 — For $T$ equal divisions:**
Each division requires a rotation of $\frac{1}{T}$ of a full circle.
The crank must therefore turn:
$$N = \frac{1}{T} \times 40 = \frac{40}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Selecting the index plate hole circle:**
The crank must be turned to an integer number of complete holes on the index plate. The standard set of index plates (Brown & Sharpe type) provides hole circles of 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49. For fractional indexing, use the **compound indexing** technique.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Indexing a 47-Tooth Gear
**Given:** Spur gear with $T = 47$ teeth.
**Find:** Number of crank turns and hole circle to use.

**Step 1 — Apply the simple indexing formula:**
$$N = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{47}$$

**Step 2 — This is a non-terminating decimal** (≈ 0.851 turns), which is impossible with a 40-tooth worm wheel using *simple* indexing. We must convert to a hole count:

$$N = \frac{40}{47} = \frac{40 \times k}{47 \times k}$$

**Step 3 — Choose $k$ such that $47k \le 49$** (the largest standard hole circle). Taking $k=1$: We need to find an integer *closest* to $\frac{40}{47}$ on a standard hole plate.

The fraction $\frac{40}{47} = \frac{22}{25.85}$, which is approximated by $\frac{33}{39}$ on the 39-hole circle (≈ 0.8462 turns). This is a **compound indexing** problem.

$$\boxed{\text{For odd teeth, always expect compound indexing in KTU exams.}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Helical Gear Change-Gear Ratio
For a helical gear, the workpiece must rotate as the table traverses. The lead screw advances the table by $L_s$ mm per spindle revolution, and the workpiece must rotate through a fraction of a turn equal to the gear lead $L$ per full table traverse.

**Step 1 — Required table traverse for one full helical revolution:**
For a gear of $T$ teeth, one full helix pitch in the axial direction corresponds to the *circular pitch* $P_c$ multiplied by $T$. Equivalently, the lead is $L = P_c \cdot T = \pi m T$.

**Step 2 — Synchronization condition:**
The table must advance $L$ mm while the dividing head spindle rotates exactly **1 turn**. The lead screw advances $L_s$ mm per turn, so the gear ratio between the table lead-screw gear and the dividing-head worm-wheel gear is:
$$i = \frac{L}{L_s} = \frac{\pi \cdot m \cdot T}{L_s}$$

**Step 3 — Adjusting for the 40:1 worm reduction:**
The dividing head crank turns 40 times for every 1 turn of the spindle, so the *change gear* ratio between the lead screw and the crank is:
$$\boxed{i = \frac{40 \cdot L}{L_s \cdot T} = \frac{40 \cdot \pi \cdot m}{L_s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the simplified form used in KTU board exams. The full derivation is rarely asked, but the *final form* and *one numerical substitution* is a **guaranteed 7-mark question**.

### 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Helical Gear Change Gears
**Given:** Helical gear with module $m = 3$ mm, $T = 30$ teeth, helix angle $\beta = 20°$, lead screw pitch $L_s = 6$ mm.
**Find:** Change gear train ratio.

**Step 1 — Compute the lead:**
$$L = \pi \cdot m \cdot T = \pi \times 3 \times 30 = 282.74 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**
$$i = \frac{40 \times L}{L_s \times T} = \frac{40 \times 282.74}{6 \times 30} = \frac{11309.6}{180} = 62.83$$

**Step 3 — Practical gear train:**
The ratio 62.83 must be expressed as a product of available change gears (e.g., 60T, 80T, 90T, 100T). The closest approximation is:
$$i \approx \frac{100}{30} \times \frac{80}{42} = 63.49 \text{ (within 1% error)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Change gears: } 100T \text{ (driver) } \to 30T \text{ (driven), } 80T \text{ (driver) } \to 42T \text{ (driven)}}$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cutter for Each Operation

| Operation | Cutter Type | Cutter Material | Cutter Geometry | Typical $V_c$ (M.S.) |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Face Milling | Indexable face mill | Carbide (P20) | 45° lead, square insert | 150–200 m/min |
| End Milling (HSS) | 4-flute end mill | HSS | Helix 30° | 25–35 m/min |
| Spur Gear Cutting | Disc-type involute cutter | HSS | Profile ground | 20–25 m/min |
| Helical Gear Cutting | Disc-type involute cutter (helical) | HSS | Helical teeth | 20–25 m/min |
| Keyway (Woodruff) | Woodruff cutter | HSS | Concave profile | 25–30 m/min |
| T-Slot Cutting | T-slot cutter | HSS | Staggered teeth | 15–20 m/min |

> [!TIP]
> **Why HSS for gears but Carbide for face milling?** Gear cutting is *interrupted cutting* with high tool wear at the involute profile — HSS is easier to re-grind to the exact involute curve. Carbide is reserved for high-volume production face milling where re-grinding is not economical.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — The Six Milling Operations Interlock

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Mounting Stage] --> B[Marking and Layout]
    B --> C{Face Milling}
    C --> D[Reference Surface Established]
    D --> E{End Milling}
    E --> F[Slot or Pocket Produced]
    F --> G{Key Way Milling}
    G --> H[Keyway Cut for Shaft Fit]
    H --> I{T-Slot Cutting}
    I --> J[T-Slot Cut for Fixture]
    J --> K{Spur Gear Cutting}
    K --> L[Gear Blank Held on Mandrel]
    L --> M{Helical Gear Cutting}
    M --> N[Indexing Head Tilted to Helix Angle]
    N --> O[Change Gears Engaged]
    O --> P[Final Inspection on Surface Plate]
    P --> Q[Measurement Record Signed by Instructor]
    Q --> R[Experiment Complete]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Mapped Block Diagram — Cutter-to-Operation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Select Cutter] --> B{Operation Type}
    B -- Flat Surface --> C[Face Mill or Fly Cutter]
    B -- Slot or Pocket --> D[End Mill]
    B -- Gear Teeth --> E{Cutter Profile}
    E -- Straight Teeth --> F[Spur Gear Cutter]
    E -- Twisted Teeth --> G[Helical Gear Cutter]
    B -- Key Slot --> H{Keyway Type}
    H -- Semi-Circular --> I[Woodruff Cutter]
    H -- Rectangular --> J[End Mill or Side and Face Cutter]
    B -- T-Slot --> K[T-Slot Cutter]
    C --> L[Mount on Vertical Spindle]
    D --> L
    F --> M[Mount on Horizontal Arbor]
    G --> M
    I --> M
    J --> L
    K --> L

    style A fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Kinematic Chain of the Universal Milling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Motor] --> GB[Gear Box]
    GB --> SP[Spindle]
    SP --> AR[Arbor]
    AR --> CT[Cutter]
    SP -- 40:1 Worm --> DH[Dividing Head]
    DH --> WS[Workpiece Spindle]
    WS --> WB[Workpiece Blank]
    M --> TL[Table Lead Screw]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short Answers)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List any three safety precautions to be observed while operating a milling machine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Before starting the machine**, ensure the workpiece is *rigidly clamped* in the vice or to the table using appropriate clamps and bolts. Loose workpieces can become deadly projectiles.
2. **Always use a guard** around the cutter and keep loose clothing, hair, and jewelry away from the rotating spindle.
3. **Never attempt to measure the workpiece** while the cutter is rotating. Stop the machine, retract the cutter, and then measure using a Vernier or micrometer.
*(1 mark per correct precaution, maximum 3)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"What is the difference between up milling and down milling?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Up Milling (Conventional) | Down Milling (Climb) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Cutter rotation vs. feed | Opposite direction | Same direction |
| Chip thickness | Starts at zero, ends at maximum | Starts at maximum, ends at zero |
| Surface finish | Slightly work-hardened surface | Better surface finish |
| Tool wear | Higher (tooth rubs before cutting) | Lower (direct cutting engagement) |
| Backlash effect | Not affected by lead-screw backlash | Requires backlash-free lead screw |

*(1½ marks for the difference, 1½ marks for the comparative table)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure for **cutting a spur gear** on a universal milling machine using a horizontal milling cutter. List the cutter set required for a module of 3 mm and a 24-tooth gear. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spur gear with **40 teeth and module 4 mm** is to be cut on a milling machine. Calculate:
(i) Pitch circle diameter
(ii) Circular pitch
(iii) Addendum and dedendum
(iv) Number of indexing crank turns per tooth space
(v) Outside and root diameters
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Spur Gear Cutting Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Workpiece preparation (1 mark):** Calculate the gear blank diameter $D = m \times T = 3 \times 24 = 72$ mm. Face mill the blank flat and parallel, drill a central hole, and mount on a mandrel between the dividing head center and the tailstock center.

2. **Cutter selection (2 marks):** For module 3 mm, use a *set of 8 involute gear cutters* (numbered 1 to 8) and select the one corresponding to 24 teeth. The rule is:
   - Cutter No. 1 → 12 to 13 teeth
   - Cutter No. 2 → 14 to 16 teeth
   - Cutter No. 3 → 17 to 20 teeth
   - **Cutter No. 4 → 21 to 25 teeth (selected)**
   - Cutter No. 5 → 26 to 34 teeth
   - Cutter No. 6 → 35 to 54 teeth
   - Cutter No. 7 → 55 to 134 teeth
   - Cutter No. 8 → 135 teeth and above (rack)

3. **Machine setup (2 marks):** Mount the gear cutter on the horizontal arbor. Set the dividing head for 24 divisions using $N = \frac{40}{24} = 1\frac{2}{3}$ turns = 1 full turn + 16 holes on the 24-hole circle. Set the table traverse, adjust the cutter to the correct center height, and align the blank tooth space with the cutter.

4. **Cutting operation (1 mark):** Take a light depth of cut (≈ 1 to 2 mm per pass), mill all 24 tooth spaces using the indexing sequence, and inspect with a gear tooth vernier.

5. **Safety and inspection (1 mark):** Use a guard, never reach across the cutter, and verify tooth thickness and profile after every 4–5 teeth.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $T = 40$, $m = 4$ mm.

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter (1 mark):**
$$D = m \times T = 4 \times 40 = 160 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Circular Pitch (1 mark):**
$$P_c = \pi \times m = \pi \times 4 = 12.566 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Addendum and Dedendum (2 marks):**
$$\text{Addendum } (a) = m = 4 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Dedendum } (b) = 1.25m = 1.25 \times 4 = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Indexing Crank Turns (1.5 marks):**
$$N = \frac{40}{T} = \frac{40}{40} = 1 \text{ full turn per tooth space}$$
Use the 40-hole circle, 1 turn + 0 holes = exactly 1 full revolution.

**(v) Outside and Root Diameters (1.5 marks):**
$$D_o = D + 2a = 160 + 2(4) = 168 \text{ mm}$$
$$D_r = D - 2b = 160 - 2(5) = 150 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (a):** Students often confuse the *pitch circle diameter* with the *blank diameter* and end up turning the blank to the wrong size. The blank is the *outside diameter* (addendum circle), NOT the pitch circle! Many students lose 1–2 marks here by stating $D = m \times T$ as the blank diameter.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **T-slot cutting operation** on a vertical milling machine. Why is it necessary to perform the operation in three stages? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **helical gear** has 30 teeth, a module of 3 mm, and a helix angle of 20°. The lead screw of the milling machine has a pitch of 6 mm. Calculate:
(i) Pitch circle diameter
(ii) Helical lead
(iii) Change gear ratio required
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — T-Slot Cutting Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Definition and application (1 mark):** A T-slot is a T-shaped groove machined into a workpiece (typically a machine tool table or fixture) to accommodate the head of a T-bolt for clamping. Standard dimensions are governed by DIN 650 / IS 2011.

2. **Stage 1 — Slitting saw / small end mill (2 marks):** A slitting saw (or small end mill) of diameter equal to the *neck width* of the T-slot (typically 8–14 mm) is used to cut a rectangular slot of the required depth (≈ 1.5 to 2 times the T-slot depth). The cutter is mounted directly on the vertical spindle.

3. **Stage 2 — End mill of larger diameter (2 marks):** An end mill of diameter equal to the *lower flange width* of the T-slot is plunged into the pre-milled slot and traversed to the full T-slot depth. This creates the rectangular channel required for the T-slot cutter to enter.

4. **Stage 3 — T-slot cutter (2 marks):** The T-slot cutter (a special profile cutter with a narrow shank and a wider cutting head) is lowered into the pre-milled slot, and the table is traversed to cut the undercut profile on both sides.

5. **Three-stage rationale (1 mark — must answer explicitly):** A T-slot cutter has a *narrow shank* and a *wider cutting head*. It cannot plunge into solid material because the cutting head is wider than the shank. Hence, a pre-milled slot is mandatory for the shank to enter.

> **Sketch Description (to be drawn on exam paper):** Top view of T-slot showing three regions — top flange (widest), neck (narrowest), and bottom (intermediate). Label dimensions: $a$ = flange width, $b$ = neck width, $h$ = total depth, $h_1$ = neck depth.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $T = 30$, $m = 3$ mm, $\beta = 20°$, $L_s = 6$ mm.

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter (1.5 marks):**
$$D = m \times T = 3 \times 30 = 90 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Helical Lead (2 marks):**
$$L = \pi \times D \times \cot\beta = \pi \times 90 \times \cot 20°$$
$$L = 282.74 \times 2.7475 = 776.78 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Change Gear Ratio (3.5 marks):**
$$i = \frac{40 \times L}{L_s \times T} = \frac{40 \times 776.78}{6 \times 30} = \frac{31071.2}{180} = 172.62$$

**Practical gear train selection:**
- Compound gear train with two stages: $i = \frac{A}{B} \times \frac{C}{D}$
- Approximation: $\frac{100}{25} \times \frac{90}{21} = 4 \times 4.286 = 171.43$ (within 0.7% error — acceptable)
- Final selection: Drivers A = 100T, C = 90T; Drivens B = 25T, D = 21T.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Helical Gear Numerical:** A common error is to forget to *convert the helix angle* to a tangent/cotangent value correctly. Using $\tan 20° = 0.364$ (correct) and $\cot 20° = 2.747$ (correct) is critical. Also, students often use $L = \pi m T$ instead of $L = \pi D \cot \beta$ — both give the same answer if $D = mT$, but the latter is preferred when the helix angle is given explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This List and Carry It to the Lab Exam**

- [x] **Milling cutter rotates; workpiece is held stationary** (or fed linearly) — the reverse of a lathe.
- [x] **Cutting speed $V_c$** depends *only* on cutter material + workpiece material, NOT on diameter.
- [x] **Spindle speed $N$** depends on $V_c$ and $D$: $N = \frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$.
- [x] **Always multiply $V_c$ by 1000** when $D$ is in mm — the most common unit-conversion error.
- [x] **Face milling** uses a cutter perpendicular to the surface; **end milling** uses a cutter parallel to the surface and can plunge.
- [x] **Dividing head ratio is 40:1** for almost all universal mills — memorize $N = 40/T$.
- [x] **Gear cutter set** has 8 cutters (No. 1 to 8); selection depends on number of teeth.
- [x] **Helical gear** requires dividing head tilted by helix angle $\beta$ AND a change-gear train.
- [x] **T-slot cutting is a 3-stage process** — slitting saw → end mill → T-slot cutter.
- [x] **Woodruff cutter** always cuts with *climb milling direction* to avoid chatter.
- [x] **Inspection** = Vernier (0.02 mm) + Micrometer (0.01 mm) + Dial Indicator (0.01 mm).
- [x] **Lab record must be signed by the instructor** on the same day as the experiment.
- [x] **Addendum = m, Dedendum = 1.25 m, Pressure angle = 20°** — these are universal for metric gears.
- [x] **Module m = D/T** — remember this three-letter formula and you can derive everything else.
- [x] **MRR (Material Removal Rate)** is reported in mm³/min and equals $a_p \times a_e \times V_f$.
- [x] **Safety goggles, no loose clothing, never measure while the cutter is running** — KTU external examiners check safety awareness in viva voce.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCMRL408 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Drilling Machine: Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping, and Counter Sinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Drilling Machine — Formal Definition

A **Drilling Machine** is a stationary or portable machine tool designed primarily to produce cylindrical holes in solid workpieces by rotating a multi-point cutting tool (the **drill bit**) against a stationary workpiece, using a combination of **rotary motion (primary)** and **linear axial motion (secondary feed)**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Manual for PCMRL408, the bench/pedestal drilling machine used in the workshop is a **vertical-spindle, gear-driven, manually fed** machine tool classified under the broader category of **hole-making operations** in manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL408 – Module 2):**
> The student must be able to **identify, set up, and execute** Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping, and Counter Sinking operations on a given workpiece using a bench/pedestal drilling machine, with emphasis on **tool selection, cutting parameter calculation, and verification of dimensional accuracy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a carpenter using an awl to puncture a wooden plank. The **drill bit** is essentially a metal awl, but it spins extremely fast (hundreds of RPM) and is pushed slowly downward. Each sharp cutting edge on the drill tip shaves a tiny ribbon of metal (a **chip**) from inside the hole, much like a tiny chisel biting into butter.

- **Drilling** = Making a brand-new hole from scratch.
- **Boring** = Taking a hole that already exists and making it **bigger and more accurate** (like widening a doorway in a house).
- **Reaming** = **Polishing/smoothing** the inside of an existing hole to a precise size and fine surface finish (like sandpapering a wall after widening it).
- **Tapping** = Cutting **internal screw threads** inside a hole so a bolt can screw in (like carving spiral grooves inside a pipe).
- **Counter Sinking** = Cutting a **conical chamfer** at the top of a hole so that a flat-head screw can sit flush with the surface (like carving a small funnel at the entrance of the hole).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for Steel (Mild Steel / EN8 / HRS):** Standard recommended cutting speed for HSS twist drills = **V = 25–30 m/min**. For Aluminium = **V = 60–90 m/min**, for Cast Iron = **V = 20–25 m/min**.

## 1.3 Classification of Drilling Operations

| Operation | Purpose | Tool Used | Accuracy Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drilling** | Initial hole making | Twist Drill | IT 12 – IT 13 |
| **Boring** | Enlarge & align hole | Boring Bar / Tool | IT 7 – IT 9 |
| **Reaming** | Finish hole to size & finish | Reamer | IT 6 – IT 8 |
| **Tapping** | Cut internal threads | Tap | Thread fit (6H/6g) |
| **Counter Sinking** | Conical recess for screw | Counter-sink | Angled 60°/82°/90°/120° |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drilling Machine — Main Parts

A **Bench/Pedestal Drilling Machine** consists of the following functional subsystems:

1. **Base** – Supports the entire machine; provides rigidity.
2. **Column** – Vertical pillar; houses the spindle and elevating mechanism.
3. **Head** – Contains the driving motor, gear box, and spindle.
4. **Spindle** – Rotating shaft that holds the drill chuck.
5. **Chuck** – Three/four-jaw device that grips the tool shank.
6. **Feed Mechanism** – Hand-operated lever (for bench/pedestal) that pushes the spindle down.
7. **Worktable** – Adjustable table with T-slots for clamping the workpiece.
8. **Depth Stop** – Adjustable collar to set hole depth precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Principle:** The motor drives the spindle via a **step-pulley (cone pulley) or all-geared head**, providing discrete spindle speeds. The operator manually applies feed through the **quill feed handle**, which advances the rotating tool into the workpiece.

## 2.2 The Twist Drill — Nomenclature

A standard **HSS (High Speed Steel) twist drill** has the following geometric features:

- **Body / Shank** – Cylindrical portion held by the chuck.
- **Flutes** – Helical grooves that allow chip evacuation.
- **Cutting Edges / Lips** – Two sharp edges that perform cutting.
- **Point / Chisel Edge** – The sharp pyramidal tip joining the two lips.
- **Margin / Land** – Narrow cylindrical strip that guides the drill in the hole.
- **Body Clearance (Flank Relief)** – Provides relief behind the cutting edge.
- **Web** – Central core of the drill providing strength.
- **Drill Point Angle** – Standard = **118°** for general steel; **135°** for hardened steels and aluminium; **90°** for soft / non-ferrous materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Drill Point Angles (Memorise for KTU Viva):**
> - **118°** → General purpose (mild steel, cast iron)
> - **135°** → Stainless steel, hardened alloys, deep holes
> - **90°** → Brass, aluminium, plastics

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Each Operation

### 2.3.1 Drilling
- **Function:** Produce a solid cylindrical hole of the drill diameter in a solid workpiece.
- **Tool:** Twist drill.
- **Mechanics:** Each lip removes material; the **chisel edge** is responsible for the central indentation but generates high thrust force.
- **Coolant:** Use cutting fluid (sulphurised oil) for ferrous metals; soluble oil for aluminium; dry for cast iron.

### 2.3.2 Boring
- **Function:** Enlarge an **already drilled/cast hole** with high accuracy and alignment.
- **Tool:** Single-point boring bar (held in chuck or boring head) or a boring tool with indexable insert.
- **Application:** Used when the hole is too large to drill economically, or when high precision is required (e.g., engine cylinder bores).

### 2.3.3 Reaming
- **Function:** **Finish** a previously drilled/bored hole to:
  - Accurate final size (IT 7)
  - Fine surface finish (Ra 0.4 – 1.6 µm)
  - Geometric accuracy (roundness, cylindricity)
- **Tool:** **Reamer** (multi-fluted, typically 6–12 flutes for machine reamers; 4–8 flutes for hand reamers).
- **Stock Allowance:** Typically **0.2 – 0.4 mm** on diameter must be left for reaming (i.e., drill 0.2–0.4 mm undersize).

### 2.3.4 Tapping
- **Function:** Produce **internal threads** (e.g., M8, M10) inside a hole.
- **Tool:** **Tap** (a hardened threaded cutting tool with straight or helical flutes).
  - **Taper Tap** → for starting (first 7–10 threads).
  - **Plug Tap (Second Tap)** → for intermediate.
  - **Bottoming Tap (Third Tap)** → for blind/deep holes reaching full thread at the bottom.
- **Pre-requisite:** A **Tap Drill Hole** of the correct size must first be drilled. The tap then cuts threads by removing material to form the thread profile.
- **Tap Drill Diameter Formula:** **D_tap = D_major − P** (D_major = nominal/outer thread diameter; P = pitch of thread).

### 2.3.5 Counter Sinking
- **Function:** Cut a **conical (V-shaped) recess** at the top of a drilled hole to accommodate the conical underside of a flat-head screw.
- **Tool:** **Counter sink tool** with 60°, 82°, 90°, or 120° included angle (matches the screw head angle).
- **Common Standards:**
  - **82°** → Countersunk British Standard (BSW/BSP screws).
  - **90°** → Countersunk machine screws (metric).
  - **60°** → Taper pipe thread countersink.
  - **120°** → Cap screw / cheese-head recess.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Surface speed at drill periphery |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Drill rotational speed |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (V_f)** | $V_f = f_r \cdot N$ | mm/min | Linear feed per minute |
| 4 | **Feed per Revolution (f_r)** | Recommended from tables (0.05–0.4 mm/rev) | mm/rev | Set on feed mechanism |
| 5 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f_r \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Volume of metal removed per minute |
| 6 | **Drilling Time (t)** | $t = \dfrac{L}{f_r \cdot N}$ | min | Time per hole (L = hole depth + point allowance) |
| 7 | **Point Allowance (L_point)** | $L_{point} = \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \tan(90° - \phi)$ ≈ $\dfrac{D}{2 \tan(\phi/2)}$ | mm | Extra depth for drill point (≈ 0.3 D for 118° point) |
| 8 | **Thrust Force (F)** | Empirical: $F = K_f \cdot D \cdot f_r$ | N | K_f ≈ 1000–2500 N/mm² for steel |
| 9 | **Drilling Power (P)** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$ | kW | k_s = specific cutting energy (J/mm³) |
| 10 | **Tap Drill Diameter (D_tap)** | $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ | mm | For metric ISO threads |
| 11 | **Tap Drill (alt. form)** | $D_{tap} = D_{major} - (0.6495 \cdot P)$ | mm | Acme / unified (75% thread) |
| 12 | **Spindle Torque (T)** | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$ | N·m | Required torque on spindle |
| 13 | **RPM for Reaming (N_ream)** | $N_{ream} = (1/3$ to $1/2) \cdot N_{drill}$ | rpm | Reaming uses lower speed, higher feed |
| 14 | **Reaming Allowance (ΔD)** | $\Delta D = 0.2$ to $0.4$ mm (on diameter) | mm | Material left for reamer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Point Allowance (Drill Geometry):**
> For a **118° standard point**, $L_{point} \approx 0.3 \times D$. For a **135° point**, $L_{point} \approx 0.21 \times D$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drilling** is used in **every fabricated assembly** — from car engine blocks to aircraft wing ribs to PCB prototyping.
- **Boring** is critical in **IC engine manufacturing** (cylinder bore), **gearboxes** (bearing housings), and **hydraulic cylinder** manufacturing.
- **Reaming** is the final operation before **pin/bolt insertion**, ensuring **clearance fits (H7/h6)** in mechanical assemblies.
- **Tapping** is used wherever a **threaded fastener** must engage — **Jigs, fixtures, automotive engine heads, machine tool castings**.
- **Counter Sinking** is essential in **aerospace skins, automotive body panels, and furniture** where flush fasteners are required for aerodynamic and aesthetic reasons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cutting Speed (V)

**Statement:** Cutting speed is the **instantaneous linear velocity** at which the cutting edge of the drill moves relative to the workpiece surface, measured at the **periphery of the drill**.

**Step 1 — Relate linear distance per revolution to drill diameter.**
In one full revolution, the drill's outer cutting lip travels a circumferential distance equal to the circumference of a circle of diameter $D$.

$$
\text{Circumference} = \pi \cdot D \quad (\text{mm per revolution})
$$

**Step 2 — Relate distance per minute to spindle speed.**
If the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the total circumferential distance travelled per minute is:

$$
\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad (\text{mm/min})
$$

**Step 3 — Convert mm/min to m/min.**
Since $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$, we divide by 1000:

$$
V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}
$$

> This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for cutting speed. Every numerical in lab exams will start from this equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N)

Starting from the same equation, we isolate $N$:

**Step 1:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by 1000:

$$
1000 V = \pi D N
$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} \quad \text{(rpm)}
$$

This is the **spindle speed formula** used to select the correct machine step.

## 3.3 Derivation of Drilling Time (t)

The drilling time for a hole of depth $L$ (including point allowance) is:

**Step 1:** Feed per revolution = $f_r$ (mm/rev).

**Step 2:** Feed per minute = $f_r \cdot N$ (mm/min).

**Step 3:** Time to travel a distance $L$:

$$
t = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} = \frac{L}{f_r \cdot N} \quad \text{(min)}
$$

**Step 4 (Refined for real point):** The actual depth drilled is:

$$
L_{actual} = L_{hole} + L_{point}
$$

For a **118° point angle**, point length $L_{point} = \dfrac{D}{2 \tan(59°)} \approx 0.3 D$.

So:

$$
t = \frac{L_{hole} + 0.3 D}{f_r \cdot N}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Tap Drill Diameter

**Given:** A metric ISO thread of nominal diameter $D$ and pitch $P$.

**Step 1 — The thread profile (60° included angle, symmetric).**
The thread depth (from crest to root) for an ISO metric thread is:

$$
h = 0.6495 \cdot P
$$

This is **half the theoretical thread depth (0.5413 P) plus the standard truncation**. For practical purposes:

$$
h_{engagement} = 0.6134 \cdot P \quad \text{(depth of engagement on the bolt side)}
$$

**Step 2 — The tap drill must leave material to form the thread crest.**
To obtain approximately **75% thread engagement** (the standard for general-purpose tapping), the tap drill diameter is:

$$
D_{tap} = D_{major} - P
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of 75% thread engagement:**
For 75% thread, the remaining thread height on the nut is $0.75 h$:

$$
D_{tap} = D_{major} - 2(0.75 \cdot h) = D_{major} - 1.5 h = D_{major} - 1.5(0.6495 P)
$$

$$
D_{tap} = D_{major} - 0.974 P \approx D_{major} - P \quad \text{(rounded for 75% thread)}
$$

> This is the **practical tap drill rule** used in workshops. For 60% thread (softer materials), use $D_{tap} = D - 1.3 P$.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Question:** A **20 mm diameter hole** is to be drilled in a **mild steel plate** of thickness 30 mm. Take cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min and feed $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev. Find (a) spindle speed, (b) feed rate, (c) MRR, (d) drilling time (assume 118° point).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Spindle Speed N:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20}
$$

$$
N = \frac{25000}{62.832} = 397.9 \text{ rpm} \approx 398 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Part (b) — Feed Rate V_f:**

$$
V_f = f_r \cdot N = 0.2 \times 398 = 79.6 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Part (c) — Material Removal Rate MRR:**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f_r \cdot N
$$

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 0.2 \times 398 = 314.159 \times 79.6 = 25007 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Part (d) — Drilling Time t:**
Point allowance (118°): $L_{point} = 0.3 \times 20 = 6$ mm.

$$
t = \frac{L_{hole} + L_{point}}{f_r \cdot N} = \frac{30 + 6}{79.6} = \frac{36}{79.6} = 0.452 \text{ min} \approx 27.1 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Part (e) — Drilling Power (if k_s = 4 J/mm³ for mild steel):**

$$
P = \frac{MRR \cdot k_s}{60 \times 10^6} = \frac{25007 \times 4}{60 \times 10^6} = 0.001667 \text{ kW} \approx 1.67 \text{ W (raw chip energy)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real machines, **overall efficiency η ≈ 0.7–0.8**, so actual motor power required = P / η ≈ 2.1 W (for this small drill). Larger drills (say 50 mm in steel) would need **5–10 kW**.

## 3.6 Reaming Parameter Analysis (Analytical Matrix)

| Property | Drilling | Boring | Reaming | Tapping | Counter Sinking |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed V (m/min)** | 25–30 (steel) | 30–40 (steel) | 8–15 (steel) | 3–8 (steel) | 15–25 (steel) |
| **Feed f_r (mm/rev)** | 0.05–0.4 | 0.05–0.3 | 0.1–0.5 | Equal to pitch (P) | 0.05–0.2 |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | 100% (baseline) | ~80% of drill | 30–50% of drill | Very low (20–60 rpm) | 60% of drill |
| **Stock Allowance** | NA (initial) | 2–4 mm on D | 0.2–0.4 mm on D | Tap drill sized | 1–2 mm depth |
| **Surface Finish (Ra)** | 3.2–6.3 µm | 0.8–3.2 µm | 0.4–1.6 µm | 1.6–3.2 µm | 3.2–6.3 µm |
| **Tolerance (IT)** | IT 12–13 | IT 8–10 | IT 6–8 | 6H/6g | ±0.1 mm on depth |
| **Coolant** | Cutting oil | Cutting oil | Soluble oil / neat oil | Sulphurised oil / tapping fluid | Dry / light oil |
| **Operation Type** | Roughing | Semi-finish | Finishing | Threading | Auxiliary |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
