# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMRL408]**
**List and briefly explain any three operations performed on a drilling machine, with a typical tool used for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Drilling (1 mark):** Producing a cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece using a **multi-point cutting tool called a twist drill**, which rotates at the spindle and is fed axially downward. The drill cuts by shearing metal chips from the workpiece interior.

**(ii) Reaming (1 mark):** A **finishing operation** carried out on a previously drilled or bored hole to bring it to **accurate size, fine surface finish (Ra 0.4–1.6 µm), and improved roundness**. The tool used is a **reamer** (multi-fluted, typically 6–12 flutes). Stock allowance of 0.2–0.4 mm on diameter is left for reaming.

**(iii) Tapping (1 mark):** Cutting **internal threads** inside a drilled hole so that a bolt or screw can be fastened. The tool is a **tap**, which is essentially a hardened, threaded cutting tool with straight or helical flutes. A pre-drilled **tap-drill hole** of diameter $(D_{major} - P)$ is required.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMRL408]**
**State and explain the formula for spindle speed in drilling. Why is reaming speed kept lower than drilling speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1.5 marks):** The spindle speed (N) in rpm is given by

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

where $V$ is the cutting speed in m/min and $D$ is the drill/reamer diameter in mm.

**Explanation (1.5 marks):** Reaming speed is kept at **1/3 to 1/2 of drilling speed** because:
- A reamer has **multiple cutting edges (6–12 flutes)**, so the total cutting action per revolution is much higher.
- The **chip thickness is very small** (only 0.2–0.4 mm stock on diameter is being removed) — high speed would cause rapid tool wear and poor finish.
- Lower speed gives **better surface finish** and longer reamer life.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PCMRL408]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and main parts of a bench-type drilling machine. State the function of the depth stop.

**(b) [7 marks]** An M16 metric coarse thread hole is to be tapped in a 25 mm thick mild steel plate. Determine: (i) Tap drill diameter, (ii) Spindle speed if V = 6 m/min, (iii) Feed per revolution, (iv) Drilling time for the tap drill hole. Take feed $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev for the tap drill.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Bench Drilling Machine Construction

**Construction (5 marks):**

The bench drilling machine consists of the following main parts:

1. **Base** (0.5 mark): Heavy cast-iron base providing stability and rigidity. It has slots for bolting the machine to the workbench.

2. **Column** (0.5 mark): Vertical pillar rising from the base; provides the structural backbone of the machine.

3. **Head** (0.5 mark): Houses the motor, belt-drive system, gear box, and spindle assembly at the top.

4. **Spindle** (0.5 mark): Vertical rotating shaft that holds the chuck; driven by motor via V-belt and gears.

5. **Chuck** (0.5 mark): Three-jaw chuck mounted on the spindle taper, grips the drill bit shank.

6. **Feed Handle / Quill Lever** (0.5 mark): Three-spoke handle that rotates to advance the quill (and hence the spindle + drill) downward manually.

7. **Worktable** (0.5 mark): Adjustable table with T-slots for clamping workpieces. It can be raised/lowered and rotated.

8. **Depth Stop** (0.5 mark): Adjustable collar/ring on the spindle quill that limits the downward travel of the drill, ensuring uniform hole depth across multiple operations.

**Function of Depth Stop (2 marks):**
The depth stop **prevents the drill from over-penetrating the workpiece** and ensures **consistent depth** when producing a batch of identical holes. It is set by loosening a locking screw, sliding the collar to the desired position (typically using a ruler or vernier), and re-tightening. When the collar hits a fixed stop on the head, the spindle can descend no further.

#### Part (b) — Tapping Calculations

**Given:** M16 × 2.0 (P = 2 mm), mild steel, V = 6 m/min, $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev, plate thickness = 25 mm.

**(i) Tap Drill Diameter (2 marks):**

$$
D_{tap} = D_{major} - P = 16 - 2.0 = 14 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Spindle Speed N for the tap drill (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_{tap}} = \frac{1000 \times 6}{\pi \times 14} = \frac{6000}{43.98} = 136.4 \text{ rpm} \approx 136 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(iii) Feed per Revolution (1 mark):** Given as $f_r = 0.2$ mm/rev.

**(iv) Drilling Time (2 marks):**
Point allowance (118°) = $0.3 \times D = 0.3 \times 14 = 4.2$ mm.

$$
t = \frac{L + L_{point}}{f_r \cdot N} = \frac{25 + 4.2}{0.2 \times 136} = \frac{29.2}{27.2} = 1.07 \text{ min} \approx 64.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, PCMRL408]**

**(a) [7 marks]** What is reaming? Differentiate between hand reaming and machine reaming. List any four specifications of a reamer.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 12 mm diameter hole is to be drilled in a cast iron plate of 20 mm thickness. Calculate the spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, and drilling time. Take $V = 20$ m/min and $f_r = 0.15$ mm/rev. Assume 118° point angle.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Reaming Concepts

**Definition (2 marks):**
Reaming is a **finishing operation** performed on a previously drilled, bored, or punched hole to:
- Achieve **accurate final diameter** (IT 6 – IT 8).
- Improve **surface finish** (Ra 0.4 – 1.6 µm).
- Improve **roundness and cylindricity**.

The tool is a **reamer** with 4–12 straight or helical flutes, and the operation leaves a **stock allowance of 0.2–0.4 mm on the diameter**.

**Differences — Hand Reaming vs. Machine Reaming (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Hand Reaming | Machine Reaming |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving** | Manually rotated using a **tap wrench / T-handle** | Rotated by **machine spindle** |
| **Speed** | Very low (~ 5–10 rpm) | Higher (8–15 m/min) |
| **Feed** | Manual, hand-driven | Automatic (0.1–0.5 mm/rev) |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (operator can "feel" the cut) | Slightly lower (machine rigid) |
| **Application** | Field work, prototypes, repair | Production batches |
| **Flute Count** | 4–8 (fewer flutes) | 6–12 (more flutes for finish) |
| **Tool Life** | Longer (gentle cuts) | Shorter (continuous duty) |

**Four Specifications of a Reamer (1 mark):**
1. **Diameter (nominal)** – e.g., Ø12 H7.
2. **Number of flutes** – 6, 8, 10, 12.
3. **Overall length & flute length**.
4. **Type** – straight shank or Morse taper shank; left-hand or right-hand cut.
5. **Tolerance grade** – H7, H8, etc.

#### Part (b) — Drilling Calculations for Cast Iron

**Given:** $D = 12$ mm, $L = 20$ mm, $V = 20$ m/min, $f_r = 0.15$ mm/rev, 118° point.

**(i) Spindle Speed N (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 12} = \frac{20000}{37.699} = 530.5 \text{ rpm} \approx 530 \text{ rpm}
$$

**(ii) Feed Rate V_f (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_f = f_r \cdot N = 0.15 \times 530.5 = 79.6 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**(iii) MRR (1.5 marks):**

$$
MRR = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f_r \cdot N = \frac{\pi \times 12^2}{4} \times 0.15 \times 530.5
$$

$$
MRR = 113.097 \times 79.6 = 9002.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 9.0 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**(iv) Drilling Time t (2 marks):**
Point allowance = $0.3 \times 12 = 3.6$ mm.

$$
t = \frac{L + L_{point}}{f_r \cdot N} = \frac{20 + 3.6}{79.6} = \frac{23.6}{79.6} = 0.296 \text{ min} \approx 17.8 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Point Allowance** — examiners frequently deduct 1 full mark if you don't add $0.3D$ for the drill point depth. ALWAYS include $L_{point}$ in the time formula.
> 2. **Unit confusion in $V$** — use m/min for $V$ in spindle speed formula, but mm/min for $V_f$ in feed rate formula. Do NOT mix them.
> 3. **Tap drill for tapping** — students often write $D_{tap} = D - 2P$ (wrong — that gives 50% thread). The correct practical formula is $D_{tap} = D - P$ (75% thread).
> 4. **Reaming speed vs drilling speed** — many students set reamer speed equal to drill speed. This is WRONG. Use $N_{ream} = (1/3 \text{ to } 1/2) N_{drill}$.
> 5. **Counter Sinking ≠ Counter Boring** — countersink is **conical** (angled); counterbore is **cylindrical** (flat-bottomed). Examiners will mark you down if you confuse them.
> 6. **Always mention the point angle** — 118° for steel, 135° for stainless/hardened, 90° for soft non-ferrous. Vivas always ask this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Drilling** = primary hole-making; tool = **twist drill**; standard point = **118°**; feed = **0.05–0.4 mm/rev**.
- 🔹 **Boring** = enlarging an existing hole; tool = **single-point boring bar**; better accuracy than drilling.
- 🔹 **Reaming** = finishing operation; tool = **reamer (6–12 flutes)**; stock allowance = **0.2–0.4 mm** on diameter; speed = **30–50% of drill speed**.
- 🔹 **Tapping** = internal thread cutting; tool = **tap** (taper, plug, bottoming); tap drill = **D − P**; tapping speed = **3–8 m/min** (very low).
- 🔹 **Counter Sinking** = conical recess for flat-head screws; common angles = **82°, 90°, 120°**; tool = countersink (no drills!).
- 🔹 **Cutting Speed Formula** → $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$.
- 🔹 **Spindle Speed Formula** → $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$.
- 🔹 **Feed Rate Formula** → $V_f = f_r \cdot N$.
- 🔹 **MRR Formula** → $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f_r \cdot N$.
- 🔹 **Drilling Time** → $t = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f_r N}$.
- 🔹 **Tap Drill** → $D_{tap} = D_{major} - P$ (75% thread).
- 🔹 **Coolant rule** → **cutting oil for steel; soluble oil for Al; dry for cast iron; sulphurised fluid for tapping**.
- 🔹 **Hand vs Machine reamer** → hand = slow, hand-driven, T-handle; machine = higher rpm, automatic feed.
- 🔹 **Workpiece clamping** → use **step blocks, clamps, and V-blocks** to keep workpiece flat and avoid drill deflection.
- 🔹 **Safety** → always **wear safety goggles, no loose clothing, secure long hair, NEVER hold workpiece by hand** (use a vice on the table).
- 🔹 **Always peck drill** (withdraw drill periodically) for **deep holes > 3D** to clear chips and prevent breakage.
- 🔹 **Viva favourite questions**: "What is the standard drill point angle?", "What is a tap drill?", "Why is reaming speed lower?", "What is the function of flutes?"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Shaping Machine/Slotting Machine: Flat Surfaces, Grooves, and Key Ways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Shaping Machine/Slotting Machine: Flat Surfaces, Grooves, and Key Ways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔧 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaping Machine:** A *reciprocating-type* machine tool in which a single-point cutting tool is held in a tool holder mounted on a **ram** that reciprocates linearly over the workpiece, generating flat surfaces, grooves, and external keyways by incremental material removal during the **forward (cutting) stroke**, while the workpiece is fed laterally during the **return stroke**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slotting Machine:** A *vertical-reciprocating-type* machine tool where the ram carrying the tool moves vertically (downward cutting stroke, upward return stroke). It is specifically employed for machining **internal surfaces**, such as internal keyways in gears/pulleys, internal slots, and square/rectangular holes that cannot be easily accessed by horizontal shaping.

> [!NOTE]
> Both machines use **quick-return mechanisms** (Whitworth, crank-and-slot, or hydraulic) so the return stroke is faster than the cutting stroke, reducing idle time and improving productivity.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are slicing bread with a long serrated knife, but instead of moving the knife over the loaf, **the loaf moves back-and-forth under your stationary hand** (shaping), or your hand moves **up-and-down over the stationary loaf** (slotting). The "knife" removes a thin ribbon of material on each pass, and the "loaf" advances sideways between cuts.

| Machine | Analogy Movement | What is Machined |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| **Shaping** | Loaf slides horizontally; knife held still | Flat sides, external keyways, V-grooves on the outside of a part |
| **Slotting** | Loaf is stationary; knife chops vertically | Internal keyways inside a bore, internal grooves, square holes |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** "**S**haping cuts **S**ideways, **S**lotting cuts **S**quare holes from above."

---

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (in **Bold**)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Cutting Speed** | **V** (m/min) | **15 – 30 m/min** for mild steel (HSS tool) |
| **Feed per stroke** | **f** (mm/stroke) | **0.2 – 0.5 mm/stroke** |
| **Depth of cut** | **d** (mm) | **1 – 4 mm** per pass |
| **Stroke length** | **L** (mm) | **(L = Work length + Tool overtravel)** |
| **Quick return ratio** | **R** | **2 : 1** typically (return is twice as fast) |
| **Ram speed (cutting)** | **N<sub>c</sub>** (strokes/min) | Computed from crank RPM |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Shaping Machine

1. **Base** – Heavy cast-iron bed supporting the column; absorbs vibration.
2. **Column** – Vertical wall housing the drive mechanism and ram guideways.
3. **Cross-slide / Saddle** – Carries the table and provides *cross-feed* (perpendicular to ram motion).
4. **Table** – Reciprocating worktable carrying the workpiece; movement is the *cutting feed* along ram axis.
5. **Ram** – Slides on guideways; holds the tool head; performs the reciprocating motion.
6. **Tool Head / Swivel Block** – Holds single-point tool; can be swivelled to cut angular surfaces (e.g. dovetails).
7. **Feed Mechanism** – Provides automatic cross-feed at end of return stroke using ratchet-worm gear.

---

## 2.2 Shaping vs. Slotting – Functional Comparison

| Parameter | Shaping Machine | Slotting Machine |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Ram motion** | Horizontal, reciprocating | Vertical (downward cut), reciprocating |
| **Stroke orientation** | Push-pull along X-axis | Push-down along Z-axis |
| **Primary feed** | Cross-slide & table (X, Y) | Rotary table + cross-slide |
| **Workpiece size** | Small to medium, clamped on table | Large/heavy work; held on fixed bed |
| **Typical operation** | Flat surface, external keyway, V-groove | Internal keyway, square hole, internal slot |
| **Tool approach** | Tool approaches from side | Tool plunges from above |
| **Drive mechanism** | Crank-and-slot or hydraulic | Crank-and-slot or Whitworth quick-return |

---

## 2.3 Quick-Return Mechanism – Working Logic

> **Why is the return stroke faster?**
> Because the cutting stroke covers 180°–200° of crank rotation while the return stroke covers only 160°–180°. With constant crank RPM, the longer arc → slower linear velocity (cutting), shorter arc → faster (return).

**Quick Return Ratio (QRR):**

$$QRR = \frac{\text{Time for cutting stroke}}{\text{Time for return stroke}} = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta_r}$$

Typical **QRR ≈ 2 : 1** (cutting takes twice as long as return).

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorisation)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed from crank RPM (D = crank diameter) | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Crank RPM for desired cutting speed | rev/min |
| 3 | $f_c = L \cdot N_{stroke}$ | Feed rate per minute (L = stroke length) | mm/min |
| 4 | $T_m = \dfrac{L + l_o}{f \cdot N_{stroke}} \cdot \dfrac{W}{1}$ | Machining time for shaping (W = width, l<sub>o</sub> = overtravel) | min |
| 5 | $L = l_w + (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5)l_o$ | Total stroke length | mm |
| 6 | $\theta_c = 180° + \alpha$ | Cutting stroke crank angle | degrees |
| 7 | $\theta_r = 180° - \alpha$ | Return stroke crank angle | degrees |
| 8 | $V_{cutting} = \dfrac{L \cdot N_{stroke}}{1000}$ | Average cutting velocity | m/min |
| 9 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | Power required at spindle | kW |
| 10 | $MRR = W \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N_{stroke}$ | Material removal rate (shaping approximation) | mm³/min |

> [!NOTE]
> In Table row formulas, the *absolute value* symbol has been replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table-breaking — see row 4 example: $\vert W \vert$ is shown as just *W* numerically. **Use $\vert x \vert$ only in display equations, never inside table pipes.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Maintenance workshops:** Shapers are used to repair worn guide-ways, machine small replacement parts, and produce keyways on shafts during field repairs.
- **Tool rooms:** Slotting machines machine internal keyways in batch-produced gears/pulleys where broaches are unavailable.
- **Foundry & forging industries:** Trimming flat surfaces on cast/forge blanks.
- **Education & training labs:** The PCMRL408 lab course trains students on these versatile, low-cost machines before CNC exposure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cutting Speed from Crank Motion

The ram in a crank-and-slot quick-return mechanism traces approximately simple harmonic motion. The *average* velocity during the cutting stroke equals:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{L}{t_c}$$

where $L$ is the stroke length (mm) and $t_c$ is the cutting stroke time (min). Since the crank rotates $N$ rev/min, each revolution produces one cutting stroke and one return stroke. The cutting stroke occupies a fraction of the revolution:

$$t_c = \frac{\theta_c}{360° \cdot N} \quad \text{[min]}$$

Substituting:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{L \cdot 360° \cdot N}{\theta_c}$$

Converting to m/min:

$$V = \frac{L \cdot N}{1000} \cdot \frac{360°}{\theta_c}$$

For a typical **crank-and-slot mechanism** with $\theta_c = 180° + \alpha$ and $\theta_r = 180° - \alpha$, if $\alpha = 30°$, then $\theta_c = 210°$, $\theta_r = 150°$. The simplified *practical* formula used by KTU examiners is:

$$\boxed{V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}}$$

This treats the crank pin as moving on a circle of diameter $D$ with angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/sec, so the tangential speed $= \omega R = \omega \cdot D/2$, and the linear speed $= \pi D N / 60$ mm/sec = $\pi D N / 1000$ m/min.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Shaping a Flat Surface

> **Problem:** A mild-steel workpiece of length **240 mm** and width **80 mm** is to be shaped on a shaping machine. Tool overtravel = **25 mm** per side. Cutting speed **V = 18 m/min**, crank diameter **D = 120 mm**, feed **f = 0.4 mm/stroke**, depth of cut **d = 2 mm**. Estimate (a) Crank RPM, (b) Stroke length, (c) Time per pass, (d) MRR.

### Step (a): Crank RPM

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 18}{\pi \times 120}$$

$$N = \frac{18000}{376.99} \approx 47.74 \;\; \text{rev/min}$$

Practical selection: **N = 48 rpm** (use nearest standard value).

### Step (b): Stroke Length

$$L = l_w + 1.3 \cdot l_o = 240 + 1.3 \times 25$$

$$L = 240 + 32.5 = 272.5 \;\; \text{mm} \;\; \approx \textbf{275 mm}$$

### Step (c): Time per Pass

Number of strokes = Width / feed = $\dfrac{80}{0.4}$ = **200 strokes**.

Time per stroke = $\dfrac{1}{N}$ = $\dfrac{1}{48}$ min/stroke = 1.25 sec/stroke.

Total time per pass:

$$T_m = \frac{200}{48} = 4.167 \;\; \text{min} \;\; \approx \textbf{4 min 10 sec}$$

### Step (d): Material Removal Rate

Per stroke, volume removed = $L \cdot f \cdot d = 275 \times 0.4 \times 2 = 220 \text{ mm}^3$.

Per minute:

$$MRR = 220 \times 48 = 10560 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; \approx \textbf{10.56 cm}^3/\text{min}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)

By geometry of crank-and-slot mechanism, the slotted lever rotates about its pivot by angle $2\beta$ (where $\sin\beta = r/L_{slot}$). The crank angle covered during cutting stroke:

$$\theta_c = 180° + 2\beta$$

The crank angle during return stroke:

$$\theta_r = 180° - 2\beta$$

Therefore:

$$QRR = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta_r} = \frac{180° + 2\beta}{180° - 2\beta}$$

**Example:** If $r = 60$ mm and $L_{slot} = 300$ mm, then $\beta = \sin^{-1}(60/300) = 11.54°$.

$$QRR = \frac{180° + 23.08°}{180° - 23.08°} = \frac{203.08}{156.92} \approx 1.294$$

So cutting stroke takes **1.294 times longer** than return stroke.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Internal Keyway on Slotting Machine

> **Problem:** Cut an internal keyway of length **50 mm**, width **10 mm**, depth **5 mm** inside a bore of $\phi$**80 mm**. Cutting speed **V = 12 m/min**, crank diameter **D = 100 mm**, feed **f = 0.3 mm/stroke**. Estimate time for the operation.

### Step 1: Crank RPM

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 12}{\pi \times 100} = 38.20 \;\; \text{rpm} \;\; \approx \textbf{38 rpm}$$

### Step 2: Stroke length

Keyway length + tool overtravel:
$$L = 50 + (1.2 \times 15) = 50 + 18 = 68 \;\; \text{mm}$$

### Step 3: Number of strokes for width 10 mm

Number of strokes = $10 / 0.3 = 33.33 \Rightarrow$ **34 strokes** (round up).

### Step 4: Total time

$$T_m = \frac{34}{38} = 0.894 \;\; \text{min} \;\; \approx \textbf{54 seconds}$$

This is for a single pass at depth 5 mm. If depth achieved in two passes (say 2.5 mm each), total time **≈ 108 sec**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Operation on Shaping vs. Slotting

| Operation | Preferred Machine | Reason | Typical Tool Geometry |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|------------------------|
| **Flat horizontal surface** | Shaping | Horizontal stroke matches natural feed direction | Straight HSS tool, 0° rake |
| **Vertical surface** | Shaping (with vertical feed) | Cross-slide feed in Y-axis | Side-facing tool |
| **V-groove (90°)** | Shaping with angled tool | Tool swivel block allows angle setting | 45° side-cutting tool |
| **Dovetail slide** | Shaping with template/angle plate | Tool angle = dovetail angle | Form tool, ground to angle |
| **T-slot** | Shaping (after initial milling/casting) | Successive horizontal + vertical passes | T-slot cutter / side tool |
| **External keyway (on shaft)** | Shaping | Open access, side approach | Keyway cutter (disk-like) |
| **Internal keyway (in bore)** | **Slotting** | Vertical ram reaches inside bore | Slotting tool, narrow shank |
| **Square/hexagonal hole** | **Slotting** | Internal geometry requires vertical plunge | Square-nose slotter |
| **Curved contour** | Shaping (with circular attachment) | Trammel/circular attachment available | Single-point tool on radius |

---

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a shaping machine and a slotting machine with respect to (i) ram direction, (ii) typical operations, and (iii) type of work holding.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Shaping Machine | Slotting Machine |
|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| (i) **Ram direction** | Horizontal reciprocation | Vertical reciprocation |
| (ii) **Typical operations** | Flat surfaces, V-grooves, external keyways | Internal keyways, square holes, internal slots |
| (iii) **Work holding** | Vice or bolts on reciprocating table | Heavy fixture on rotary/fixed table |

The shaping machine's table moves the workpiece past a stationary tool, whereas in a slotter the workpiece stays still and the ram-driven tool plunges into it. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the function of a quick-return mechanism in a shaping machine? Define quick-return ratio.**

**Model Answer:**
The quick-return mechanism makes the **return (idle) stroke faster** than the cutting stroke, reducing non-productive time and improving productivity. For a crank-and-slot mechanism, this is achieved by designing the crank angles asymmetrically.

**Quick-Return Ratio (QRR):**

$$QRR = \frac{\text{Time of cutting stroke}}{\text{Time of return stroke}} = \frac{\theta_c}{\theta_r}$$

Typically **QRR ≈ 2 : 1**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Shaping a V-Groove and Flat Surface

> **Q.A.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Question II.A]
>
> A mild-steel workpiece of **length 300 mm** and **width 100 mm** is to be machined on a shaping machine to produce a flat surface and then a **90° V-groove** of depth **15 mm** along its length.
>
> **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for setting up and machining the V-groove using the swivel tool head. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the cutting speed is **20 m/min**, crank diameter **100 mm**, feed **0.5 mm/stroke**, and overtravel **20 mm per side**, calculate (i) crank RPM, (ii) stroke length, (iii) time for flat surface machining, and (iv) MRR. (7 marks)

#### **Part (a) Model Solution – V-Groove Machining Procedure:**

1. **Work Holding:** Clamp the workpiece on the shaping machine table using a precision vice, ensuring the marked centerline of the groove is parallel to the ram's line of motion.
2. **Tool Selection:** Use a **45° side-cutting HSS tool** (one side ground at 45° for each flank).
3. **Swivel Block Setting:** Rotate the tool head (swivel block) by **45°** on either side of vertical.
   - First pass: swivel tool **45° to the right** → cut one flank.
   - Second pass: swivel tool **45° to the left** → cut the other flank.
   - The intersection forms the **90° V-groove**.
4. **Depth Control:** Use the depth-adjustment screw on the down-feed of the tool head; take light cuts (0.5–1 mm) progressing to full 15 mm.
5. **Coolant:** Apply cutting fluid continuously to avoid built-up edge and tool wear.
6. **Verification:** Use a **V-block + dial indicator** or **profile projector** to check the 90° angle.

**Sketch (Functional Block Representation):**
```
   Tool →  /         ← Right flank tool, 45° swivel
   Tool →  \         ← Left flank tool, 45° swivel
   Intersection →  V apex (90° groove)
```

**(7 marks allocated: 2 procedure + 2 tool selection + 2 swivel setting + 1 verification)**

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution – Calculations:**

**(i) Crank RPM:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{20000}{314.16} \approx \textbf{63.66 rpm} \;\; (\text{use } 64 \text{ rpm})$$

**(ii) Stroke length:**

$$L = 300 + 1.25 \times 20 = 300 + 25 = \textbf{325 mm}$$

**(iii) Time for flat surface (one pass):**

Number of strokes = $\dfrac{100}{0.5} = 200$ strokes.

Time per stroke = $\dfrac{1}{64}$ min = 0.9375 sec.

$$T_m = \frac{200}{64} = 3.125 \;\; \text{min} \;\; \approx \textbf{3 min 7.5 sec}$$

**(iv) Material Removal Rate:**

Per stroke volume $= L \cdot f \cdot d = 325 \times 0.5 \times 2 = 325 \text{ mm}^3$.

$$MRR = 325 \times 64 = 20800 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; = \textbf{20.8 cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(7 marks allocated: 2 + 1 + 2 + 2)**

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Slotting Machine for Internal Keyway

> **Q.B.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Question II.B]
>
> An internal keyway of **length 60 mm, width 12 mm, depth 6 mm** is to be cut in a **gear bore of $\phi$100 mm** using a slotting machine.
>
> **(a)** Describe with a sketch the setup, tool, and procedure for cutting the internal keyway. Why is a slotting machine preferred over shaping for this operation? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Calculate (i) crank RPM, (ii) stroke length, (iii) number of strokes, and (iv) total machining time. Given: cutting speed **15 m/min**, crank diameter **80 mm**, feed **0.3 mm/stroke**, overtravel **15 mm per side**, cutting depth achieved in **3 passes**. (7 marks)

#### **Part (a) Model Solution – Internal Keyway Setup:**

1. **Work Holding:** Mount the gear on the **rotary table** of the slotting machine using a mandrel or three-jaw chuck; align the keyway starting position with the ram's vertical axis.
2. **Tool Selection:** Use a **narrow-shank slotting tool** (width = 12 mm) with proper clearance angle. Ensure the tool shank fits inside the bore.
3. **Stroke Adjustment:** Set ram stroke length so that the tool enters the bore, cuts the full keyway length (60 mm), and exits during overtravel.
4. **Depth Control:** Use the **down-feed screw** of the tool head to advance into the bore progressively (e.g. 2 mm per pass × 3 passes = 6 mm depth).
5. **Why Slotting Preferred Over Shaping?**
   - The **vertical ram** can plunge into the bore directly.
   - In shaping, the horizontal stroke cannot access an internal feature inside a closed bore.
   - Slotter allows the workpiece to remain stationary and heavy.
6. **Verification:** Use a **key gauge** or **bore gauge with keyway attachment**.

**(7 marks allocated: 2 setup + 2 tool + 2 preference + 1 verification)**

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution – Calculations:**

**(i) Crank RPM:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 15}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{15000}{251.33} \approx \textbf{59.68 rpm} \;\; (\text{use } 60 \text{ rpm})$$

**(ii) Stroke length:**

$$L = 60 + 1.3 \times 15 = 60 + 19.5 = \textbf{79.5 mm} \;\; (\text{use } 80 \text{ mm})$$

**(iii) Number of strokes:**

For width 12 mm at feed 0.3 mm/stroke:
$$N_{stroke} = \frac{12}{0.3} = 40 \text{ strokes per pass}$$

For 3 passes: $40 \times 3 = \textbf{120 strokes}$ total.

**(iv) Total machining time:**

$$T_m = \frac{120}{60} = \textbf{2 minutes}$$

**(7 marks allocated: 2 + 1 + 2 + 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting overtravel in stroke length** → loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing cutting speed formula** V = πDN/1000 with V = πDN/60 (the latter is in mm/sec).
> 3. **Not specifying QRR (Quick Return Ratio)** when asked about mechanism — examiners expect the formula and typical value.
> 4. **Swapping operations:** stating "shaper for internal keyway" or "slotter for external flat surface" → **2-mark penalty + concept zero.**
> 5. **Forgetting the depth multiplier** in MRR when multiple passes are taken.
> 6. **Calculation errors:** always show units and substitution step — partial marks awarded for correct setup even if arithmetic slips.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Shaper** = horizontal reciprocating ram → **external** flat surfaces, grooves, keyways.
- ✅ **Slotter** = vertical reciprocating ram → **internal** keyways, slots, square holes.
- ✅ **Quick-Return Ratio** typically **2 : 1** (cutting : return).
- ✅ **Stroke length** L = Work length + overtravel (1.2–1.5 × tool overtravel).
- ✅ **Cutting speed formula:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min (D in mm, N in rpm).
- ✅ **MRR (shaping):** Volume per stroke × strokes/min = L × f × d × N.
- ✅ **Feed per stroke** in shaping/slotting is given by **cross-feed / number of strokes**, typically 0.2–0.5 mm for finishing, 0.5–1.0 mm for roughing.
- ✅ **V-grooves** require **swivel tool head** set at half the included angle on each side.
- ✅ **Dovetails** use angled tool + template; **T-slots** use successive horizontal then vertical passes.
- ✅ **Tool material:** HSS for low/medium speed; carbide tipped for higher productivity.
- ✅ **Coolant** is essential to prevent built-up edge and improve surface finish.
- ✅ **Safety:** Always retract tool, stop machine before measurement; never touch reciprocating parts.

---

topics.title: Exercises on Grinding Machine: Surface Grinding, Cylindrical Grinding, Tool Grinding, and Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Grinding Machine: Surface Grinding, Cylindrical Grinding, Tool Grinding, and Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Grinding** is a material removal process that uses an **abrasive wheel** rotating at very high peripheral speed as the cutting tool. Each abrasive grain on the wheel periphery acts as a microscopic single-point cutting tool, removing chips of the order of a few micrometres. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCMRL408 (Mechanical Engineering Lab)**, grinding exercises cover four cardinal operations:

1. **Surface Grinding** – producing flat surfaces on a reciprocating or rotary table.
2. **Cylindrical Grinding** – producing external/internal cylindrical or conical surfaces.
3. **Tool Grinding** – re-sharpening of single-point tools, drills and milling cutters.
4. **Inspection** – verification of dimensional accuracy, geometric tolerances, and **surface roughness** using metrological instruments such as the **Dial Test Indicator (DTI)**, **sine bar**, **Toolmaker's microscope**, and **Surface Roughness Tester (Talysurf)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRL408 – Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *identify grinding machine parts, select grinding wheels using the standard marking system (e.g., AA-36-K-5-V), carry out dressing and truing, perform surface/cylindrical/tool grinding, and conduct inspection for dimensional and surface integrity.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine sanding a wooden plank by hand. You press a sandpaper strip against the wood and slide it back and forth. Now imagine a sandpaper strip shaped into a spinning disc running at **30 m/s** with **hundreds of tiny, razor-sharp teeth** (abrasive grains) glued to its edge. That's a grinding wheel. The plank is the **workpiece**, and the spinning disc is the **wheel**.

A second analogy: shaving with a razor is a macro-version of grinding. The blade edge is hardened, very thin, and removes a hair-thickness slice per stroke. A grinding wheel's grit does exactly the same, but with thousands of microscopic edges operating in parallel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuitive Facts to Remember**
> - The grinding wheel is **not sharp** as a whole; only the **individual grits** are sharp.
> - Most of the grinding energy (> 80 %) is converted into **heat**, which is why **coolant supply** is non-negotiable.
> - The hardness of the wheel is denoted by a **letter grade (A–Z)** — *softer letters = harder wheel* (counterintuitive!).

### 1.3 Standardised Grinding Wheel Marking (KTU Emphasis)

A grinding wheel specification is read as:
**AA – 36 – K – 5 – V**

| Position | Symbol | Meaning | Example Value |
|:--------:|:------:|:-------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | AA | Abrasive Type | AA = Aluminium Oxide, C = Silicon Carbide, D = Cubic Boron Nitride |
| 2 | 36 | Grit Size (mesh per inch) | 36 = Coarse, 60 = Medium, 120 = Fine |
| 3 | K | Hardness Grade | A = Soft, Z = Hard |
| 4 | 5 | Structure (spacing) | 1 = Dense, 8 = Open |
| 5 | V | Bond Type | V = Vitrified, B = Resinoid, R = Rubber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Standard Metric (KTU Reference):** Hardness scale letters range from **A (softest)** to **Z (hardest)**. The wheel selection rule — *Use a soft wheel for hard workpiece, hard wheel for soft workpiece* — is a guaranteed 3-mark question in viva-voce.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Operational Parameters of Grinding (The "Big Five")

Every grinding operation, irrespective of machine type, is governed by five inter-dependent parameters. Mastering these unlocks every KTU numerical.

**Step 1 — Wheel Peripheral Speed (V_c)**
The linear velocity at which the abrasive grit strikes the workpiece. If wheel diameter = D and rotational speed = N:

$$V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

where V_c is in m/s, D in metres, N in rev/s.

**Step 2 — Workpiece Speed (V_w)**
Surface speed of the workpiece relative to the wheel. For cylindrical grinding:

$$V_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot n_w$$

For surface grinding with reciprocating table, the equivalent V_w is the **table feed per minute**.

**Step 3 — Depth of Cut (a or d)**
Infeed per pass; typically 0.005 mm to 0.05 mm in finish grinding.

**Step 4 — Feed (f)**
- Surface grinding: cross-feed per table stroke (mm/stroke).
- Cylindrical grinding: feed per revolution of workpiece (mm/rev).

**Step 5 — Spark-Out**
Number of traverses after feed is stopped, to allow the wheel to "clean up" the surface.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter

- **Why is V_c so high (≈ 30–35 m/s for conventional abrasives)?** Higher speed means more grits engage per second → finer surface finish and higher MRR per grit (each grit removes less, but thousands cut simultaneously).
- **Why use coolant?** Heat removal, lubrication, chip flushing, and prevention of **thermal damage (re-hardening burn, residual tensile stress)**.
- **Why dress the wheel?** Dressing exposes fresh, sharp grits and restores the wheel geometry; truing restores the concentric shape.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application |
|:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----:|:-----------:|
| 1 | Wheel speed (m/s) | $V_c = \pi D N$ | m/s | All grinders |
| 2 | Workpiece speed (m/s) | $V_w = \pi d n_w$ | m/s | Cylindrical grinding |
| 3 | Equivalent chip thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{V_w}{V_c}\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{d_e}}$ | mm | Specific energy |
| 4 | MRR – Surface grinding | $Q = V_w \cdot a \cdot b$ | mm³/s | Plunge/Reciprocating |
| 5 | MRR – Cylindrical grinding | $Q = V_w \cdot a_p \cdot f$ | mm³/s | External grinding |
| 6 | Specific grinding energy | $e = \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | J/mm³ | Power estimation |
| 7 | Grinding power | $P = F_c \cdot V_c$ | W | Tangential force |
| 8 | Surface roughness (Ra) – grinding | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{32 r}$ | µm | Theoretical finish |
| 9 | Number of active grits | $N_a = C \cdot b \cdot l$ | — | Wheel contact area |
| 10 | Equivalent diameter (cylindrical) | $d_e = \dfrac{d \cdot D}{d + D}$ | mm | Cylindrical geometry |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used Above:**
> - $D$ = wheel diameter, $d$ = workpiece diameter, $N$ = wheel rpm.
> - $a$ = depth of cut (mm), $b$ = width of grinding (mm), $f$ = feed per rev (mm/rev).
> - $F_c$ = tangential cutting force (N), $e$ = specific energy (J/mm³).
> - $r$ = nose radius of grit (µm); $C$ = active grit density (grits/mm²).

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **MRR and specific energy** allow process planning: a designer can predict cycle time and spindle motor sizing before the first chip is cut.
- **Ra formula** provides a theoretical lower bound for finish achievable — useful in **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing)** call-outs on engineering drawings.
- **Equivalent chip thickness** is the single parameter that correlates grinding performance to turning/milling, enabling the **unified machinability charts** used in CAM software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Surface Grinding

We define MRR as the volume of material removed per unit time. For a horizontal-spindle reciprocating surface grinder:

**Given:**
- Table traverse speed: $V_w$ (mm/min or mm/s)
- Cross-feed per stroke: $f_c$ (mm/stroke)
- Wheel width engaged: $b$ (mm)
- Down-feed (depth of cut): $a$ (mm/pass)

**Logic Step 1:** In one complete table stroke, the wheel traverses a length equal to the workpiece length, $L$, while removing material across width $b$ to depth $a$.

**Logic Step 2:** Volume removed in one stroke = $L \cdot b \cdot a$ (mm³).

**Logic Step 3:** Time per stroke = $L / V_w$.

**Logic Step 4:** Therefore, instantaneous MRR when wheel is engaged:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{\text{Volume per stroke}}{\text{Time per stroke}} \\
  &= \frac{L \cdot b \cdot a}{L \,/\, V_w} \\
  &= V_w \cdot b \cdot a
\end{aligned}$$

**Result (boxed formula):** $Q = V_w \cdot b \cdot a$ mm³/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same derivation logic for **cylindrical grinding** yields $Q = \pi \cdot d \cdot n_w \cdot a_p \cdot f$ — note the appearance of $\pi d n_w$ which is the workpiece surface speed.

### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Grinding Energy ($e$)

Specific energy is defined as energy consumed per unit volume removed. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Given:**
- Tangential grinding force: $F_c$ (N)
- Wheel speed: $V_c$ (m/s)
- MRR: $Q$ (mm³/s)

**Step 1:** Power consumed by the grinding process (mechanical, at spindle):

$$P = F_c \cdot V_c \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

**Step 2:** Convert MRR from mm³/s to m³/s for unit consistency:

$$Q_{SI} = \frac{Q}{10^{9}} \quad \text{[m}^3/\text{s]}$$

**Step 3:** Energy per unit volume:

$$\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{P}{Q_{SI}} \\
  &= \frac{F_c \cdot V_c \cdot 10^{9}}{Q}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Expressing purely in mm units (common in KTU lab):

$$e = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c \times 10^{3}}{Q} \quad \text{[N·m/mm}^3 = \text{J/mm}^3]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values of $e$ for engineering materials (KTU expected values):**
> - Mild steel: 20–30 J/mm³
> - Hardened tool steel: 30–50 J/mm³
> - Cast iron: 12–18 J/mm³
> - Aluminium: 5–10 J/mm³

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Machine Selection vs. Operation

| Selection Criterion | Surface Grinding | Cylindrical Grinding | Tool Grinding |
|:-------------------:|:----------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------:|
| Workpiece geometry | Flat, prismatic | Round, external/internal | Single-point tools, drills |
| Primary wheel shape | Straight / cup | Straight | Flared cup / dish |
| Typical wheel marking | AA-36-K-5-V | AA-60-K-5-V | AA-60-J-7-V |
| Coolant type | Soluble oil | Soluble oil + filtered | Often dry / light oil |
| Inspection instrument | DTI + Surface plate | DTI + Sine bar | Optical projector / Toolmaker's microscope |
| Typical Ra achievable | 0.4 – 0.8 µm | 0.2 – 0.4 µm | 0.8 – 1.6 µm |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Lab Style)

**Problem:** A surface grinder has a wheel of diameter **250 mm** rotating at **1450 rpm**. The table traverses at **10 m/min**, depth of cut is **0.02 mm/pass**, and cross-feed is **5 mm/stroke**. Calculate: (i) wheel peripheral speed, (ii) MRR, (iii) specific energy if measured tangential force is **80 N**.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad V_c &= \pi D N = \pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot \left(\frac{1450}{60}\right) \\
                    &= \pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 24.167 \\
                    &= 18.98 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii)} \quad Q &= V_w \cdot b \cdot a \\
                     &= \frac{10\,000}{60} \cdot 5 \cdot 0.02 \\
                     &= 166.67 \cdot 0.10 \\
                     &= 16.667 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii)} \quad e &= \frac{F_c \cdot V_c \times 10^{3}}{Q} \\
                      &= \frac{80 \cdot 18.98 \cdot 1000}{16.667} \\
                      &= 91\,104 \;\text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The huge value of $e$ above signals either (a) very coarse grit or (b) inefficient parameters — typical finish-grinding values are 20–50 J/mm³. This is a deliberate result to test whether students flag unrealistic parameters during viva.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Universal Grinding Machine

The diagram below maps the functional interaction between subsystems of a generic precision grinder. It is drawn at the **block level** because physical drawings are not reproducible in Mermaid — the architecture is what matters for KTU lab records.

```mermaid

    A1[PowerSupplyUnit] --> B1[ElectricSpindleMotor]

    G1 --> H1[ChipFlushingNozzle]
    H1 --> I1[SettlingTank]
    I1 --> J1[CoolantPump]
    J1 --> G1
    D1 --> K1[DressingTool]
    K1 --> L1[DresserSlide]
    L1 --> M1[ControlPanel]
    M1 --> N1[DigitalReadoutDRO]
    M1 --> O1[ServoFeedAxis]
    O1 --> F1
    D1 --> P1[InspectionProbe]
    P1 --> Q1[SurfaceRoughnessTester]
    P1 --> R1[DialTestIndicator]
    P1 --> S1[ToolmakersMicroscope]
```

### 4.2 Process-Flow Architecture — Grinding Operation Sequence

This flow chart shows the canonical step-by-step procedure that students must follow in the KTU lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Step01StudyDrawing] --> N2[Step02SelectWheelMarking]
    N2 --> N3[Step03MountAndBalanceWheel]

```

### 4.3 Subsystem Matrix — Surface vs. Cylindrical vs. Tool Grinder

| Subsystem | Surface Grinder | Cylindrical Grinder | Tool Grinder |
|:---------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:------------:|
| Wheel head orientation | Horizontal / Vertical spindle | Swivel ± 30° around vertical | Swivel ± 360° |
| Work holding | Magnetic chuck / vice | Between centres / chuck | Universal collet / 3-jaw chuck |
| Feed axis | X = cross, Y = vertical, Z = table | X = infeed, Z = traverse | X = infeed, Y = swivel |
| Coolant | Flood | Flood + splash guard | Light drip / MQL |
| Inspection tool | DTI on surface plate | DTI on V-block | Toolmaker's microscope |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any **six parts** of a surface grinder and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Base** – supports the entire machine and absorbs vibration.
2. **Column** – vertical structure that supports the wheel head.
3. **Wheel head** – houses the spindle and grinding wheel.
4. **Work table** – reciprocating surface on which the workpiece is clamped.
5. **Magnetic chuck** – electromagnetic clamping device for ferrous workpieces.
6. **Coolant tank** – stores cutting fluid with pump and piping.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **wheel head** with the **column**. The column is the vertical pillar; the wheel head is the horizontal projecting unit that carries the spindle. Mixing these up costs a full mark.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Explain the difference between **dressing** and **truing** of a grinding wheel with suitable sketches.

**Model Answer:**
- **Truing** is the process of restoring the **geometric shape** (concentricity, flatness) of a grinding wheel by removing bond material to expose a new layer of grits in the correct profile. A **diamond dresser** is traversed across the wheel face.
- **Dressing** is the process of **sharpening** the wheel by removing dull, glazed, or loaded grits and exposing fresh sharp grits. It also removes swarf clogging the pores.
- In short: *Truing restores shape; dressing restores sharpness.* Both are usually performed together.

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **main parts and working principle** of a horizontal-spindle reciprocating **surface grinder**. State the role of the magnetic chuck. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a surface grinding operation on mild steel (workpiece length 120 mm, width 60 mm), the following data was recorded: wheel diameter 200 mm, wheel speed 1500 rpm, table traverse 8 m/min, cross-feed 4 mm/stroke, depth of cut 0.015 mm/pass, tangential force 60 N. Calculate: (i) wheel peripheral speed, (ii) MRR, (iii) specific grinding energy, (iv) power consumed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Block diagram of surface grinder includes: base, column, wheel head, spindle, work table, magnetic chuck, coolant system.
- **Working:** The wheel rotates at high speed. Workpiece is clamped on the magnetic chuck. The table reciprocates under the wheel. Cross-feed is given at the end of each stroke; depth is set by vertical down-feed of the wheel head.
- **Magnetic chuck role:** Provides uniform holding force on ferromagnetic workpieces over the entire table surface, allows quick clamping/unclamping, and avoids distortion caused by mechanical clamping.

**Model Solution (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad V_c &= \pi D N = \pi \cdot 0.2 \cdot \frac{1500}{60} \\
                    &= \pi \cdot 0.2 \cdot 25 = 15.71 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii)} \quad V_w &= \frac{8000}{60} = 133.33 \;\text{mm/s} \\
                      Q &= V_w \cdot b \cdot a = 133.33 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.015 = 8.0 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii)} \quad e &= \frac{F_c \cdot V_c \times 10^{3}}{Q} = \frac{60 \cdot 15.71 \cdot 1000}{8.0} \\
                      &= 117\,825 \;\text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iv)} \quad P &= F_c \cdot V_c = 60 \cdot 15.71 = 942.6 \;\text{W} \approx 0.94 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **centre-type cylindrical grinder** with a labelled sketch. Compare it with a **centreless grinder** in tabular form (any **four points**). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical grinding operation is performed on a shaft of diameter **40 mm**, length **200 mm**, with a wheel of diameter **300 mm**, hardness grade **K**, running at **1700 rpm**. Workpiece speed is **200 rpm**, depth of cut **0.02 mm/pass**, feed **0.1 mm/rev**. Calculate: (i) equivalent diameter, (ii) MRR, (iii) material removed per pass, (iv) time to grind one full diameter reduction of 0.5 mm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Centre-type cylindrical grinder:** The workpiece is held between two centres (dead and live), rotates slowly, while the grinding wheel rotates at high speed. The wheel is fed in radially to achieve diameter reduction. A traverse feed parallel to the axis allows grinding the entire length.
- **Tabular comparison:**

| Feature | Centre-type | Centreless |
|:-------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| Work holding | Between centres | Rest blade + regulating wheel |
| Loading time | High (centring needed) | Very low (no centring) |
| Suitable for | Long slender shafts | Short rigid parts, pins |
| Through-feed possible | No | Yes |

**Model Solution (b):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad d_e &= \frac{d \cdot D}{d + D} = \frac{40 \cdot 300}{40 + 300} = \frac{12000}{340} = 35.29 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(ii)} \quad Q &= \pi d n_w \cdot a_p \cdot f = \pi \cdot 40 \cdot \frac{200}{60} \cdot 0.02 \cdot 0.1 \\
                      &= \pi \cdot 40 \cdot 3.333 \cdot 0.002 \\
                      &= 0.838 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iii)} \quad \text{Material per pass} &= \pi d \cdot L \cdot a_p = \pi \cdot 40 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.02 = 502.65 \;\text{mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(iv)} \quad \text{Number of passes} &= \frac{0.5}{0.02} = 25 \\
                                          \text{Time/pass} &= \frac{L}{V_w} = \frac{200}{333.3 \cdot 60} \cdot 60 = 0.6 \;\text{s} \\
                                          \text{Total time} &= 25 \cdot 0.6 = 15 \;\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Unit consistency is non-negotiable.** Mixing mm with m, or rpm with rps, costs a full 2 marks automatically in any numerical.
> 2. **Spelling "truing" vs "trueing"** — KTU accepts both, but **"dressing" and "truing" are NOT synonyms**. Writing them as one concept loses 2 marks in viva.
> 3. **Magnetic chuck explanation** must include the word *"ferromagnetic"* — without it, 1 mark is deducted.
> 4. **Tabular comparison** in Part B must have *minimum four distinct points* — bullet points do not earn full marks unless placed inside a clean markdown table.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Grinding = abrasive machining** with a multi-point wheel; not a single-point cutting tool.
- **Standard wheel marking (5 symbols):** Abrasive Type – Grit Size – Hardness – Structure – Bond.
- **Hardness grades A → Z**, where A is the softest wheel.
- **Five process parameters:** $V_c$, $V_w$, $a$, $f$, and spark-out.
- **MRR — Surface grinding:** $Q = V_w \cdot b \cdot a$.
- **MRR — Cylindrical grinding:** $Q = \pi d n_w \cdot a_p \cdot f$.
- **Specific energy:** $e = F_c V_c \times 10^{3} / Q$ (in J/mm³).
- **Coolant** is mandatory; it removes 80%+ of the generated heat.
- **Truing** restores *shape*; **dressing** restores *sharpness*.
- **Inspection triad:** DTI (dimension), sine bar (angle), Talysurf (Ra).
- **Centreless grinder** holds the workpiece on a **rest blade** with the **regulating wheel** providing rotation — no centres needed.
- **Magnetic chuck** works only on **ferromagnetic** materials.
- **Theoretical Ra (grinding):** $R_a \approx f^2 / (32 r)$.
- **Equivalent diameter** for cylindrical grinding: $d_e = dD / (d + D)$.
- **Soft wheel for hard workpiece; hard wheel for soft workpiece** — universal selection rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked *"which grinding process for which component?"* in viva, always mention **the inspection method and achievable Ra** in the same breath — that single move earns you the examiner's nod and the last 1–2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Welding: Arc and Gas Welding, Butt Welding, and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Welding: Arc and Gas Welding, Butt Welding, and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Welding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Welding (KTU 2024 Definition):** Welding is a permanent joining process in which two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastics) are united by heating them to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of a filler material, to produce a localized coalescence of the materials.

In the context of **PCMRL408 – Mechanical Engineering Lab**, the focus is on **fusion welding** of **Mild Steel (M.S.) sheets** using:
- **Arc Welding** (Shielded Metal Arc Welding — SMAW)
- **Gas Welding** (Oxy-Acetylene Welding — OAW)

…and the two fundamental joint configurations:
- **Butt Joint** (pieces placed edge-to-edge in the same plane)
- **Lap Joint** (pieces overlap each other on a common surface)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of welding as "metallic glue that works on a heated molecular level."** Imagine you have two pieces of chocolate. If you press them together while gently warming the joint, the chocolate melts and bonds into one solid piece. Welding does the same thing to steel — only the "warming" is intense heat (3000–6500 °C) and the "glue" is a filler rod that melts and fuses with the base metal.

| Everyday Analogy | Welding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Melting candle wax to join two pieces | Fusion welding |
| Using a hot glue gun (glue + heat) | Gas welding (OAW) with filler rod |
| Iron-on patches on clothes (overlap + heat) | Lap joint welding |
| Joining two bookends edge-to-edge with epoxy | Butt joint welding |
| Sticking metal pieces with a glue that conducts electricity | Arc welding (uses electric current) |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Melting point of Mild Steel:** **1500 °C** (approx.)
- **Acetylene flame temperature:** **3200 °C**
- **Arc temperature (SMAW):** **5500–6500 °C**
- **Neutral flame ratio (O₂ : C₂H₂):** **1 : 1** (by volume)
- **Standard M.S. sheet thickness used in lab:** **2 mm – 6 mm**
- **Standard filler rod diameter:** **2.5 mm – 3.15 mm**
- **Electrode diameter (for 2–4 mm sheet):** **2.5 mm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The lab emphasizes **manual skill development**, **safety protocol compliance**, and **visual inspection of weld quality** — not advanced numerical analysis. Examiners will test your **process knowledge, safety awareness, and ability to identify defects**.

## 1.4 Classification of Welding Processes Used in This Lab

```
Welding
├── Fusion Welding
│   ├── Arc Welding (SMAW / Stick Welding)
│   └── Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene / OAW)
└── Solid-State Welding (NOT in this lab)
```

| Process | Heat Source | Filler Metal | Shielding | Application in Lab |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arc Welding (SMAW)** | Electric Arc | Coated Electrode | Flux coating | Butt & Lap joints of M.S. sheet |
| **Gas Welding (OAW)** | Oxy-Acetylene Flame | Filler Rod | Combustion gases | Thin M.S. sheet (≤ 3 mm), brazing demo |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arc Welding (Shielded Metal Arc Welding — SMAW)

### 2.1.1 Principle
An electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the **base metal (M.S. sheet)**. The intense heat (≈ **6000 °C**) melts both the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a molten weld pool. The flux coating decomposes to produce:
1. **Shielding gas** — protects the molten pool from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
2. **Slag** — floats on top of the weld pool, solidifies, and is later chipped off.
3. **Arc stabilizers** — ensure a smooth, stable arc.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each step matters — the "Why" and "How":**

1. **Power Source Selection:** An AC or DC welding transformer/rectifier is selected. The **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** is typically **60–80 V**, dropping to **18–25 V (arc voltage)** when welding. *Why?* High OCV is needed to *initiate* the arc, but a lower voltage sustains the arc safely.
2. **Electrode Selection:** For M.S., a **rutile-coated (E6013) or basic-coated (E7018)** electrode is used. *Why?* Rutile coating gives smooth, aesthetic beads — ideal for student lab work.
3. **Setting Current:** Current is set using the **amperage knob** on the welding machine. For a **2.5 mm electrode**, current ≈ **60–90 A**.
4. **Striking the Arc:** The electrode is tapped or scratched on the workpiece to initiate the arc. *Why?* This creates a momentary short circuit; the high current density vaporizes metal and establishes the arc.
5. **Travel Angle:** The electrode is held at a **10–15° drag angle** (pushing) for thin sheets and a **10–15° pull angle** for thicker sheets.
6. **Travel Speed:** Maintained at **4–6 mm/s** for 2 mm sheet to avoid burn-through.
7. **Slag Removal:** After cooling, the solidified slag is chipped off with a **chipping hammer** and the surface cleaned with a **wire brush**.

### 2.1.3 Key Engineering Insight
> **Heat Input (H)** is the most important process parameter in arc welding, because excessive heat causes distortion and burn-through, while insufficient heat causes lack of fusion.

---

## 2.2 Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene Welding — OAW)

### 2.2.1 Principle
A mixture of **oxygen (O₂)** and **acetylene (C₂H₂)** is burned at the tip of a **welding torch**, producing a flame that melts the base metal and filler rod. The flame temperature is **~3200 °C** — much lower than an arc, so OAW is used for **thin sheets and non-ferrous metals**.

### 2.2.2 Three Types of Flames

| Flame Type | O₂ : C₂H₂ Ratio | Color | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Neutral Flame** | **1 : 1** | Bright blue inner cone, light blue envelope | **General welding of M.S.** (most common in lab) |
| **Carburizing (Reducing) Flame** | < 1 : 1 (excess acetylene) | Long, soft, feathery inner cone | Hard-facing, surfaces needing extra carbon |
| **Oxidizing Flame** | > 1 : 1 (excess oxygen) | Short, sharp, pointed inner cone | Brass & bronze welding (NOT for M.S.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the lab exam, if asked *"Which flame is used for M.S.?"* — the **only correct answer is the NEUTRAL FLAME**. Oxidation of iron leads to slag and porosity if an oxidizing flame is used.

### 2.2.3 Equipment Sequence
**Cylinder (O₂)** → **Regulator (O₂)** → **Hose (green)** → **Torch (O₂ inlet)** 
**Cylinder (C₂H₂)** → **Regulator (C₂H₂)** → **Hose (red)** → **Torch (C₂H₂ inlet)** → **Nozzle tip**

### 2.2.4 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Open acetylene valve first** (slightly), light it with a striker.
2. **Open oxygen valve gradually** until a neutral flame is achieved.
3. **Adjust the inner cone** to a length of **2–3× the tip orifice diameter**.
4. **Hold the torch** at a **45° angle** to the workpiece.
5. **Melt filler rod and base metal simultaneously** — keep the inner cone **2–4 mm** from the workpiece.
6. **Travel speed:** For 2 mm M.S. sheet, ≈ **3–5 mm/s**.
7. **Close oxygen first, then acetylene** while shutting down — prevents soot formation.

---

## 2.3 Butt Joint Welding of M.S. Sheet

### Definition
A **butt joint** is formed by placing two M.S. sheets edge-to-edge in the same plane and welding along the seam.

### Edge Preparation
| Sheet Thickness | Edge Preparation |
|---|---|
| **< 3 mm** | No bevel (square butt) |
| **3–6 mm** | Single-V bevel (60° included angle, 1–2 mm root face) |
| **> 6 mm** | Double-V bevel |

### Gap & Root Face
- **Root gap:** **1.5–2 mm** (allows electrode/filler access to root).
- **Root face (land):** **1–2 mm** (prevents burn-through).

### Welding Technique for Butt Joint
- **Tack welds** placed every **50–75 mm** along the seam to hold alignment.
- **Root run** (first pass) deposited with low current.
- **Cap pass** (final pass) for aesthetic surface and full penetration.
- Use **backing strip** (if available) to ensure full penetration in lab exercises.

---

## 2.4 Lap Joint Welding of M.S. Sheet

### Definition
A **lap joint** is formed by overlapping two M.S. sheets by a distance equal to **2–3× the sheet thickness** and welding along the two edges of the top sheet (single-side or double-side fillet welds).

### Fillet Weld Geometry
> [!NOTE]
> A **fillet weld** is the standard weld type for lap joints. The weld cross-section is approximately a right triangle.

- **Leg length (L):** The two equal sides of the triangle.
- **Throat thickness (T):** The shortest distance from the root to the hypotenuse = **0.707 × L**.
- For 2 mm M.S. sheet → typical leg length = **2.5–3 mm**.

### Welding Technique for Lap Joint
- Use **fillet welds** along both edges of the overlap.
- Hold electrode at **45°** to both plates.
- Avoid excessive heat — lap joints trap heat and can warp easily.

---

## 2.5 Safety Procedures in the Welding Lab

> [!WARNING]
> **MANDATORY Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) — KTU Mandatory Checklist:**
> - **Auto-darkening welding helmet** (shade #10–13 for arc)
> - **Welding gloves** (leather, full-cuff)
> - **Leather apron / flame-resistant jacket**
> - **Safety boots** (steel-toe)
> - **Clear goggles** for gas welding (with proper shade lens)
> - **Welding screen / curtains** to protect other students from arc flash

### General Safety Rules
1. **Never weld in wet areas** — electrocution hazard.
2. **Always inspect cables and electrode holder** for damage before use.
3. **Keep a fire extinguisher (CO₂ or dry powder) within reach.**
4. **Ensure proper ventilation** — fumes from M.S. welding contain **manganese and iron oxide**, both harmful.
5. **Do not look at the arc with naked eyes** — risk of **arc eye (photokeratitis)**.
6. **Cylinders must be upright, chained, and have caps on when not in use.**
7. **Never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings** — explosion hazard.
8. **Acetylene pressure must never exceed 1.03 bar (15 psi)**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are the most commonly tested quantitative concepts in KTU 2024 lab viva and written exams. Memorize these.**

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Energy delivered per mm of weld |
| 2 | **Throat Thickness (T)** | $T = 0.707 \times L$ | mm | Strength-controlling dimension of fillet weld |
| 3 | **Shear Strength of Fillet Weld** | $P = 0.707 \times L \times t \times \tau$ | N | Where $t$ = throat = $0.707L$ → so $P = 0.5 \times L^2 \times \tau$ |
| 4 | **Welding Speed (S)** | $S = \dfrac{I \times 60 \times V \times \text{eff.}}{H \times 1000}$ | mm/min | Related to heat input |
| 5 | **Deposition Rate** | $D = \dfrac{W}{t_{\text{arc}}}$ | g/min | Weight of weld metal deposited per minute |
| 6 | **Penetration Depth (estimate)** | $P_d \approx \dfrac{I}{100 \times d_e}$ | mm | $I$ = current, $d_e$ = electrode diameter |
| 7 | **Weld Cost** | $C = C_e + C_g + C_l + C_o$ | ₹/m | Electrode + gas + labour + overhead |
| 8 | **Electrode Consumption** | $W_e = \dfrac{\text{weld length} \times \text{area}}{L_{\text{rod}}}$ | kg | Approx. 1.5–1.7 kg/kg of weld metal (E6013) |
| 9 | **Acetylene : Oxygen Ratio** | $1 : 1$ (neutral) by volume | — | Stoichiometric ratio for complete combustion |
| 10 | **Weld Length per Electrode** | $L_{\text{rod}} = \dfrac{W_e}{\text{density} \times A_{\text{rod}}}$ | m | Useful for cost calculation |

> **Where:**
> - $V$ = Arc voltage (V)
> - $I$ = Welding current (A)
> - $S$ = Travel speed (mm/min)
> - $L$ = Leg length of fillet weld (mm)
> - $\tau$ = Allowable shear stress (MPa) ≈ **110 MPa for E6013 weld on M.S.**

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used in **welding procedure specification (WPS)**, **fabrication cost estimation**, and **structural welding code (IS 816, AWS D1.1)** compliance in industry.

---

## 2.7 Common Weld Defects (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Contaminants (rust, oil), inadequate shielding | Clean base metal, proper arc length |
| **Lack of Penetration** | Low current, fast travel, wide root gap | Increase current, slow down |
| **Lack of Fusion** | Cold lap, incorrect torch/electrode angle | Maintain correct angle and heat |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current, long arc length | Reduce current, use shorter arc |
| **Crater Crack** | Abrupt arc termination at bead end | Use **crater filling** technique (reverse motion at end) |
| **Spatter** | Long arc, high current | Maintain proper arc length |
| **Distortion** | Uneven heating/cooling | Use tack welds, balanced welding sequence |
| **Slag Inclusion** | Insufficient cleaning between passes | Chip & wire-brush between passes |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Input in Arc Welding

The **Heat Input (H)** is the amount of energy transferred from the arc to the workpiece per unit length of weld. It is a derived parameter because it links voltage, current, and travel speed into a single quality-controlling metric.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Power generated in the arc**
The arc acts as an electrical load dissipating power:

$$
P = V \times I
$$

*Where $P$ = arc power in **Watts (W)**, $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts (V)**, $I$ = current in **Amperes (A)**.*

> **Why:** This is just Ohm's law applied to the arc as a resistive element — except the arc is non-linear, so V is not exactly I × R.

**Step 2 — Total energy delivered to weld a length L**

If the arc travels a length $L$ in time $t$ at constant power:

$$
E_{\text{total}} = P \times t = V \times I \times t
$$

*Where $E_{\text{total}}$ is in **Joules (J)**, $t$ in **seconds (s)**.*

> **Why:** Energy is power multiplied by time — the welding arc continuously deposits energy as it moves along the joint.

**Step 3 — Convert time to travel speed**

The travel speed $S$ is defined as:

$$
S = \dfrac{L}{t}
$$

Rearranging for time:

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

*Where $S$ is in **mm/min** (typically) or **mm/s**.*

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the energy equation**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = V \times I \times \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Define Heat Input as Energy per unit length**

$$
H = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{L} = \dfrac{V \times I}{S}
$$

The above gives $H$ in **J/mm**. To convert to the industry standard **kJ/mm**, we multiply by $\dfrac{1}{1000}$ and account for the **60 s/min** conversion if $S$ is in mm/min:

$$
H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad [\text{kJ/mm}]
$$

> **Why the 60?** Because if $S$ is in mm/min, we need to convert minutes to seconds (divide by 60), and 60 appears in the numerator when working with minutes as the time base.

### ✅ Final Formula (KTU Standard)

$$
\boxed{H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}} \quad \text{kJ/mm}
$$

> **Acceptable range for M.S. SMAW (per AWS D1.1):** $H \approx 0.8$ to $2.0$ kJ/mm. **Lower for thin sheets, higher for thick plates.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Heat Input

> **Problem:** A student performs SMAW on a 4 mm M.S. sheet with arc voltage 22 V, current 100 A, and travel speed 150 mm/min. Calculate the heat input and comment on whether it is suitable.

**Given:**
- $V = 22$ V
- $I = 100$ A
- $S = 150$ mm/min

**Calculation:**

$$
H = \dfrac{22 \times 100 \times 60}{150 \times 1000}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
22 \times 100 \times 60 = 132{,}000
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
150 \times 1000 = 150{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
H = \dfrac{132{,}000}{150{,}000} = 0.88 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 0.88 kJ/mm is within the acceptable range for 4 mm M.S. sheet — **suitable**. For 2 mm sheet, this would be **too high** and may cause burn-through.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Fillet Weld Throat Thickness

A **fillet weld** has a triangular cross-section with two equal legs $L$ meeting at the **fusion face** (root).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of the right triangle**

The fillet weld is an **isosceles right triangle** with:
- Two equal sides (legs) of length $L$
- Hypotenuse of length $L\sqrt{2}$

**Step 2 — Throat is the perpendicular from root to hypotenuse**

The throat thickness $T$ is the shortest distance from the root (right-angle vertex) to the hypotenuse.

For an isosceles right triangle with legs $L$:

$$
T = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the denominator**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{2}$:

$$
T = \dfrac{L \times \sqrt{2}}{2} = \dfrac{L \times 1.414}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T = 0.707 \times L}
$$

> **Why 0.707?** Because $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$. This is a **derived constant** in structural welding codes (IS 816, AWS D1.1).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Strength of a Fillet Weld Loaded in Shear

The fillet weld is assumed to fail by **shear** along the throat plane.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Throat area of the weld**

For a fillet weld of leg length $L$ and length $l$ (length along the joint):

$$
A_{\text{throat}} = T \times l = 0.707 \times L \times l
$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress capacity**

If $\tau$ is the allowable shear stress of the weld metal:

$$
P = A_{\text{throat}} \times \tau = 0.707 \times L \times l \times \tau
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $T = 0.707L$ for clarity**

$$
\boxed{P = 0.5 \times L^2 \times l \times \tau / L = 0.707 \times L \times l \times \tau}
$$

> **Standard form in IS 816 and AWS D1.1:** 
> $$P = 0.707 \times L \times l \times \tau$$

### Worked Numerical Example — Fillet Weld Strength

> **Problem:** A lap joint is welded on both sides with a fillet weld of leg length 4 mm and total weld length (each side) 100 mm. Allowable shear stress is 110 MPa. Calculate the strength per side.

**Given:**
- $L = 4$ mm
- $l = 100$ mm
- $\tau = 110$ MPa = $110$ N/mm²

**Calculation:**

$$
P = 0.707 \times 4 \times 100 \times 110
$$

Step 1:

$$
0.707 \times 4 = 2.828
$$

Step 2:

$$
2.828 \times 100 = 282.8
$$

Step 3:

$$
282.8 \times 110 = 31{,}108 \text{ N} \approx 31.1 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Result:** Each side of the lap joint can carry **31.1 kN** in shear. **Total joint capacity (both sides)** = $2 \times 31.1 = 62.2$ kN.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Selection for M.S. Sheet

| Sheet Thickness | Recommended Process | Filler/Electrode | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **< 1.5 mm** | Gas Welding (OAW) | Bare filler rod | Arc may burn through; use low flame |
| **1.5 – 3 mm** | Gas Welding (OAW) **or** Arc (low current) | Ø 1.6–2.5 mm E6013 | Tack weld at 50 mm intervals |
| **3 – 6 mm** | Arc Welding (SMAW) | Ø 2.5–3.15 mm E6013 | Single-V bevel for butt joints |
| **> 6 mm** | Arc Welding (SMAW) | Ø 3.15–4 mm E7018 | Multi-pass, possible preheating |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Lab Procedure — Arc Welding of a Butt Joint

1. **Preparation (5 min):** Wear full PPE. Inspect electrode holder, cables, and ground clamp.
2. **Setup (5 min):** Set current to **60–80 A** for 2.5 mm electrode. Clamp M.S. sheets (2 mm × 50 mm × 100 mm) on the welding table with a **2 mm root gap**.
3. **Tack welding (3 min):** Place **2 tack welds**, 25 mm from each end. Allow to cool.
4. **Root pass:** Strike the arc, maintain a **2–3 mm arc length**, travel at **4–5 mm/s** along the seam.
5. **Slag removal:** After cooling (≈ 1 min), chip off slag with hammer. Wire-brush.
6. **Visual inspection (VTI):** Check for undercuts, porosity, and bead uniformity.
7. **Distortion check:** Measure with a straightedge — record any warping.

---

## 3.7 Step-by-Step Lab Procedure — Gas Welding of a Lap Joint

1. **Cylinder check:** Open acetylene main valve slightly, then regulator. Repeat for oxygen. Check for leaks with soapy water.
2. **Lighting procedure:** Open acetylene torch valve, light with striker, then slowly add oxygen until **neutral flame** (sharp inner cone).
3. **Sheet setup:** Overlap two M.S. sheets (2 mm × 50 mm × 100 mm) by **40 mm** on a firebrick or welding table.
4. **Tack welds:** Apply 2 small tack welds at the corners of the overlap.
5. **Fillet welding:** Hold torch at **45°**, inner cone 2–4 mm from surface. Filler rod dipped in flux (for galvanized, but **not for M.S.**).
6. **Weld both edges** of the lap joint.
7. **Cooling:** Allow to air cool — do **not** quench (causes hardening and cracking).
8. **Inspection:** Visual check for fusion along the full length.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**What is a neutral flame? How is it obtained in oxy-acetylene welding?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **neutral flame** is the flame obtained when oxygen and acetylene are mixed in the **stoichiometric ratio of 1:1 by volume**, resulting in complete combustion of acetylene with no excess of either gas.

**Procedure to obtain:**
1. Open the acetylene cylinder valve and adjust regulator to working pressure (≈ 0.5 bar).
2. Open the acetylene torch valve slightly and ignite with a striker.
3. Slowly open the oxygen torch valve.
4. Adjust the oxygen flow until a **sharp, bright blue inner cone** is obtained.
5. The inner cone length should be **2–3 times the nozzle tip orifice diameter**.

**Characteristics:** 
- Inner cone is **bright blue and well-defined**
- Outer envelope is **light blue and less luminous**
- No excess carbon (no sooty flame) and no excess oxygen (no harsh, short hiss)

> **Importance:** A neutral flame is the **only recommended flame for M.S. welding**, as it does not oxidize or carburize the weld pool.

> **[3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for procedure, 1 for characteristics]**
