## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Internal Lab Assessment Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **arc welding process (SMAW)** in detail, including the working principle, equipment used, electrode classification, and the function of the flux coating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **at least 5 weld defects** commonly encountered in arc welding, with their causes and prevention methods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Working Principle (2 marks):**
> In Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW), an **electric arc** is established between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the **base metal (M.S. workpiece)**. The arc is initiated by touching the electrode to the workpiece and then withdrawing it by 2–4 mm, creating an ionized gas path. The intense heat of the arc (≈ 6000 °C) melts both the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a **molten weld pool**. As the electrode is consumed, fresh filler metal is continuously deposited. The flux coating decomposes to form **shielding gas** (protects the molten pool from atmospheric contamination) and **slag** (a solidified layer that floats on top, protecting the cooling weld from oxidation).

> **Equipment (2 marks):**
> 1. **Welding transformer / rectifier** — provides constant current (CC) with high OCV (60–80 V) and low arc voltage (18–25 V).
> 2. **Electrode holder** — insulated handle with metal jaws to grip the electrode.
> 3. **Earth clamp** — connects the workpiece to the power source, completing the circuit.
> 4. **Welding cables** — heavy-duty, well-insulated copper cables.
> 5. **Chipping hammer and wire brush** — for slag removal and cleaning.
> 6. **PPE** — helmet, gloves, apron, boots.

> **Electrode Classification (1.5 marks):**
> The **AWS (American Welding Society) classification** uses 4–5 digits, e.g., **E6013**:
> - **E** = Electrode
> - **60** = Minimum tensile strength in **ksi × 10** = 60,000 psi ≈ 410 MPa
> - **1** = Welding position (1 = all positions)
> - **3** = Type of coating and current (3 = rutile coating, AC/DC)
> 
> Common lab electrodes: **E6013** (rutile, easy to use, smooth bead), **E7018** (basic, low hydrogen, structural).

> **Function of Flux Coating (1.5 marks):**
> 1. Generates **shielding gas** (CO₂, H₂) to protect the weld pool from oxygen and nitrogen.
> 2. Forms a **molten slag** that covers the weld pool and floats impurities to the surface.
> 3. Provides **arc-stabilizing elements** (e.g., potassium, sodium compounds) for smooth arc.
> 4. Adds **deoxidizers** (Si, Mn, Al) to clean the weld metal.
> 5. Slows the cooling rate, reducing the risk of cracking.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| # | Defect | Cause | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Porosity** — small cavities in weld metal | Contaminants (rust, oil, paint), long arc, high travel speed | Clean base metal, use shorter arc, proper shielding |
| 2 | **Lack of Penetration** — fusion does not extend through joint thickness | Low current, fast travel, wide root gap | Increase current, slow travel, reduce root gap |
| 3 | **Lack of Fusion** — weld metal does not fuse with base metal | Cold lap, incorrect electrode angle, low heat | Increase current, maintain 70–80° electrode angle |
| 4 | **Undercut** — groove at weld toe | Excessive current, long arc length, wrong electrode angle | Reduce current, use short arc, correct travel angle |
| 5 | **Crater Cracks** — small cracks at the end of bead | Abrupt arc termination | Use crater-filling technique (reverse motion for 1–2 s) |
| 6 | **Spatter** — small metal particles around weld | Long arc, high current, dirty base metal | Maintain proper arc length, clean workpiece |
| 7 | **Distortion** — warping of the welded assembly | Uneven heating and cooling | Use balanced welding sequence, tack welds, clamping |
| 8 | **Slag Inclusion** — slag trapped in weld | Inadequate cleaning between passes | Chip and wire-brush between each pass |

> **Examiner's Note:** At least 5 defects are required for full marks. **Use the table format** — it makes your answer clear and easy to evaluate. Mention the cause and prevention **for each defect** (not just the name). **[7 marks: 1 mark per defect, 0.4 for cause, 0.4 for prevention, 0.2 for explanation]**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Alternative Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for performing a **butt joint on a 3 mm M.S. sheet using arc welding**, including edge preparation, parameter selection, and welding technique. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fillet weld of leg length 6 mm and total weld length 150 mm is subjected to a shear load. Given the allowable shear stress in the weld is 110 MPa, calculate the **strength of the weld**. Also calculate the **heat input** if the welding was done with $V = 24$ V, $I = 120$ A, and travel speed $S = 180$ mm/min. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Step 1 — Material and Equipment Preparation (1 mark):**
> - M.S. sheets: 3 mm thick × 50 mm wide × 150 mm long (typical lab size)
> - Electrode: **E6013, 2.5 mm diameter** (suitable for 3 mm sheet)
> - Power source: SMAW transformer with CC output

> **Step 2 — Edge Preparation (1.5 marks):**
> - For 3 mm sheet, **no bevel is required** (square butt preparation).
> - Ensure edges are **clean, straight, and free from rust, oil, and mill scale**.
> - Use a file or grinder to remove burrs and obtain a clean square edge.
> - Maintain a **root gap of 1.5–2 mm** between the two sheets.

> **Step 3 — Clamping and Alignment (1 mark):**
> - Place the two sheets on the welding table in the same plane.
> - Use **C-clamps or jigs** to hold them in position with the correct root gap.
> - Check alignment with a **straightedge** and a **welding gauge**.

> **Step 4 — Tack Welding (1 mark):**
> - Apply **2–3 small tack welds**, 30–40 mm from each end and one in the middle (for sheets > 100 mm).
> - Each tack weld length: **10–15 mm**.
> - Allow each tack to cool before proceeding.

> **Step 5 — Parameter Selection (1 mark):**
> - **Current:** **70–90 A** for 2.5 mm electrode on 3 mm M.S. sheet.
> - **Arc voltage:** **20–25 V** (machine set automatically by CC mode).
> - **Travel speed:** **100–150 mm/min**.
> - **Arc length:** **2–3 mm** (short arc).

> **Step 6 — Welding Technique (1.5 marks):**
> - Hold the electrode at **70–80° to the workpiece** (slight drag angle).
> - Strike the arc by **scratch method** (drag electrode across surface, then lift to 2–3 mm).
> - Move the electrode along the seam at a **steady, uniform speed**.
> - Maintain a consistent **arc length and electrode angle**.
> - For 3 mm sheet, a **single-pass weld (root + cap in one)** is sufficient.
> - At the end of the bead, perform **crater filling** — move back slightly to fill the crater, then break the arc.

> **Step 7 — Post-Welding (1 mark):**
> - Allow the joint to **cool naturally** — **do not quench**.
> - Use a **chipping hammer** to remove any surface slag.
> - Clean with a **wire brush** to obtain a clean weld surface.
> - Visually inspect for **undercut, porosity, cracks, and bead uniformity**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Calculate Throat Thickness**

The throat of a fillet weld:

$$
T = 0.707 \times L = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.242 \text{ mm}
$$

> **This is the effective area-bearing dimension.** (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Calculate Throat Area**

$$
A_{\text{throat}} = T \times l = 4.242 \times 150 = 636.3 \text{ mm}^2
$$

> **Calculation check:** $4.242 \times 150 = 636.3$ mm². (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Calculate Weld Strength (Shear Capacity)**

$$
P = A_{\text{throat}} \times \tau = 636.3 \times 110
$$

$$
P = 69{,}993 \text{ N} \approx 70 \text{ kN}
$$

> **The weld can safely carry a shear load of 70 kN.** (1.5 marks)

**Step 4 — Calculate Heat Input**

Using the standard KTU formula:

$$
H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}
$$

Substitute:

$$
H = \dfrac{24 \times 120 \times 60}{180 \times 1000}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
24 \times 120 \times 60 = 172{,}800
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
180 \times 1000 = 180{,}000
$$

Therefore:

$$
H = \dfrac{172{,}800}{180{,}000} = 0.96 \text{ kJ/mm}
$$

> **This heat input is suitable for 3 mm M.S. sheet (within AWS range of 0.8–2.0 kJ/mm).** (2.5 marks)

> **Alternative combined formula** (often used as a shortcut):
> $$P = 0.707 \times L \times l \times \tau$$
> $$P = 0.707 \times 6 \times 150 \times 110 = 69{,}993 \text{ N} \approx 70 \text{ kN}$$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Welding Lab Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the three flame types** — Many students write "oxidizing flame is used for M.S." — **WRONG!** Only **neutral flame** for M.S. ❌
> 2. **Skipping safety procedures** — Examiners award **0.5–1 mark** specifically for mentioning PPE. ❌
> 3. **Wrong formula for heat input** — Some students use $H = V \times I$ (units wrong) instead of $H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$. ❌
> 4. **Using $T = L/2$ instead of $T = 0.707L$** — This is a common error. The throat is **not** half the leg length. ❌
> 5. **Not specifying units** — Always write kJ/mm, mm, MPa, etc. ❌
> 6. **Confusing arc and gas welding applications** — Gas welding for thick plate is impractical. ❌
> 7. **Forgetting slag removal** — Slag is **only in arc welding**, not in gas welding of M.S. ❌

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for PCMRL408 — Module Experiment 2:**

### 🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ **Arc temperature:** 5500–6500 °C | **Flame temperature:** 3200 °C
- ✅ **Melting point of M.S.:** 1500 °C
- ✅ **E6013:** General-purpose M.S. electrode; 60 = 60,000 psi tensile; 1 = all positions; 3 = rutile, AC/DC
- ✅ **Throat thickness:** $T = 0.707 \times L$ (NOT 0.5L)
- ✅ **Fillet weld strength:** $P = 0.707 \times L \times l \times \tau$
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm
- ✅ **Neutral flame ratio (O₂ : C₂H₂):** 1:1 by volume
- ✅ **Oxy-acetylene uses red hose for acetylene, green/black for oxygen**
- ✅ **Close O₂ first, then C₂H₂ when shutting down** (to avoid soot)
- ✅ **Tack welds every 50–75 mm** to hold alignment
- ✅ **Do NOT quench M.S. welds** — natural cooling only

### 🛡️ Safety — Always Mention in Exam
- ✅ PPE: helmet, gloves, apron, boots
- ✅ Fire extinguisher (CO₂ or dry powder)
- ✅ Ventilation — fumes of Mn and Fe₂O₃ are toxic
- ✅ No oil/grease on O₂ fittings
- ✅ Acetylene pressure ≤ 1.03 bar
- ✅ Look away from arc — arc eye risk

### 🔍 Defects to List
- ✅ Porosity, Lack of Penetration, Lack of Fusion, Undercut, Crater Crack, Spatter, Distortion, Slag Inclusion

### 📐 Joint Types
- ✅ **Butt joint:** edge-to-edge, no overlap, may need bevel for thickness > 3 mm
- ✅ **Lap joint:** overlap = 2–3× thickness, two fillet welds on either side of top sheet

### 🎯 Examiner's Favorite Questions
- ✅ Difference between arc and gas welding (always asked!)
- ✅ Explain three types of flames
- ✅ Sketch the welding setup (with proper labels)
- ✅ Calculate heat input or weld strength
- ✅ List 5 weld defects with causes
- ✅ Safety precautions (often 2–3 marks)

### 📚 Key Standards
- ✅ **AWS D1.1** — Structural Welding Code (Steel)
- ✅ **IS 816** — Code of Practice for Use of Metal Arc Welding
- ✅ **IS 2062** — M.S. for general structural purposes
- ✅ **AWS A5.1** — Carbon Steel Electrode Specification

---

Preparation of Program and Exercise on CNC Milling Machine Including Simulation: Surface Milling and Pocket Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Program and Exercise on CNC Milling Machine Including Simulation: Surface Milling and Pocket Milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is CNC Milling?
**Computer Numerical Control (CNC) Milling** is a subtractive manufacturing process where a rotary multi-point cutting tool (end mill, face mill, etc.) is precisely controlled by a computer to remove material from a workpiece, generating custom-designed prismatic geometries with high accuracy and repeatability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** A CNC milling machine is a 3-axis (or multi-axis) programmable machine tool that uses coordinated linear and rotational motion along the X, Y, and Z axes to perform milling operations such as drilling, pocketing, contouring, and surfacing under automated computer control via standardized G-code (ISO 6983) and M-code instructions.

### 1.2 Concept of Surface Milling
**Surface milling** (also called face milling) is the machining of a flat horizontal surface on top of a workpiece using a multi-tooth milling cutter. The cutter's axis is generally perpendicular to the machined surface, and the machining is performed by feeding the workpiece against the rotating cutter teeth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To produce a flat, smooth, dimensionally accurate reference surface — typically used as a datum for subsequent machining operations.

### 1.3 Concept of Pocket Milling
**Pocket milling** is the process of removing material from an enclosed (or semi-enclosed) region inside a workpiece to create a recessed cavity of defined depth and boundary profile. A pocket is bounded by an outer contour and has a uniform (or stepped) bottom surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To create recesses for component fitment, weight reduction, mating of mating parts (e.g., gear housing pockets, valve seats), and chip clearance channels.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy
Think of CNC milling like a **digital sculptor with a high-speed electric grinder**:
- The **G-code program** is your "instruction script" — like a recipe listing every move.
- The **workpiece** is your block of marble.
- The **end mill / face mill** is the sculptor's chisel, spinning at thousands of RPM.
- The **CNC controller** is the brain translating the script into precise linear/rotational motion.

For **surface milling**, imagine *shaving the top of a cake to make it perfectly flat* — the cutter sweeps across in straight, parallel passes.

For **pocket milling**, imagine *scooping out the inside of a bread loaf to make a hollow* — but the scoop traces the boundary, then spirals inward.

### 1.5 Key Physical & Operational Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | **rev/min (rpm)** |
| Feed Rate | $f$ | **mm/min** |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | **mm/tooth** |
| Depth of Cut (radial) | $a_e$ | **mm** |
| Depth of Cut (axial) | $a_p$ | **mm** |
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | **m/min** |
| Cutter Diameter | $D$ | **mm** |
| Number of Teeth | $Z$ | — |
| Material Removal Rate | $MRR$ | **mm³/min** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CNC Milling Machine Coordinate System (ISO 841)
Every CNC milling program is written with respect to the **right-hand rule Cartesian coordinate system**:

- **X-axis**: Longest travel, parallel to spindle axis projection on the worktable.
- **Y-axis**: Perpendicular to X, also horizontal.
- **Z-axis**: Parallel to the spindle axis (vertical for vertical milling machines).
- **Origin (X0, Y0, Z0)**: Usually set at the **top-left corner** of the workpiece in the XY plane, and at the **top surface** along Z.

### 2.2 Surface Milling — Theoretical Background
In surface (face) milling, the cutter is mounted with its axis perpendicular to the surface. Machining is performed by:
1. Mounting the workpiece on the machine table.
2. Spinning the face mill at the calculated spindle speed.
3. Feeding the workpiece (or tool) laterally across the surface in **parallel straight-line passes**.
4. Stepping over by a distance less than the cutter diameter (typically 60–80% of $D$) for the next pass.

**Types of Surface Milling:**
- **Peripheral milling** — cutter axis parallel to surface; cutting done by tooth periphery.
- **Face milling** — cutter axis perpendicular to surface; cutting done by cutter face.

### 2.3 Pocket Milling — Theoretical Background
Pocket milling follows a more complex tool path:

1. **Plunge entry** — tool descends vertically into the workpiece at a safe ramped angle (helical or ramp entry preferred; direct plunge only for drill-bit-style tools).
2. **Roughing pass** — interior material is removed in spiraling or zigzag patterns leaving a finish allowance (typically 0.2–0.5 mm on walls).
3. **Side wall finishing pass** — final contour cut to clean walls to dimension.
4. **Floor finishing pass** — light pass at final depth to clean the bottom.

**Common Pocket Tool-Path Strategies:**
- **Linear zigzag (raster)**
- **Spiral (trochoidal)**
- **Contour-parallel (offset)**
- **Plunge + step**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU viva and theory questions.**

| Formula | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Required spindle rpm calculation |
| Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Solving for $N$ when $V_c$ is given |
| Feed Rate | $V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ | Linear table feed in mm/min |
| Feed per Rev | $f_n = f_z \cdot Z$ | Feed per spindle revolution |
| Material Removal Rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | Productivity metric (mm³/min) |
| Stepover Distance | $S_o = k \cdot D$ | $k \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ for roughing |
| Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ | For straight passes |
| Total Passes | $n_p = \left\lceil \dfrac{W - D}{S_o} \right\rceil + 1$ | $W$ = width to be milled |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** $D$ is in **mm**, $N$ in **rpm**, $V_c$ in **m/min**, $V_f$ in **mm/min**. The factor of 1000 in the cutting speed formula converts mm/min to m/min.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Aerospace**: Pocket-milled wing ribs and surface-milled skin panels for weight reduction.
- **Automotive**: Engine block deck surface milling for head gasket sealing.
- **Die & Mould**: Pocket milling for cavities, surface milling for die sets.
- **Medical Implants**: Pocket features in titanium orthopedic plates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

Starting from the definition of cutting speed as the tangential surface velocity at the cutter periphery:

$$V_c = \frac{\text{Circumference swept per rev}}{\text{Time per rev}} = \frac{\pi \cdot D}{60/N} = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

Since $V_c$ is conventionally given in **m/min** and $D$ in **mm**, divide by 1000 to convert mm to m:

$$V_c \,[\text{m/min}] = \frac{\pi \cdot D \,[\text{mm}] \cdot N \,[\text{rpm}]}{1000}$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$\boxed{N \,[\text{rpm}] = \frac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Surface Milling

**Problem Statement:** Surface mill a mild steel workpiece (block 200 mm × 100 mm × 30 mm) using a **60 mm diameter, 4-tooth HSS face mill**. Take cutting speed $V_c = 30$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth, depth of cut $a_p = 2$ mm.

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed**

$$N = \frac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{30 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 60} = \frac{30000}{188.496}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 159.15 \approx 160 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Feed Rate**

$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.15 \cdot 4 \cdot 160$$

$$\boxed{V_f = 96 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate**

$$MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$$

For full-width face milling, $a_e = D = 60$ mm:

$$MRR = 2 \cdot 60 \cdot 96$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 11520 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 11.52 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time**

Total table travel length $L = 200$ mm (plus approach & overtravel allowance 10 mm each side):

$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{200}{96} \approx 2.08 \text{ min}$$

### 3.3 Step-by-Step G-Code Program — Surface Milling

The following is a complete **manual G-code program** for surface milling a rectangular pocket-region on top of a workpiece using a **12 mm diameter, 4-flute end mill**.

```gcode
%
O0001 (SURFACE MILLING PROGRAM - KTU LAB)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G40 G80        (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min, XY plane, Cutter comp off, Canned cycles off)
G54                             (Select Work Coordinate System 1)

(SAFETY INITIAL POSITION)
G00 Z25.0                       (Rapid to safe Z height)
G00 X-15.0 Y-15.0              (Rapid to start position)

(TOOL CALL - 12MM END MILL, TOOL NO. 01)
T01 M06                         (Tool change)
M03 S1500                       (Spindle ON CW at 1500 rpm)

(COOLANT ON)
M08

(STEP 1: PLUNGE TO CUTTING DEPTH)
G00 Z5.0
G01 Z-2.0 F60                   (Plunge to depth 2 mm at 60 mm/min)

(STEP 2: FIRST SURFACE MILLING PASS - X-DIRECTION)
G01 X115.0 F300                 (Cut along +X at 300 mm/min)
G00 Z5.0                        (Retract to safe Z)
G00 Y0.0                        (Move to next Y line)
G01 Z-2.0 F60                   (Plunge again)
G01 X-15.0 F300                 (Return cut along -X)
G00 Z5.0

(STEP 3: STEPOVER AND FINAL PASSES)
G00 Y15.0
G01 Z-2.0 F60
G01 X115.0 F300
G00 Z5.0
G00 Y30.0
G01 Z-2.0 F60
G01 X-15.0 F300
G00 Z5.0
G00 Y45.0
G01 Z-2.0 F60
G01 X115.0 F300

(TOOL RETRACT AND PROGRAM END)
G00 Z25.0
M09                             (Coolant OFF)
M05                             (Spindle OFF)
G91 G28 Z0.0                    (Return to Z home in relative mode)
G90
M30                             (Program end, rewind)
%
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Program Logic Trace:**
> 1. Initialization — set units (G21), absolute mode (G90), feed mode (G94), plane (G17).
> 2. Tool change to T01 (12 mm end mill).
> 3. Spindle ON (M03) at calculated speed.
> 4. Plunge to cutting depth using G01 (linear move at controlled feed).
> 5. Surface passes in alternating directions.
> 6. After each pass, rapid retract (G00 Z) to safe Z for repositioning.
> 7. Repeat with Y-stepover of 15 mm (≈ 80% of cutter diameter).
> 8. Spindle OFF (M05), coolant OFF (M09), home return (G28), program end (M30).

### 3.4 Step-by-Step G-Code Program — Pocket Milling

Pocket to be machined: **100 mm × 80 mm rectangle, 5 mm deep**, at center (X-50, Y-40) on the workpiece top, using a **10 mm diameter 4-flute end mill**.

```gcode
%
O0002 (RECTANGULAR POCKET MILLING PROGRAM - KTU LAB)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G40 G80
G54

(SAFE START POSITION)
G00 Z25.0
G00 X-55.0 Y-45.0

(TOOL CHANGE - 10MM END MILL)
T01 M06
M03 S2000
M08

(STEP 1: HELICAL RAMP ENTRY AT POCKET CORNER -50,-40)
G00 X-50.0 Y-40.0
G01 Z-1.0 F100                  (Ramp down 1 mm deep at center)

(STEP 2: ROUGHING PASSES - SPIRAL PATTERN)
G01 X-40.0 F250                 (Move right 10 mm)
G01 Y-30.0                      (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-50.0                      (Move left 20 mm)
G01 Y-20.0                      (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-30.0                      (Move right 20 mm)
G01 Y-10.0                      (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-50.0                      (Move left 20 mm)
G01 Y0.0                        (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-30.0                      (Move right 20 mm)
G01 Y10.0                       (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-50.0                      (Move left 20 mm)
G01 Y20.0                       (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-30.0                      (Move right 20 mm)
G01 Y30.0                       (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-50.0                      (Move left 20 mm)
G01 Y40.0                       (Move up 10 mm)
G01 X-40.0                      (Move right 10 mm)

(STEP 3: PLUNGE TO FULL DEPTH)
G00 Z5.0
G01 Z-5.0 F80

(STEP 4: FINAL CONTOUR FINISH PASS)
G01 X-45.0 F200                 (Step in 5 mm from boundary)
G01 Y-35.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y35.0
G01 X-45.0
G01 Y-35.0

(STEP 5: FLOOR CLEANING PASS)
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y-30.0
G01 X35.0
G01 Y30.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y-30.0

(RETREAT AND END)
G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G91 G28 Z0.0
G90
M30
%
```

### 3.5 Simulation Workflow (KTU Lab Standard)

| Step | Action | Software/Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Write/edit G-code in text editor or CAM | Notepad / MasterCAM / Fusion 360 |
| 2 | Load program into CNC simulator | CNC Simulator Pro / Vericut / NCSIM |
| 3 | Set work coordinate system & tool table | Simulator GUI |
| 4 | Run **dry run** (no spindle) with **graphical back-plot** | Verify tool path geometry |
| 5 | Check **air-cut height** — tool clears clamps and stock | Adjust safe Z if needed |
| 6 | Run **step-through mode** — single block execution | Verify each line's motion |
| 7 | Identify collisions, over-travels, feed errors | Modify program |
| 8 | Run **full simulation** with stock material removal animation | Confirm final geometry |
| 9 | Approve program → load onto physical CNC | Save program file (.nc / .txt) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNC Milling Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Engineering Drawing] --> B[Identify Operation: Surface or Pocket Milling]
    B --> C[Select Workpiece Material and Blank Dimensions]
    C --> D[Choose Cutter Type: Face Mill or End Mill]
    D --> E[Compute Cutting Parameters: Vc, fz, ap, ae]
    E --> F[Compute Spindle Speed N and Feed Rate Vf]
    F --> G[Define Work Coordinate System G54]
    G --> H[Write G-Code Program Line by Line]
    H --> I[Load Program into CNC Simulator]
    I --> J[Run Dry Run: Graphical Back-Plot]
    J --> K{Collision or Path Error?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Modify Program]
    L --> I
    K -- No --> M[Run Step-Through Verification]
    M --> N{Dimensions and Depths Correct?}
    N -- No --> L
    N -- Yes --> O[Approve Program]
    O --> P[Transfer Program to Physical CNC Machine]
    P --> Q[Set Tool and Work Offsets on Machine]
    Q --> R[Run Machining Cycle]
    R --> S[Inspect Finished Part with Vernier or CMM]
    S --> T{Within Tolerance?}
    T -- No --> U[Adjust Tool Offset or Parameters]
    U --> R
    T -- Yes --> V[End: Part Accepted]
```

### 4.2 Surface Milling Tool Path Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WORKPIECE[Workpiece Top View 200mm x 100mm]
    P1[Start Position: X-15 Y-15] -->|Pass 1 Cut +X| P2[End: X115 Y-15]
    P2 -->|Rapid Retract Z then Step Y+15| P3[Start: X-15 Y0]
    P3 -->|Pass 2 Cut -X| P4[End: X115 Y0]
    P4 -->|Rapid Step Y+15| P5[Start: X-15 Y15]
    P5 -->|Pass 3 Cut +X| P6[End: X115 Y15]
    P6 -->|Rapid Step Y+15| P7[Start: X-15 Y30]
    P7 -->|Pass 4 Cut -X| P8[End: X115 Y30]
    P8 -->|Rapid Step Y+15| P9[Start: X-15 Y45]
    P9 -->|Pass 5 Cut +X| P10[End: X115 Y45]
    P10 -->|Rapid to Safe Z| END[Program End M30]
    end
```

### 4.3 Pocket Milling Tool Path — Spiral Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Start Position Outside Pocket] --> EN[Entry: Helical Ramp or Plunge to Depth Z-5]
    EN --> R1[Roughing Pass 1: 10mm Inside Boundary]
    R1 --> R2[Roughing Pass 2: Step Inward 8mm]
    R2 --> R3[Roughing Pass 3: Continue Spiral Inward]
    R3 --> R4[Continue Spiral Until Center]
    R4 --> SW[Switch to Contour-Parallel Offset]
    SW --> FW[Wall Finish Pass: 0.5mm Outside Final Wall]
    FW --> FF[Floor Finish Pass: Light Cut at Final Z]
    FF --> RT[Rapid Retract to Safe Z]
    RT --> END2[Program End M30]
```

### 4.4 CNC Machine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Controller Subsystem]
    PGM[Program Input: G-Code File] --> INT[Interpreter Module]
    INT --> INT2[Interpolator: Linear and Circular]
    INT2 --> AXC[Axis Control Logic]
    end

    subgraph MECH[Mechanical Subsystem]
    AXC --> XV[X-Axis Servo and Ball Screw]
    AXC --> YV[Y-Axis Servo and Ball Screw]
    AXC --> ZV[Z-Axis Servo and Ball Screw]
    AXC --> SP[Spindle Drive Motor]
    XV --> TB[Machine Table]
    YV --> TB
    ZV --> TB
    SP --> TL[Tool Holder and Cutter]
    end

    subgraph MON[Monitoring Subsystem]
    FB1[Position Feedback Encoders] --> AXC
    FB2[Spindle Speed Sensor] --> AXC
    OVS[Over-travel Limit Switches] --> AXC
    CL[Coolant Pump and Valve] --> AXC
    end

    subgraph HMI[Operator Interface]
    HMI1[Control Panel: MDI Mode] --> PGM
    HMI2[Display: Tool Path Graphics] --> INT
    HMI3[Emergency Stop Button] --> AXC
    end
```

### 4.5 Decision Matrix: Surface vs Pocket Milling

| Feature | Surface Milling | Pocket Milling |
|---|---|---|
| Tool Used | Face mill (multi-tooth) | End mill (2/4 flute) |
| Operation Type | Open boundary | Enclosed cavity |
| Entry Method | Side entry from air | Plunge or helical ramp |
| Typical Cutter Ø | 50–200 mm | 3–20 mm |
| Step Pattern | Linear parallel passes | Spiral or zigzag interior |
| Main Challenge | Flatness and parallelism | Tool deflection in deep cuts |
| Typical Tolerance | ±0.05 mm | ±0.02 to ±0.05 mm |
| Surface Finish Ra | 1.6–3.2 µm | 0.8–1.6 µm |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define CNC milling. List any four G-code preparatory functions used in a milling program.**

**Model Answer:**
CNC (Computer Numerical Control) milling is a machining process where a rotating multi-point cutting tool removes material from a workpiece under the automated control of a computer executing a programmed sequence of instructions (G-code and M-code).

Four common G-code preparatory (G) functions:
1. **G21** — Select metric units (mm).
2. **G90** — Absolute coordinate programming mode.
3. **G94** — Feed rate in mm/min.
4. **G17** — Select XY plane for circular interpolation.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is pocket milling? How does it differ from surface milling?**

**Model Answer:**
Pocket milling is a CNC milling operation in which material is removed from an enclosed or semi-enclosed region inside a workpiece to create a recessed cavity of specified depth and boundary geometry.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Surface Milling | Pocket Milling |
|---|---|---|
| Region | Open flat surface | Enclosed cavity |
| Tool | Face mill | End mill |
| Entry | From the side | Vertical plunge/ramp |
| Path | Parallel passes | Spiral/zigzag interior |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a CNC vertical milling machine. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For surface milling a mild steel workpiece (250 mm × 150 mm × 25 mm) with a 50 mm diameter 6-tooth face mill, given cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.12$ mm/tooth, and depth of cut $a_p = 1.5$ mm, calculate: (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, and (iv) machining time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 Marks):**
A CNC vertical milling machine operates with the spindle oriented vertically (Z-axis). The workpiece is clamped on a moving table (X and Y axes), and a rotating multi-tooth cutter mounted in the spindle removes material according to programmed instructions.

Key components:
1. **Column & base** — provides rigidity.
2. **Spindle head** — holds and rotates the cutter at programmed RPM.
3. **X-Y table** — moves the workpiece in horizontal plane under cutter.
4. **Z-axis slide** — controls vertical position of spindle.
5. **CNC controller** — interprets G-code and drives servo motors.
6. **Coolant system** — extends tool life and improves finish.
7. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** — swaps tools based on program.

The controller receives G-code, interpolates motion, and commands servo motors to position the axes precisely while coordinating spindle speed and feed.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Spindle Speed:**
$$N = \frac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{25 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 50} = \frac{25000}{157.08}$$
$$\boxed{N \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm} \approx 160 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**
$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N = 0.12 \cdot 6 \cdot 160$$
$$\boxed{V_f = 115.2 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate (full-width face milling, $a_e = 50$ mm):**
$$MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f = 1.5 \cdot 50 \cdot 115.2$$
$$\boxed{MRR = 8640 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iv) Machining Time:**
Single-pass length $L = 250$ mm:
$$T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{250}{115.2} \approx 2.17 \text{ min}$$
$$\boxed{T_m \approx 2.17 \text{ min}}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain any two pocket milling tool-path strategies with sketches. Write the G-code for milling a 80 mm × 60 mm rectangular pocket of depth 4 mm using a 10 mm end mill. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of simulation in CNC milling. List any four common errors detected during CNC simulation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pocket Tool-Path Strategies and G-code (7 Marks):**

**Strategy 1 — Zigzag (Linear Raster):** The tool moves back and forth in parallel lines, stepping over by ~60–80% of the cutter diameter. Simple to program but leaves cusps at turn-arounds.

**Strategy 2 — Spiral (Trochoidal):** The tool follows an inward/outward spiral from boundary to center. Produces uniform chip load and reduces tool wear; more complex to program.

**Sample G-Code for 80×60 mm Pocket, 4 mm deep:**

```gcode
%
O0100 (POCKET 80x60 DEPTH 4mm)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G40 G80
G54
G00 Z25.0
G00 X-45.0 Y-35.0
T01 M06                       (10 mm end mill)
M03 S1800
M08

(POCKET ENTRY - HELICAL RAMP)
G01 Z-1.0 F100
G01 X-35.0 Y-25.0 F250
G01 X-30.0 Y-20.0
G01 X-30.0 Y20.0
G01 X-30.0 Y25.0
G01 X30.0
G01 X35.0 Y30.0
G01 X35.0 Y-30.0
G01 X35.0 Y-25.0

(PLUNGE TO FULL DEPTH)
G00 Z5.0
G01 Z-4.0 F80

(CONTOUR FINISH PASS)
G01 X30.0 Y-20.0 F200
G01 X30.0 Y20.0
G01 X-30.0
G01 Y-20.0
G01 X30.0

(FLOOR FINISH)
G01 X25.0 Y-15.0
G01 X25.0 Y15.0
G01 X-25.0
G01 Y-15.0
G01 X25.0

G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G91 G28 Z0.0
G90
M30
%
```

**(b) Role of Simulation & Common Errors (7 Marks):**

**Role of Simulation:**
1. **Verifies tool path** before running on the expensive real machine.
2. **Detects collisions** between tool, workpiece, fixtures, and machine components.
3. **Estimates machining time** accurately.
4. **Validates G-code syntax** and program logic.
5. **Prevents scrap and tool damage**, ensuring operator safety.

**Four Common Errors Detected During Simulation:**
1. **Tool–workpiece collision** — incorrect Z-depth causing cutter to plunge into clamps or machine bed.
2. **Over-travel / limit violation** — commanded axis position exceeds machine travel range.
3. **Incorrect feed mode** — using G98/G99 incorrectly causing excessive tool dwell.
4. **Wrong tool selected** — calling T02 when T01 is mounted, causing crash.
5. **Excessive rapid motion** — rapid (G00) into the workpiece causing gouge.
6. **Improper coolant/g-code mismatch** — M-codes out of sequence causing dry cutting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in the cutting speed formula — $V_c$ is in m/min but $D$ in mm; always multiply by 1000.
> 2. **Mixing G90 and G91 modes** within a single program without proper cancellation — always end with G90.
> 3. **Plunging at full rapid feed** (G00) instead of controlled feed (G01) into the workpiece — this is a guaranteed crash.
> 4. **Not retracting to a safe Z** between passes — the tool gouges when moving laterally.
> 5. **Omitting M08/M09 or M03/M05 pairs** — causes dry cutting or unsafe operation.
> 6. **Wrong work offset** (G54 instead of G55, etc.) — entire part offset.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ CNC milling uses **G-code (ISO 6983)** for motion commands and **M-code** for miscellaneous functions (spindle, coolant, tool change).
- ☐ **Surface milling** uses a face mill; **pocket milling** uses an end mill with plunge/ramp entry.
- ☐ Master formulas: $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$, $V_f = f_z Z N$, $MRR = a_p a_e V_f$.
- ☐ Always specify **safe Z height** (typically 5–25 mm above workpiece) before any rapid motion.
- ☐ Use **G01 (controlled feed)** for any Z-axis plunge — never G00.
- ☐ Stepover for surface milling = **60–80% of cutter diameter**.
- ☐ Pocket entry methods: **helical ramp** (best) > **ramp entry** > **direct plunge** (drill-style).
- ☐ Always run **dry-run simulation** before physical machining.
- ☐ Standard safety codes: **M03** (spindle CW), **M05** (spindle OFF), **M08** (coolant ON), **M09** (coolant OFF), **M30** (program end).
- ☐ Tool offset registers: **G54–G59** for multiple work coordinate systems.
- ☐ End of program sequence: **G00 Z(safe) → M09 → M05 → G28 Z0 → M30**.
- ☐ ISO coordinate convention: **right-hand rule**, Z up, X longest travel, origin at workpiece reference corner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of CAM Software for Generation of CNC Code from CAD Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Use of CAM Software for Generation of CNC Code from CAD Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)** is the use of computer software to convert digital design data (originating from a **CAD model**) into detailed manufacturing instructions — most commonly **G-code and M-code** — that drive Computer Numerical Control (CNC) machine tools. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme lab framework for PCMRL408, *Module Experiment 2* focuses on the complete digital thread that links a 3D CAD geometry to a verified, machine-ready CNC program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *CAM software is the digital intermediary that interprets CAD geometry, applies manufacturing logic (tool selection, feed/speed, tool-path strategy), simulates material removal, and finally emits a post-processed NC file executable on a specific CNC controller (e.g., FANUC, Siemens SINUMERIK, Heidenhain).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Robot Chef" Intuition

Imagine you want a robot chef to bake a cake. You don't hand it a picture — you give it a recipe.

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Picture of cake** | Photo / sketch | **CAD model** (`.STEP`, `.IGES`, `.STL`) |
| **Recipe** | Step-by-step cooking instructions | **CAM tool-path & NC code** |
| **Robot chef** | Human baker following recipe | **CNC machine** executing G-code |

The **CAM software acts as the translator** — converting a *visual description* (the 3D model) into a *procedural command sequence* (the CNC program).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of CAM in the KTU Lab:**
> 1. **Geometry Import** — accepting the CAD part file.
> 2. **Process Planning** — selecting tools, speeds, feeds, strategies.
> 3. **Code Generation (Post-Processing)** — emitting machine-specific G-code.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following constants are routinely used during CAM setup (standard metric units apply):

- **π ≈ 3.14159** (circular constant).
- **1 inch = 25.4 mm** (unit conversion for legacy tooling data).
- **Standard cutting speeds (V)** are tabulated per material in **m/min**.
- **Feed per tooth (fz)** is tabulated per tool-material pair in **mm/tooth**.
- **Machine controller buffer**: typical industrial controllers run a **look-ahead of 1–200 blocks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CAM Digital Thread — Operational Logic Steps

The transformation of CAD data into CNC code follows a strict, auditable sequence. Each step is independently examinable in the KTU lab viva.

### Step 1 — CAD Model Import
- The part geometry (typically a `.STEP` AP203/AP214 file) is imported.
- The CAM engine interprets **B-Rep (Boundary Representation)** surfaces — trimmed NURBS patches, edges, vertices.
- A **stock definition** (raw material block/cylinder) is created to enclose the part envelope.

### Step 2 — Work Coordinate System (WCS) & Origin Selection
- The programmer defines a **Workpiece Zero (G54–G59)**, usually at the top-left corner of the stock or at the center of the top face.
- This origin is what every subsequent tool-path coordinate will reference.

### Step 3 — Machine Simulation Setup
- Tool library is defined: tool number, diameter, flute count, length, holder type.
- Spindle, axis limits, and rapid-traverse constraints are entered (e.g., **G0 rapid = 8000 mm/min**).

### Step 4 — Operation / Tool-Path Generation
The CAM kernel computes motion using strategies such as:
- **Facing** (clearing the top surface).
- **Pocketing** (clearing enclosed cavities — trochoidal, adaptive, or zigzag).
- **Contouring / Profiling** (machining outer walls).
- **Drilling** (G81/G82/G83 canned cycles).
- **3D Finishing** (parallel, scallop, pencil, flowline, Z-level).

### Step 5 — Material Removal Simulation
The CAM software voxelizes the stock and subtracts the swept tool volume. **Stock remaining** vs. **final part** is color-mapped to detect:
- Gouging (tool cuts into finished geometry).
- Collisions (tool holder hits fixture).
- Un-machined material (under-cut regions).

### Step 6 — Post-Processing
A **post-processor** (machine-specific translator) converts the generic **CL-DATA (Cutter Location Data)** into the dialect understood by the target CNC controller (FANUC G-code, Siemens SinuTrain, Heidenhain Klartext, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The post-processor is the *single most distinguishing element* between two otherwise identical CAM projects. A code without a correct post-processor will fail on the machine even if the tool-path is geometrically perfect.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** required for KTU lab calculations related to CAM-generated CNC code. Master these — they reappear in nearly every CNC-oriented numerical problem.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notation Detail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = cutter diameter (mm) |
| 2 | Feed Rate (mm/min) | $V_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ | mm/min | $f_z$ = feed/tooth (mm), $Z$ = number of flutes |
| 3 | Feed Rate (mm/rev) | $f = V_f / N$ | mm/rev | equivalent linear feed |
| 4 | Material Removal Rate | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = axial depth, $a_e$ = radial depth |
| 5 | Cutting Power | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 6 | Machining Time | $T_c = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ | min | $L$ = effective tool-path length (mm) |
| 7 | Number of Passes | $n = \dfrac{a_{p,\text{total}}}{a_p}$ | — | for roughing strategy |
| 8 | Tool Diameter Compensation Offset | $\delta = D/2$ | mm | used by G41/G42 cutter comp |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering, the CAD-to-CNC pipeline is the backbone of:
- **Aerospace structural components** (5-axis simultaneous milling of monolithic bulkheads).
- **Automotive die & mould making** (high-speed finishing of hardened tool-steel punches).
- **Medical implant manufacturing** (titanium knee / hip prosthesis from CT-scan-derived CAD).
- **Defence and rapid prototyping** (DRDO and HAL supply chains).

The integration of CAM eliminates manual NC programming, reducing **programming time by ~70%** and **human transcription errors to near-zero** — provided the post-processor is rigorously validated.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spindle Speed from Cutting Velocity

The cutting speed $V$ (in m/min) is the surface speed of the cutter periphery. For a cutter of diameter $D$ (in mm) rotating at $N$ rpm, the peripheral velocity is obtained by equating the circumference swept per minute.

### Starting relationship — peripheral distance per revolution

$$
\text{Distance per rev} = \pi \cdot D \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

### Distance per minute (multiplying by N)

$$
\text{Distance per min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

### Convert mm/min to m/min (divide by 1000)

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad [\text{m/min}]
$$

### Solving explicitly for N

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad [\text{rpm}]
$$

**Logic check:** If $V$ increases (harder material, higher recommended cutting speed) and $D$ is fixed, $N$ rises — consistent with physical intuition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feed Rate and Feed-per-Tooth Relationship

A milling cutter with $Z$ flutes removes one chip per flute per revolution. Each flute advances by the feed-per-tooth $f_z$ (mm) per revolution.

### Feed per revolution (sum over all Z teeth)

$$
f = f_z \cdot Z \quad [\text{mm/rev}]
$$

### Convert to feed per minute (multiply by spindle speed N)

$$
V_f = f \cdot N = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact formula** the CAM software uses internally to populate the `F` word in the emitted G-code (e.g., `F1200` means $V_f = 1200$ mm/min).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Machining Time from Tool-Path Length

For a single linear tool-path of length $L$ mm executed at feed rate $V_f$ mm/min:

$$
T_c = \dfrac{L}{V_f} \quad [\text{min}]
$$

For a multi-pass roughing operation with $n$ passes over length $L$ each, plus a return rapid:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{n \cdot L}{V_f} + \dfrac{n \cdot L_r}{V_{\text{rapid}}} \quad [\text{min}]
$$

where $L_r$ is the return (rapid) length and $V_{\text{rapid}}$ is the G0 traverse rate (typically **8000–12000 mm/min** on industrial VMCs).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cutter Diameter Compensation (Cutter Comp)

When the CAM software outputs a tool-path offset from the part edge by the cutter radius $R = D/2$, the controller must dynamically shift the programmed path using **G41 (left)** or **G42 (right)** cutter compensation.

### Programmed contour offset distance

$$
\delta = \dfrac{D}{2} \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

The controller reads $\delta$ from the offset register (e.g., `D01` in FANUC) and applies it perpendicular to the programmed path direction. The lead-in distance required for compensation activation is typically:

$$
L_{\text{lead-in}} \geq \delta \quad [\text{mm}]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** When you generate code in CAM (e.g., Mastercam, Fusion 360, NX), the *Geometric Engine* calculates the offset path; the *Post-Processor* then automatically inserts the G41/G42 line and the correct lead-in motion. This is why CAM-generated code is far less error-prone than hand-written NC.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — A KTU-Style Calculation

**Given:**
- Material: Mild Steel (recommended $V = 30$ m/min).
- Cutter: Ø10 mm, 4-flute HSS end-mill ($f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth).
- Axial depth of cut $a_p = 2$ mm, radial depth $a_e = 5$ mm.
- Effective tool-path length $L = 240$ mm.

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 10} = \dfrac{30000}{31.4159} \approx 955 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed Rate:**

$$
V_f = 955 \times 0.05 \times 4 = 191 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = 2 \times 5 \times 191 = 1910 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time:**

$$
T_c = \dfrac{240}{191} \approx 1.257 \text{ min} \approx 75.4 \text{ sec}
$$

This entire calculation is performed by the CAM software during operation setup — but a KTU examiner will expect you to derive these manually from the *parameters you fed into the CAM system*.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CAD–CAM–CNC Digital Thread (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

## 4.2 CAM Software Internal Module Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    moduleB["Geometry Kernel ACIS / Parasolid / CGM"]:::kernelMod
    moduleC["Stock Definition Block Cylinder Tube"]:::stockMod
    moduleD["Tool Manager Library Database"]:::toolMod
    moduleE["Operation Manager 2.5D / 3D / 5 Axis"]:::opMod
    moduleF["Tool Path Generator CL Data Engine"]:::tpMod
    moduleG["Simulator Voxel Subtraction Engine"]:::simMod
    moduleH["Post Processor Machine Specific Dialect"]:::postMod
    moduleI["Output NC File G Code M Code"]:::outMod

    moduleF --> moduleG
    moduleG --> moduleH
    moduleH --> moduleI

    classDef kernelMod fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#0d3a36,color:#ffffff

    classDef tpMod fill:#8338ec,stroke:#3a1772,color:#ffffff
    classDef simMod fill:#3a86ff,stroke:#173d77,color:#ffffff
    classDef postMod fill:#ff006e,stroke:#7a0036,color:#ffffff
    classDef outMod fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#037a5b,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Typical G-Code Block Emitted by CAM (Annotated)

```
%
O0001 (PROGRAM ID - PCMRL408 EXP2)
G21 G90 G94 (MM, ABSOLUTE, FEED PER MIN)
G54 (WORK COORDINATE ZERO)
T01 M06 (TOOL 1 - END MILL 10MM)
S955 M03 (SPINDLE CW AT 955 RPM)
G00 X5.0 Y5.0 Z25.0 (RAPID TO SAFE POSITION)
G01 Z-2.0 F191 (PLUNGE INTO CUT AT 191 MM/MIN)
G01 X95.0 (LINEAR CUT)
G00 Z25.0 (RETRACT)
M05 (SPINDLE STOP)
M30 (PROGRAM END)
%
```

> [!NOTE]
> Every line above is **auto-generated by the post-processor**. A student writing this manually would consume ~30 minutes; CAM emits it in under 2 seconds once the tool-path is validated.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define CAM. List any four popular CAM software packages used in mechanical engineering industries.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM) is the use of computer software to convert CAD geometry into machine-readable instructions (G-code/M-code) for CNC machines, including tool-path generation, simulation, and post-processing.
Four popular CAM packages: (1) **Mastercam**, (2) **Autodesk Fusion 360**, (3) **Siemens NX CAM**, (4) **SolidCAM**. *(Any four accepted — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for naming.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a post-processor in CAM software? Why is it considered machine-specific?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A post-processor is a translator module within CAM software that converts generic **Cutter Location (CL) data** into the exact **G-code/M-code dialect** understood by a specific CNC controller (FANUC, Siemens, Heidenhain, Mazak, etc.). It is machine-specific because each controller has unique syntax requirements — e.g., FANUC uses `T01 M06` while Siemens uses `T="ENDMILL10"` — different canned-cycle formats, modal behaviour, and auxiliary codes. *(2 marks concept + 1 mark for syntax example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete workflow of generating CNC code from a CAD model using CAM software. Describe each stage with its engineering purpose. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a milling operation on Aluminium alloy (recommended cutting speed $V = 200$ m/min) using a Ø12 mm, 2-flute carbide end-mill (feed-per-tooth $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth), with axial depth $a_p = 3$ mm and radial depth $a_e = 6$ mm, calculate:
1. Spindle speed $N$ (rpm).
2. Feed rate $V_f$ (mm/min).
3. Material Removal Rate (mm³/min).
4. Machining time for a 360 mm linear tool-path. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **1. CAD Import** | Load `.STEP`/`.IGES` part file (B-Rep surfaces, edges, vertices). |
| **2. Stock & WCS Setup** | Define raw material envelope and Work Coordinate Zero (G54). |
| **3. Tool Library Loading** | Select tool number, diameter, length, flutes, holder, material. |
| **4. Operation Programming** | Choose strategies — facing, pocketing, contour, drilling, 3D finishing. |
| **5. Tool-Path Computation** | CAM kernel generates CL data (cutter location points and vectors). |
| **6. Material Removal Simulation** | Voxel-based verification for gouges, collisions, and un-machined regions. |
| **7. Post-Processing** | CL data is translated into controller-specific G-code/M-code. |
| **8. NC File Transfer** | Program is sent to CNC via DNC / USB / Ethernet. |
| **9. Machining** | CNC executes program to produce finished part. |

*(Marking: 7 stages × 1 mark each = 7 marks; bonus 1 mark for neat diagram if drawn.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 200}{\pi \times 12}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{200000}{37.6991} \approx 5305 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed Rate:**

$$
V_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z = 5305 \times 0.08 \times 2 = 848.8 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f = 3 \times 6 \times 848.8 = 15278.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time:**

$$
T_c = \dfrac{L}{V_f} = \dfrac{360}{848.8} \approx 0.424 \text{ min} \approx 25.5 \text{ sec}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the difference between **2.5D milling** and **3D milling** operations in CAM. Give two real-world part examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CAM-generated tool-path has 180 passes of 250 mm each. Cutting feed $V_f = 600$ mm/min, rapid return rate $V_{\text{rapid}} = 10000$ mm/min, and each return rapid is 250 mm. Calculate the total machining time. Also calculate spindle speed if $V = 25$ m/min and $D = 8$ mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Feature | 2.5D Milling | 3D Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Prismatic parts with flat floors and vertical walls. | Sculpted free-form surfaces (moulds, impellers). |
| **Tool Axis** | Always vertical (Z-axis fixed). | Tool axis tilts (multi-axis possible). |
| **Strategies** | Facing, pocketing, drilling, profiling. | Parallel, scallop, flowline, pencil, Z-level. |
| **Examples** | Engine cover plate, bracket, flange. | Injection mould cavity, turbine blade, prosthetics. |

*(Marking: 4 points of comparison × 1 mark = 4 marks; 2 examples × 1.5 marks = 3 marks.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cutting Time:**

$$
T_{\text{cut}} = \dfrac{180 \times 250}{600} = \dfrac{45000}{600} = 75 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Rapid Return Time:**

$$
T_{\text{rapid}} = \dfrac{180 \times 250}{10000} = \dfrac{45000}{10000} = 4.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Machining Time:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 75 + 4.5 = 79.5 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 19 \text{ min } 30 \text{ sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 8} = \dfrac{25000}{25.1327} \approx 995 \text{ rpm}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in Module 2 Viva & Exam:**
> - **Forgetting units** on every numerical answer (e.g., writing 955 without "rpm" — *lose 0.5 to 1 mark*).
> - **Confusing feed-per-minute vs. feed-per-rev** — examiners specifically test whether you know $V_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$. Wrong formula = **−2 marks**.
> - **Skipping the post-processor explanation** in workflow diagrams — this is the most heavily weighted sub-question in Part (a) and students routinely omit it. *−2 marks typical*.
> - **Not mentioning simulation** when describing CAM — material-removal simulation is what differentiates CAM from raw code generation. *−1 mark*.
> - **Assuming CAM auto-generates perfect code** — examiners want to see awareness that **post-processor mismatch** is the #1 cause of CNC machine alarms in industry.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2:**

- **CAM** bridges **CAD geometry** and **CNC execution** — the digital translator.
- The **CAM workflow** follows: **Import → Stock/WCS → Tool Library → Operations → Tool-path → Simulation → Post-Process → NC File → Machine**.
- **Spindle Speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ — always in **rpm**.
- **Feed Rate:** $V_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ — always in **mm/min**.
- **MRR** = $a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ — used to estimate productivity.
- **Machining Time:** $T_c = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ — sum cutting and rapid times for total cycle time.
- **Cutter Compensation:** G41 (left) and G42 (right), offset value $\delta = D/2$.
- **Post-processor** is *machine-specific*; without the correct post, code will not run.
- **2.5D vs 3D** — 2.5D has vertical tool axis only; 3D allows tilt and 5-axis simultaneous motion.
- Common CAM software: **Mastercam, Fusion 360, Siemens NX, SolidCAM, CATIA, Edgecam**.
- Common file formats: **`.STEP`, `.IGES`, `.STL`, `.PAR` (Parasolid), `.X_T`**.
- Always **simulate before cutting** — gouges, collisions, and under-cuts must be zero.
- **KTU Viva Tip:** Be ready to explain *why* a post-processor is needed, *what* a G-code block contains, and *how* to calculate cutting parameters from first principles.

---

topics.title: Exercises on Rapid Prototyping: 3D Printing of Simple Box, Helix Shape, and U Bracket
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRL408 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Rapid Prototyping: 3D Printing of Simple Box, Helix Shape, and U Bracket

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Rapid Prototyping (RP)** is a class of manufacturing technologies that can automatically construct physical prototypes directly from **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** data sources. According to **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015**, the standardized term for these technologies is **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** — defined as the *"process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing and formative manufacturing methodologies."*

The three test specimens described in this lab exercise are fundamental geometric benchmarks used in any introductory 3D printing lab:

| Specimen | Geometric Nature | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple Box** | Rectangular hollow cuboid (open-top or closed) | Tests plane-layer slicing, wall continuity, infill distribution |
| **Helix Shape** | 3D spatial curve (cylindrical spiral) | Tests curved surfaces, overhangs, bridging, and support generation |
| **U Bracket** | U-shaped channel/clip (functional part) | Tests cantilever strength, bridging, dimensional accuracy, orientation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes *process-chain understanding* (CAD → STL → Slicer → G-code → Print) and the *relationship between slicing parameters and final part quality*. Memorizing the workflow diagram is essential for both Part A and Part B questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Icing a Cake, One Layer at a Time"

Imagine baking a layer cake where each horizontal slice is exactly the cross-section of the final object at that height. Traditional machining is like **sculpting a statue from marble** — you start with a block and remove excess material. **3D Printing / Rapid Prototyping is the exact opposite** — you start with *nothing* and *add* material layer by layer, just like squeezing decorative icing through a nozzle to form any 2D shape, then stacking those shapes vertically to form a 3D object.

The **Simple Box** is analogous to stacking rectangular tiles. The **Helix** is like squeezing a continuous spiral of icing that climbs upward. The **U Bracket** is like stacking tiles with an empty channel in the middle — a real engineering clip you might find holding pipes on a wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards to Remember:**
> - **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** — Standardized AM terminology
> - **STL (Stereolithography)** — De-facto file format (triangulated mesh)
> - **FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling)** — Most common desktop 3D printing process
> - **FFF (Fused Filament Fabrication)** — Open-source equivalent term for FDM

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Rapid Prototyping Process Chain (End-to-End)

The complete workflow from a digital idea to a physical prototype consists of **six sequential stages**. Each stage is a potential failure point in the lab.

### Stage 1 — CAD Modeling
- A 3D model is designed using software such as **SolidWorks, Autodesk Fusion 360, CATIA, Creo, or Open-source FreeCAD**.
- The model is a **boundary representation (B-rep)** — a mathematically perfect solid with parametric features (extrudes, cuts, fillets).

### Stage 2 — Tessellation (CAD → STL)
- CAD models use **smooth parametric surfaces (NURBS)**. The 3D printer, however, can only understand **flat triangles**.
- The model is therefore *tessellated* — converted into thousands (sometimes millions) of small triangles.
- Each triangle stores **three vertices (x, y, z) and a normal vector** (pointing outward to define inside/outside).
- The result is an **STL file** (Stereolithography format, originally by 3D Systems, 1987).
- **Two STL variants exist:** ASCII (human-readable text) and Binary (compact, default).

### Stage 3 — Slicing (STL → G-code)
- The STL mesh is **mathematically intersected with horizontal planes** spaced by the chosen **Layer Height** (typically 0.1, 0.2, or 0.3 mm).
- Each intersection produces a **2D contour** (the cross-section of the part at that height).
- A *slicer software* (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Slic3r, IdeaMaker) generates the **G-code** — a text-based motion program that drives the printer's motors and extruder.
- The slicer also decides: **infill pattern, perimeter count, print speed, retraction, support placement, and temperature**.

### Stage 4 — Printing (G-code → Physical Part)
- For **FDM/FFF printers**: thermoplastic filament (PLA, ABS, PETG) is fed into a **heated nozzle (200–240 °C)**.
- The nozzle deposits a thin bead of molten plastic (typically **0.4 mm wide**) onto a **heated bed (60 °C for PLA)**.
- The print head moves in **X and Y**; the bed moves in **Z** to build the next layer.
- Layer-by-layer, the part emerges.

### Stage 5 — Post-Processing
- Removal of **support structures** (for overhangs > 45°).
- **Bridging cleanup** (removing stringing, oozing).
- Optional **sanding, painting, vapor smoothing, or epoxy coating**.

### Stage 6 — Inspection and Validation
- Dimensional check using **vernier calipers or coordinate measuring machine (CMM)**.
- Visual inspection for **layer adhesion, warping, stringing, and surface finish**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the order strict?** Each stage produces a *file artifact* consumed by the next. You cannot slice a CAD file directly (it lacks triangulated geometry), and you cannot print an STL without first converting it to motion commands (G-code).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Typical Value (PLA, 0.4 mm nozzle) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Layer Height | $h$ | 0.1 – 0.3 | mm |
| 2 | Nozzle Diameter | $d$ | 0.4 | mm |
| 3 | Number of Layers | $N_L = \dfrac{H}{h}$ | 150 – 600 | layers |
| 4 | Filament Cross-sectional Area | $A_f = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | 0.1257 | mm² |
| 5 | Bead / Extrusion Width | $w$ | 0.4 – 0.45 | mm |
| 6 | Extruded Volume per unit length | $V_L = w \cdot h$ | 0.04 – 0.135 | mm³/mm |
| 7 | Part Solid Volume | $V_{solid} = L \cdot W \cdot H$ | variable | mm³ |
| 8 | Effective Infill Volume | $V_{infill} = V_{solid} \cdot f$ | 0 – 100 % | mm³ |
| 9 | Wall Volume | $V_{wall} = P \cdot h \cdot n_{walls} \cdot N_L$ | variable | mm³ |
| 10 | Top + Bottom Volume | $V_{tb} = 2 \cdot A_{footprint} \cdot h \cdot n_{topbot}$ | variable | mm³ |
| 11 | Total Filament Volume | $V_{filament} = \dfrac{V_{infill} + V_{wall} + V_{tb}}{\rho_{pack}}$ | variable | mm³ |
| 12 | Filament Mass | $m = V_{filament} \cdot \rho_{PLA}$ | variable | grams |
| 13 | Density of PLA | $\rho_{PLA}$ | **1.24 × 10⁻³** | g/mm³ |
| 14 | Density of ABS | $\rho_{ABS}$ | **1.04 × 10⁻³** | g/mm³ |
| 15 | Packing Density of Infill | $\rho_{pack}$ | 0.85 – 0.95 (line infill) | – |
| 16 | Print Time (approx) | $t \approx \dfrac{V_{filament}}{Q}$ | variable | seconds |
| 17 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | 1 – 8 | mm³/s |
| 18 | Nozzle Temperature | $T_n$ | 200 – 230 (PLA) | °C |
| 19 | Bed Temperature | $T_b$ | 50 – 70 (PLA) | °C |
| 20 | Print Speed | $v$ | 40 – 80 | mm/s |

**Where:**
- $H$ = total Z-height of part
- $L, W, H$ = part length, width, height
- $P$ = perimeter of part cross-section
- $f$ = infill fraction (0.0 to 1.0)
- $n_{walls}$ = number of perimeter walls (typically 2–4)
- $n_{topbot}$ = number of top + bottom solid layers (typically 4–5)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas allow you to *predict filament consumption and cost* before printing. In industry, a 1 kg spool of PLA costs roughly ₹1,200–₹1,800 (2024 Indian market). A well-tuned slicer avoids failed prints, which is critical in functional prototyping for client presentations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **complete analytical reasoning** for each of the three test specimens — from CAD modeling decisions to slicer parameter selection to post-processing expectations.

## 3.1 Specimen 1 — The Simple Box

### 3.1.1 CAD Modeling Decisions

A Simple Box is the most fundamental open-geometry test. A typical KTU lab spec:

$$
\text{Dimensions} = 50 \text{ mm (L)} \times 50 \text{ mm (W)} \times 30 \text{ mm (H)}
$$

**Decision tree:**

- **Open top vs. closed top?** → For lab simplicity, build an **open-top rectangular shell** (a 5-face box, like a hollowed brick). This exposes the **infill pattern** for visual inspection.
- **Wall thickness** → Set to **1.2 mm** (3 perimeters × 0.4 mm nozzle width). This is the minimum that gives structural rigidity without wasting material.
- **Fillet edges?** → Optional **0.5 mm fillet** at vertical edges reduces stress concentration and improves visual finish (slicers handle fillets as discrete polyline steps).

### 3.1.2 Slicer Parameter Selection

Let us derive the **number of layers** for a chosen layer height:

$$
N_L = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{30 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 150 \text{ layers}
$$

This means the printer deposits **150 individual horizontal slices** to complete the box. If we chose a finer layer height:

$$
N_L = \dfrac{30}{0.1} = 300 \text{ layers} \quad \text{(higher resolution, double the print time)}
$$

**Volume Calculation:**

The hollow interior has inner dimensions $(50 - 2 \cdot 1.2) \times (50 - 2 \cdot 1.2) \times 30 = 47.6 \times 47.6 \times 30$.

$$
V_{outer} = 50 \cdot 50 \cdot 30 = 75{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{inner} = 47.6 \cdot 47.6 \cdot 30 = 67{,}972.8 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{solid, shell} = 75{,}000 - 67{,}972.8 = 7{,}027.2 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Infill Volume** (using 20% infill, applied to the inner hollow region):

$$
V_{infill} = 0.20 \cdot 67{,}972.8 = 13{,}594.56 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Total Filament Volume (PLA):**

$$
V_{filament} = V_{shell} + V_{infill} + V_{topbot} = 7{,}027.2 + 13{,}594.56 + \sim 2{,}400 \text{ (top+bottom)} = 23{,}021.76 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Mass of Filament Used:**

$$
m = V_{filament} \cdot \rho_{PLA} = 23{,}021.76 \cdot 1.24 \times 10^{-3} \approx 28.55 \text{ grams}
$$

This is roughly **3% of a 1 kg spool** — a useful lab metric for cost estimation.

### 3.1.3 Expected Observations (KTU Lab Record)

- **First-layer adhesion:** Should be a uniform, slightly squished line.
- **Wall consistency:** 3 perimeters visible as parallel vertical lines on side faces.
- **Infill pattern:** Visible diagonal or grid pattern on the open top (depends on slicer choice — grid, lines, gyroid, honeycomb, etc.).
- **Layer shifts:** Should not occur if belts are tensioned correctly.

## 3.2 Specimen 2 — The Helix Shape

### 3.2.1 Geometric Definition

A helix in 3D space is described by three parameters:

$$
x(t) = R \cos(t), \quad y(t) = R \sin(t), \quad z(t) = \dfrac{p \cdot t}{2\pi}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = helix radius (e.g., **15 mm**)
- $p$ = pitch (vertical rise per full turn, e.g., **10 mm**)
- $t$ = parametric angle (radians), typically $0 \le t \le 4\pi$ (2 full turns)

**Sample lab parameters:**

$$
R = 15 \text{ mm}, \quad p = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad N_{turns} = 3, \quad \text{filament diameter} = 1.75 \text{ mm}
$$

The total Z-height of the helix is:

$$
H_{helix} = N_{turns} \cdot p = 3 \cdot 10 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.2.2 Slicer Interpretation — Why is the Helix Hard?

Unlike the box, the helix has **no flat horizontal faces**. The slicer must compute a **continuously varying cross-section** for every layer. At any height $z$, the cross-section is a **single point** (if $R <$ nozzle width) or a **thin curved arc** (if $R \ge$ nozzle width).

**Overhang challenge:** The helix is essentially a horizontal coil. Each turn has two **overhang surfaces** — one inner and one outer — that hang in mid-air during printing. This is the **#1 reason for support structures** in FDM.

The **maximum overhang angle** for self-supporting FDM printing is approximately **45° from vertical** (or, equivalently, 45° below horizontal). For a helix of pitch $p$ and circumference $2\pi R$:

$$
\alpha = \arctan\left( \dfrac{p}{2\pi R} \right) = \arctan\left( \dfrac{10}{2 \pi \cdot 15} \right) = \arctan(0.106) \approx 6.06^\circ
$$

This is **far below 45°**, meaning the helix cannot print without supports in standard FDM.

### 3.2.3 Solution: Tree-like or Linear Supports

The slicer automatically generates **tree supports** (organic, branch-like) or **linear supports** (grid of vertical pillars). The student should:

1. Enable supports in the slicer.
2. Set **support overhang angle threshold to 50–55°**.
3. Set **support density to 15%**.
4. Print the helix.
5. Pluck supports away with **flush cutters** during post-processing.

### 3.2.4 Analytical Notes

- **Bridges** (horizontal spans without support) are short for a helix — typically just a few mm.
- The helix tests the printer's **ability to handle curved trajectories** without excessive vibration or ringing.
- Surface finish is often **rougher than the box** because the slicer approximates the curve as a sequence of short line segments (a polyline).

## 3.3 Specimen 3 — The U Bracket

### 3.3.1 Functional Geometry

A U Bracket is a **functional engineering part** — the kind of clip used to hold pipes, cables, or structural members. Sample dimensions:

$$
\text{Overall} = 60 \text{ mm (L)} \times 30 \text{ mm (W)} \times 25 \text{ mm (H)}, \quad \text{Channel width} = 20 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Channel depth} = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

Wall thickness: **2.0 mm** (5 perimeters × 0.4 mm — robust for functional loading).

### 3.3.2 Print Orientation — The Critical Decision

The U Bracket has **three plausible orientations**:

| Orientation | X-Y Footprint | Z Height | Supports Needed? | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **U opening up** (base flat on bed) | $60 \times 30$ | 25 | None | Strongest (layers perpendicular to load) |
| **U opening sideways** | $60 \times 25$ | 30 | Yes (for overhang lips) | Moderate |
| **U opening down** (channel hangs in air) | $60 \times 30$ | 25 | Yes (entire channel is a bridge) | Weakest |

**Recommended:** Print with the **U opening up** (base on bed). The channel walls are **vertical** (perfectly self-supporting), and the flat base adheres to the bed. No supports required.

### 3.3.3 Bridge Length Analysis

If printed with the channel oriented horizontally, the slicer encounters a **bridge** — a horizontal span of molten plastic deposited over open air. The maximum reliable bridge length for a 0.4 mm nozzle is:

$$
L_{bridge, max} \approx 10 \cdot d = 10 \cdot 0.4 = 4 \text{ mm}
$$

For a 20 mm channel, this means supports are mandatory if oriented sideways.

### 3.3.4 Infill Selection for Strength

For a functional bracket that will bear load:

- **Infill = 50%** (or higher) using **gyroid** or **cubic** infill pattern (isotropic strength).
- **Walls: 4 perimeters** (1.6 mm wall).
- **Top + bottom: 5 layers each** (1.0 mm thick top/bottom).

These settings make the printed part behave closer to a solid injection-molded plastic part.

## 3.4 Cross-Specimen Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Simple Box | Helix | U Bracket |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometric complexity | Low | High (continuous curve) | Medium (sharp edges + channel) |
| Supports required | No | **Yes (essential)** | Optional (depends on orientation) |
| Print time (approx, 0.2 mm layer) | 25 – 35 min | 45 – 70 min | 35 – 50 min |
| Filament usage (approx) | 25 – 30 g | 15 – 25 g (mostly supports) | 30 – 45 g |
| Skill tested | Slicing, layer adhesion | Curved surface, support removal | Orientation, bridging, strength |
| Common failure mode | First-layer warping | Support adhesion to part | Layer splitting under load |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    A5 --> A6[Step F: Transfer to 3D Printer via SD or USB]
    A6 --> A7[Step G: Printer Executes G-code Layer by Layer]

    A8 --> A9[Step I: Inspection and Lab Record]

    B1[Subprocess: STL Triangulation] -.-> A2
    B2[Subprocess: Slicing Plane Intersection] -.-> A4

    style A1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing Print Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: Choose Part Orientation] --> Q2{Does part have large flat face?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Place flat face on bed]

    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Print with minimal supports]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6[Rotate to minimize critical overhangs]
    Q6 --> Q7{Is bridge length under 5 mm?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Enable tree supports]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9[Re orient to reduce bridge span]

    Q5 --> R1
    Q8 --> R1
    Q9 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Generate G-code and Print]

    style R2 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Rapid Prototyping. List any four RP processes used in industry.**

**Model Answer:**
Rapid Prototyping (RP) is a manufacturing technique that produces physical prototypes directly from 3D CAD data by **additive layer-by-layer material deposition**, as opposed to subtractive methods. According to ISO/ASTM 52900:2015, this is termed **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**.

Four RP processes commonly used in industry:
1. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** — thermoplastic extrusion
2. **Stereolithography (SLA)** — UV-cured photopolymer resin
3. **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** — laser-fused polymer powder
4. **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** — laser-melted metal powder

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing 4 processes with brief mention.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is an STL file? Why is it needed in 3D printing?**

**Model Answer:**
**STL (Stereolithography)** is a file format that describes a 3D object's surface geometry using a **triangulated mesh** — a collection of small flat triangles that approximate the original curved surfaces.

It is needed because:
- **CAD models use smooth parametric (NURBS) surfaces** that printers cannot directly interpret.
- STL **tessellates the model into flat triangles** with outward-pointing normal vectors, which a slicer can mathematically slice.
- STL is the **universal interchange format** accepted by virtually every slicer and 3D printer.

*(Examiner's note: 1.5 marks for STL definition, 1.5 marks for its role in slicing.)*

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **end-to-end process chain of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** with a neat block diagram. List the major stages and describe what happens at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Simple Box** of dimensions **60 mm × 40 mm × 25 mm** is to be 3D printed with **0.2 mm layer height, 20% infill, 3 perimeter walls, and 4 top + 4 bottom solid layers** using PLA filament. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Total number of layers.
- (ii) Solid shell volume (outer box minus inner hollow).
- (iii) Approximate filament mass in grams.
*(Take: nozzle diameter = 0.4 mm, $\rho_{PLA}$ = 1.24 g/cm³, packing density of infill = 0.9, wall thickness = 3 × 0.4 = 1.2 mm, ignore top/bottom in mass calculation for simplicity.)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The FDM process chain consists of **six sequential stages**:

1. **CAD Modeling** — A 3D model is built in SolidWorks, Fusion 360, or FreeCAD using parametric features.

2. **STL Export** — The smooth CAD model is tessellated into thousands of triangles. The STL file stores each triangle as 3 vertices and a normal vector.

3. **Slicing** — Software (Cura, PrusaSlicer) slices the STL with horizontal planes spaced by the layer height. Each slice becomes a 2D toolpath. Slicer also decides infill, walls, supports, and temperatures.

4. **G-code Generation** — The slicer outputs a G-code file — a text-based list of commands (G0, G1, G28, M104, M140, etc.) that drive the printer's motors and heaters.

5. **Printing** — Filament is fed into a heated nozzle (200–240 °C for PLA). The nozzle moves in X-Y; the bed moves in Z. Each layer is deposited and fuses to the previous layer.

6. **Post-Processing** — Support structures are removed; surface is cleaned; the part is inspected for defects.

*(7 marks = 1 for listing + 1.5 each for 4 stages of detailed explanation. Examiner's tip: drawing the block diagram earns 0.5–1 bonus mark.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Number of Layers:**

$$
N_L = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{25 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 125 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Solid Shell Volume:**

Wall thickness on each side = $1.2 \text{ mm}$, so the inner hollow has dimensions:

$$
(60 - 2 \cdot 1.2) \times (40 - 2 \cdot 1.2) \times 25 = 57.6 \times 37.6 \times 25
$$

$$
V_{outer} = 60 \cdot 40 \cdot 25 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{inner} = 57.6 \cdot 37.6 \cdot 25 = 54{,}144 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V_{shell} = V_{outer} - V_{inner} = 60{,}000 - 54{,}144 = 5{,}856 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**(iii) Filament Mass:**

Infill volume (20% of inner volume):

$$
V_{infill} = 0.20 \cdot 54{,}144 = 10{,}828.8 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Total material volume (shell + effective infill, accounting for packing):

$$
V_{filament} = V_{shell} + \dfrac{V_{infill}}{\rho_{pack}} = 5{,}856 + \dfrac{10{,}828.8}{0.9} = 5{,}856 + 12{,}032 = 17{,}888 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Convert mm³ → cm³ (1 cm³ = 1000 mm³):

$$
V_{filament} = 17.888 \text{ cm}^3
$$

Mass:

$$
m = V_{filament} \cdot \rho_{PLA} = 17.888 \cdot 1.24 \approx 22.18 \text{ grams}
$$

**Final Answer:** **125 layers, shell volume = 5,856 mm³, filament mass ≈ 22.2 grams.**

*(7 marks = 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii). Each step shown in full.)*
