### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]

**(a)** A **Helix Shape** of radius **20 mm, pitch 8 mm, and 4 turns** is to be printed on an FDM printer. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the total Z-height of the helix.
- (ii) Calculate the overhang angle and comment on whether supports are required.
- (iii) Explain what bridging means in 3D printing and how it applies to this helix.

**(b)** Explain the **print orientation strategy** for a **U Bracket** with the U-channel opening on top. Justify why this is the preferred orientation over rotating the part sideways. Mention all key slicing parameters suitable for a functional bracket. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Total Z-Height:**

$$
H_{helix} = N_{turns} \cdot p = 4 \cdot 8 = 32 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Overhang Angle:**

$$
\alpha = \arctan\left( \dfrac{p}{2\pi R} \right) = \arctan\left( \dfrac{8}{2 \pi \cdot 20} \right) = \arctan\left( \dfrac{8}{125.66} \right) = \arctan(0.0637) \approx 3.65^\circ
$$

Since $\alpha \approx 3.65°$ is **far below the 45° self-supporting threshold**, the helix **absolutely requires support structures**. The slicer should be configured to enable tree supports with an overhang threshold of ~50°.

**(iii) Bridging:**

**Bridging** is the FDM process of depositing molten plastic over an **open horizontal gap** (no support material beneath). The molten bead stretches across the gap and hardens in place.

In a helix, the *outer* overhang of each turn creates short bridges (length ≈ filament diameter × several). Since the helix pitch is small (8 mm) and the circumference is large (~125.66 mm), each layer's cross-section is essentially a thin curved arc. **Tree supports** anchor these arcs and are removed after printing. Bridges longer than ~5 mm typically sag; the helix's effective bridge length is small enough to handle well with supports.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Print Orientation Strategy for U Bracket:**

The **U Bracket should be printed with its U-channel opening facing UP** and its flat base resting on the heated bed. Justification:

1. **Maximum bed adhesion** — The largest flat face (60 × 30 mm base) is in direct contact with the bed, preventing warping.
2. **Vertical channel walls are self-supporting** — The two side walls of the U rise vertically (0° overhang), so no support material is needed.
3. **Z-height is minimized** — Only 25 mm tall, reducing print time and improving stability.
4. **Layer lines run perpendicular to the load** — If the bracket is loaded vertically (e.g., pipe weight), layers running horizontally are in tension/shear, giving good strength.

**Why NOT sideways orientation?** — Rotating the U so the channel faces sideways introduces a **20 mm horizontal bridge** (the channel opening becomes a roof to be printed across). 20 mm far exceeds the safe bridging distance of 4 mm, requiring extensive supports and producing a weaker, uglier part.

**Key Slicer Parameters for Functional U Bracket:**

| Parameter | Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Layer Height | **0.2 mm** | Balance of speed and surface quality |
| Perimeters | **4 walls** (1.6 mm thick) | Strength and rigidity |
| Top/Bottom Layers | **5 each** (1.0 mm) | Solid, watertight faces |
| Infill | **50%** | Strength without excessive material |
| Infill Pattern | **Gyroid or Cubic** | Isotropic strength in all directions |
| Print Speed | **50 mm/s** | Slower = better layer adhesion |
| Nozzle Temp | **215 °C** | Standard for PLA |
| Bed Temp | **60 °C** | First-layer adhesion |
| Supports | **None** (channel faces up) | Self-supporting orientation |

*(7 marks = 2 for orientation diagram, 2 for justification, 2 for parameter table, 1 for explanation of strength.)*

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Checklist (Print this before your exam!):**

- **Rapid Prototyping = Additive Manufacturing** (ISO/ASTM 52900:2015). Layer-by-layer, not subtractive.
- **Process chain (memorize order):** CAD → STL → Slicer → G-code → Print → Post-process → Inspect.
- **STL format** = triangulated mesh. Each triangle has 3 vertices + 1 normal. Two variants: ASCII (text) and Binary (compact).
- **FDM** = Fused Deposition Modeling. Open-source synonym: **FFF** (Fused Filament Fabrication).
- **Slicer software:** Cura, PrusaSlicer, Slic3r, IdeaMaker.
- **Typical PLA settings:** Nozzle 200–230 °C, Bed 50–70 °C, Speed 40–80 mm/s, Nozzle diameter 0.4 mm, Layer height 0.1–0.3 mm.
- **Density of PLA = 1.24 g/cm³** (memorize — it appears in numericals).
- **Maximum self-supporting overhang = 45°.** Anything steeper requires supports.
- **Maximum reliable bridge length ≈ 10 × nozzle diameter = 4 mm** (for 0.4 mm nozzle).
- **Simple Box** tests: layer adhesion, wall consistency, infill visibility.
- **Helix** tests: curved surfaces, overhang management, support removal.
- **U Bracket** tests: print orientation strategy, bridging, functional strength.
- **Critical print orientation rule:** Largest flat face goes on the bed. Avoid overhangs > 45°. Minimize Z-height to reduce print time and wobble.
- **Common print defects to identify in lab:**
  - **Warping** — bed too cold / first-layer adhesion poor.
  - **Stringing** — retraction settings too low.
  - **Layer shifting** — loose belts or stepper driver overheating.
  - **Under-extrusion** — clogged nozzle or low temperature.
  - **Over-extrusion** — temperature too high or flow rate too high.
- **Filament mass formula:** $m = V_{filament} \cdot \rho_{PLA}$, where $V_{filament} = V_{shell} + V_{infill}/\rho_{pack}$.
- **Number of layers formula:** $N_L = H / h$ (Z-height divided by layer height).
- **Always state the standard** (ISO/ASTM 52900:2015) when defining any AM term — it's a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark bonus.
